Analysis of changes\n\n3.1\n\nDeduction for contributions to the employees welfare fund is allowable if paid by the due date for filing the return of income Section 29(1)(e) of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 36(1)(va) of the ITA 1961\n\n3.1-1.\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nAs a part of the employee retirement benefit schemes, the employer and employee contribute to these schemes, such as a provident fund, pension, or insurance. While the employer contributes from his pocket, the employees contribution is deducted from his salary and remitted to the appropriate fund by the employer.\n\nThe following provisions govern the tax treatment of employee contributions in the employers hands.\n\n(a)\n\nSection 2(24)(x) Corresponding to Section 2(49)(o) of ITA 2025\n\n(b)\n\nSection 36(1)(va) Corresponding to Section 29(1)(e) of ITA 2025\n\n(c)\n\nSection 43B Corresponding to Section 37 of ITA 2025\n\n3.1-1a.\n\nEmployees contribution is treated as income\n\nSection 2(24)(x) of the ITA 1961 provides that any sum received by an employer from employees as contributions to any provident fund, superannuation fund, ESI fund, or any other fund established for the welfare of employees is deemed to be income of the employer. This means such amounts are initially included in the employers total income.\n\n3.1-1b.\n\nDeduction allowed for the remittance made\n\nTo offset the income inclusion under Section 2(24)(x), Section 36(1)(va) provides that any sum received by the employer as contribution from his employees, towards any welfare fund, i.e., PF or ESI, etc., is allowed as deduction if such sum is paid by the employer to the employees account in the relevant fund on or before the due date specified under the relevant Act. As per the\n\nExplanation\n\n1\n\nto Section 36(1)(va), the due date shall mean the due date specified under the relevant Act.\n\n3.1-1c.\n\nDeduction on actual payment\n\nSection 43B allows certain expenses, such as taxes, duties, and employer contributions to welfare funds, to be deducted only based on actual payments, regardless of the accounting method used. It ensures that specified statutory liabilities are deductible only when they are actually paid. Further, if the payment is made on or before the due date of filing the return, the deduction is allowed in the same financial year.\n\n3.1-1d.\n\nInterplay between Section 36(1)(va) and Section 43B\n\nOne of the most contentious disputes regarding the interplay between Section 36(1)(va) and Section 43B concerns the deductibility of employees contributions to provident funds and other welfare funds. While Section 36(1)(va) allows an employer to claim a deduction for employees contributions to welfare funds only if such contributions are deposited before the due date prescribed under the relevant law. Section 43B, on the other hand, is a general provision that overrides other sections and allows deductions for certain statutory payments, including employers contributions, on an actual-payment basis before filing the income tax return. The controversy arose because the courts have held that Section 43B overrides Section 36(1)(va) and, consequently, allows a deduction for the employees contribution even if deposited before the return filing date.\n\nSubsequently, the Finance Act, 2021, inserted\n\nExplanation\n\n2 to Section 36(1)(va), effective from 1 April 2021. The\n\nExplanation\n\nexpressly clarifies that the provisions of Section 43B shall not apply and shall be deemed never to have applied for the purpose of determining the due date under this clause.\n\nExplanation 5\n\nwas also inserted in Section 43B, with effect from 1 April 2021, clarifying that Section 43B shall not apply, and shall be deemed never to have applied, to any sum received by the assessee from employees which is treated as income under Section 2(24)(x).\n\nLater, the Supreme Court settled this debate in\n\nCheckmate Services Pvt. Ltd. v. CIT 2022 143 taxmann.com 178 (SC)\n\n, holding that Section 43B does not override Section 36(1)(va) for employees contributions. It held that employees contributions are not the employers own funds. They are amounts deducted from employees salaries held in trust, and therefore, the stricter due date condition under Section 36(1)(va) must be strictly complied with. Any delay in depositing employees contributions, even by a single day beyond the prescribed due date, results in a permanent disallowance of the deduction. This ruling applied even for assessment years before the Finance Act, 2021 amendments, reinforcing the legislative intent.\n\nAccordingly, the Finance Act, 2021 and the Supreme Courts ruling have conclusively settled the law that employees contributions are deductible only when deposited within the due dates prescribed under the relevant welfare laws, and cannot avail the extended timeline available under Section 43B.\n\n3.1-2.\n\nPosition under the ITA 2025\n\n(as amended by the Finance Act 2026)\n\nThe ITA 2025 retains provisions similar to those in the ITA 1961 regarding the treatment of employees contributions to welfare funds.\n\nHowever, the Finance Act 2026, with effect from 01-04-2026, substituted provisions of Section 29(1)(e) corresponding to section\n\n36(1)(va)\n\nof ITA 1961\n\nto allow the deduction to the employer if the amount is credited to the employees account in the relevant fund on or before the due date of filing the return of income under section 263(1) of ITA 2025 corresponding to Section 139(1) of the ITA 1961.\n\nThe substituted Section 29(1)(e) reads as follows:\n\nThe following sums, in the case of an assessee being an employer, shall be allowed as deduction in computing income chargeable under section 26:\n\n(e) the amount of contribution received from an employee to which the provisions of section 2(49)(o) apply, if it is credited by the assessee to the account of the employee in the relevant fund or funds, on or before the due date of filing of return of income under section 263(1) for the tax year.\n\nThe substituted provision takes a lenient approach, allowing the deduction contribution to the employees welfare fund if paid before the return filing due date. It reverses the strict interpretation of Section 36(1)(va) adopted by the Supreme Court in\n\nCheckmate Services (P.) Ltd. (supra).\n\nThe practical implications and consequences of this change would be as follows:\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nNo consequential amendment made to Section 37(6)\n\nThe Finance Act, 2026, amended Section 29(1)(e) to allow a deduction for employee contributions if deposited on or before the due date of filing the ITR, but no corresponding amendment has been made to Section 37(6) of ITA 2025 (corresponding to\n\nExplanation\n\n5 to Section 43B of ITA 1961), which provided an exclusion that this provision shall not apply to sums received from employees as contributions to funds referred to in Section 2(49)(o). It is recommended that a consequential amendment be made to Section 37 by omitting sub-section (6).\n\n3.1-2b.\n\nImplications on pending litigations\n\nThe amendment to Section 29(1)(e) of ITA 2025 comes into force with effect from Tax Year 2026-27 and is prospective in nature. It carries no retrospective or retroactive effect on prior tax years. For assessment years 2026-27 (previous year 2025-26) and earlier, the provisions of the ITA 1961 continue to govern, and accordingly, Section 36(1)(va) remains fully applicable. Thus, any disallowance in these years stands unaffected by the amendment.\n\n3.2\n\nProvisions for the deduction for employee welfare expenses have been consolidated at one place\n\nIn the 1961 Act, provisions related to expenses for contributions to Provident Fund, Gratuity Fund, Superannuation Fund, etc., were spread across various sections, such as 40A(7), (9) and 36(1)(iv), (iva), (v), (va). To determine the allowability of these expenses, one had to refer to multiple provisions in addition to the charging sections. In the 2025 Act, these provisions have been consolidated under Section 29, making them easier to understand and comply with. While the language has been simplified, there is no change in the tax incidence or deductibility.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 40:\nSection 28 (ITA 1961) v. Section 26/Section 66 (ITA 2025)\n\nProfits and gains of business or profession\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 28\n\n: Profits and gains of business or profession\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 26\n\n: Income under head Profits and gains of business or profession\n\nSection 66\n\n: Interpretation\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 28 | Section 26(1) & (2) |\n| Section 28( i ) | Section 26(2)( a ) |\n| Section 28( ii )( a ) | Section 26(2)( b )( i )( A ) & long line |\n| Section 28( ii )( b ) | Section 26(2)( b )( i )( B ) & long line |\n| Section 28( ii )( c ) | Section 26(2)( b )( ii ) & long line |\n| Section 28( ii )( d ) | Section 26(2)( c ) |\n| Section 28( ii )( e ) | Section 26(2)( b )( iii ) & long line |\n| Section 28( iii ) | Section 26(2)( d ) |\n| Section 28( iiia ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iiib ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iiic ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iiid ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iiie ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iv ) | Section 26(2)( f ) |\n| Section 28( v ) & its proviso | Section 26(2)( g ) |\n| Section 28( va ) | Section 26(2)( h ) |\n| Proviso to Section 28( va ) | Section 26(2)( h )( i )(A) & (B) |\n| Explanation (i) to Section 28( va ) | Section 66(1) |\n| Explanation (ii) to Section 28( va ) | Section 66(29) |\n| Section 28( vi ) | Section 26(2)( i ) |\n| Explanation to Section 28( vi ) | Section 66(8) |\n| Section 28( via ) | Section 26(2)( j ) |\n| Section 28( vii ) | Section 26(2)( k ) |\n| Explanation 2 to Section 28 | Section 26(3) |\n| Explanation 3 to Section 28 | Section 26(4) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nChanges in the charging provision for the profits and gains from business and profession\n\nThe following FAQs are issued by the Income-tax Department concerning Chapter IV-D containing sections 26 to 66 of the 2025 Act:\n\nQV.1 What are the major changes in the chapter Profits and gains of business and profession\n\nAns:\n\nMajor changes brought about in the chapter Profits and Gains of Business and Profession are as follows:\n\n(i)\n\nThe flow of the sections has been revised to improve coherence, create logical links, and ensure a smoother progression.\n\n(ii)\n\nSimilar sections, such as those on presumptive taxation for residents and non-residents, have been merged to simplify the content, make it more concise, and consolidate all related scenarios in one place.\n\n(iii)\n\nProvisions on similar topics, such as deductions on employee welfare, which were previously scattered across the chapter, have been grouped together for easier understanding and compliance.\n\n(iv)\n\nFormula-based explanations have been added to clarify complex concepts that were previously difficult to understand, such as the definition of Written-down Value.\n\n(v)\n\nProvisions involving multiple scenarios, like those for determining Actual Cost, which were previously presented as provisos or explanations, have been organized into a table for better readability and comprehension.\n\n(vi)\n\nAll provisos and explanations in the chapter have been converted into sub-sections to eliminate ambiguity and ensure clarity.\n\n(vii)\n\nRedundant and already sun-set provisions have been removed.\n\n(viii)\n\nProvisions specific to a single business segment which are used by small sub set of taxpayers have been moved to a new Schedule, while some procedural provisions have been transferred to rules to reduce complexity and focus on the substance of the section.\n\n(ix)\n\nAs a result, the total word count has been reduced by more than half, and the number of sections has decreased from 65 to 41.\n\nQV.2 How and why the order of sections been changed in the chapter on PGBP\n\nAns:\n\nAn effort has been made to group similar provisions together. For example, provisions related to general expenditure allowances (section 37) and deductions for actual payments (section 43B) have been placed along with the provisions of sections 40 and 40A. Similarly, provisions on scientific research expenditure, section 35AD (investment-linked deductions), skill development project expenditure, and agricultural extension project expenditure have been grouped together in the proposed sections 45, 46 and 47.\n\nQV.9 Which provisions have been shifted to Schedule and the reasons for the same\n\nAns:\n\nProvisions related to the Site Restoration Fund, as well as those for the Tea Development Account, Coffee Development Account, and Rubber Development Account, which apply only to specific trades and a small number of taxpayers, have been moved to the Schedules. The provisions related to the insurance business remain in the schedule as they were previously.\n\n3.1-1.\n\nAll export incentives are taxable Section 28(iiia) to (iiie) of the 1961 Act/Section 26(2)(e) of the 2025 Act\n\n3.1-1a.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nAs a general rule, all revenue receipts arising in the course of business shall be taxable under the head profits and gains from business or profession. Section 28 of the 1961 Act provides an inclusive list of all receipts or income that are chargeable to tax under this head, including the following.\n\n(a)\n\nProfits on the sale of licenses granted under the Import (Control) Order 1955\n\nSection 28(iiia)\n\n.\n\n(b)\n\nCash assistance received or receivable against exports under any Government of India scheme\n\nSection 28(iiib)\n\n.\n\n(c)\n\nAny customs or excise duty repaid or repayable as a drawback against exports\n\nSection 28(iiic)\n\n.\n\n(d)\n\nAny profit on the transfer of the Duty Entitlement Pass Book Scheme, being the Duty Remission Scheme under the export and import policy formulated and announced under section 5 of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 Section 28(iiid)\n\n.\n\n(e)\n\nany profit on the transfer of the Duty Free Replenishment Certificate, being the Duty Remission Scheme under the export and import policy formulated and announced under section 5 of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 Section 28(iiie)\n\n.\n\n3.1-1b.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nSection 26(2)(e) of the 2025 Act brings all kinds of import-export incentives within the ambit of the charging provision for taxability under the head of business income. The new provision refrained from specifying the name of the scheme under which the incentives or benefits are received for taxability. Instead, it provides that\n\nthe amount of any profit on sale of import licence, cash assistance against export, duty drawback or duty remission or any other export incentive, received or receivable\n\nshall be covered within the scope of business or profession income. Thus, all export benefit schemes, whether in the form of remission, refund, incentive, or profit on the sale of a license, shall be considered income and taxable as business income. Here is an illustrative list of Government Export Benefit Schemes:\n\n(a)\n\nDuty Remission and Refund Schemes\n\nRoDTEP (Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products)\n\nDuty Drawback Scheme\n\nRoSCT (Rebate of State and Central Taxes and Levies)\n\n(b)\n\nDuty-Free Import Schemes\n\nAdvance Authorization Scheme\n\nDuty-Free Import Authorization (DFIA)\n\n(c)\n\nCapital Goods Schemes\n\nExport Promotion Capital Goods (EPCG) Scheme\n\nPost Export EPCG\n\n(d)\n\nCredit and Financial Schemes\n\nNIRVIK Scheme\n\nECGC Export Credit Insurance\n\n(e)\n\nSpecial Economic Zone and Unit Benefits\n\nSpecial Economic Zones (SEZ) Benefits\n\nExport Oriented Units (EOU) Benefits\n\nDeemed Exports\n\n(f)\n\nSector-Specific Benefits\n\nTowns of Export Excellence (TEE)\n\nFocus Product and Market Schemes\n\n3.1-2.\n\nGross amount v. Profit arising from export incentives Sections 28(iiid) and 28(iiie) of the 1961 Act/Section 26(2)(e) of the 2025 Act\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder Sections 28(iiid) and 28(iiie) of the 1961 Act, only the profit portion on transfer of Duty Remission Scheme (such as the Duty Entitlement Pass Book Scheme and the Duty Free Replenishment Certificate) under the export and import policy formulated and announced under Section 5 of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 are taxable.\n\nThe Supreme Court, in the case of\n\nTopman Exports v. CIT 2012 18 taxmann.com 120 (SC)\n\n, provided a definitive interpretation of Section 28(iiid) concerning DEPB. In the instant case, the assessee, an exporter, sold DEPB and DFRC certificates. The core dispute revolved around whether the entire sale proceeds of these certificates or only the profit on their transfer (i.e., sale value minus face value) should be considered as business income under Section 28(iiid) for tax purposes. The Supreme Court meticulously analysed the DEPB scheme and the language of Section 28. T\n\nhe Supreme Court held that\n\nwhile the face value of the DEPB will fall under clause (iiib) of section 28, the difference between the sale value and the face value of the DEPB will fall under clause (iiid) of section 28\n\n. The Court explicitly stated that the High Court was incorrect in holding that the entire sale proceeds represented profit on transfer. This interpretation avoids double taxation, as the face value is taxed when it accrues (under Section 28(iiib)) and the profit on transfer is taxed when the transfer occurs (under Section 28(iiid)).\n\n3.1-2b.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, the words used are\n\nduty remission or any other export incentive\n\n. Even in the absence of an explicit mention that the profit arising on the transfer of DEPB/DFRC shall be taxable, it cannot be assumed that the whole amount of duty remission shall be taxed as profits and gains of business or profession, instead of taxing only the profit portion on the transfer of specified duty remission scheme(s).\n\nThe principle that one income cannot be taxed twice is a well-established tenet of Indian income tax law, primarily aimed at preventing the same income from being subjected to tax more than once in the hands of the same person under the same law. The judiciary has consistently upheld the general principle that income should not be taxed twice. This is not merely a matter of convenience but is rooted in the inherent fairness expected from fiscal statutes.\n\nIn the case of\n\nBarnes (Inspector of Taxes), Hutchinson 1939 7 ITR 124 (CA)\n\n, while interpreting the English Income-tax Act, 1918, the Court of Appeal recognised this fundamental principle. Clauson, LJ., stated that ...the Court recognises the principle that the Acts do not impose double taxation.... Brett, LJ., in Gilbertson v. Fergusson (as cited in Barnes (Inspector of Taxes), Hutchinson), articulated this strongly, observing that ...it would be so clearly unjust and obviously contrary to the meaning of the statute that the Government should have the tax payable twice over by the same person in respect of the same thing, that I should say it was a necessary implication that that could not be right. This highlights that even in the absence of specific statutory words, the principle against double taxation is a necessary implication of a just tax system. The principle is further emphasised in\n\nMutha Sarvarayudu v. CIT 1926 2 ITC 208 (Madras)\n\nand\n\nK.V.A.L.M. Ramanathan Chettiar, Commissioner of Income-tax 1973 88 ITR 169 (SC)\n\n.\n\nThus, where the gross amount of the DEPB/DFRC is already taxed on face value at the time of accrual, only the profit arising on its transfer should be taxed, which shall be the consideration received on transfer less the face value.\n\n3.1-3.\n\nSum received or receivable on non-compete business/professional agreement, whether chargeable under the head Profits and gains of business or profession or Capital gains Proviso (i) to Section 28(va) of the 1961 Act/Section 26(2)(h)(i)(A) of the 2025 Act\n\n3.1-3a.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder the 1961 Act, any sum, whether received or receivable in cash or in kind, on account of transfer of the right to manufacture, produce or process any article or thing or right to carry on any business or profession, which was chargeable under the head Capital gains, was not chargeable under the head Profits and gains of business or profession.\n\nThe 1961 Act generally taxes capital gains on an accrual basis, meaning the gain is taxable in the previous year in which the transfer of the capital asset takes place, as the right to receive consideration typically accrues at that point. This is reflected in Section 45(1) and the phrase consideration received or accruing in Section 48. However, the Act carves out significant exceptions where taxability is explicitly linked to the actual receipt of the sum or the occurrence of a specific event, rather than the date of transfer. For example, Section 45(5) for compulsory acquisition (taxable on receipt of compensation); Section 45(5A) for Joint Development Agreements (taxable on issuance of completion certificate); and Section 45(1B) for certain Unit Linked Insurance Policies (taxable on receipt of the amount). These exceptions are designed to align tax liability with the actual realisation by the taxpayer, providing relief or addressing practical challenges.\n\n3.1-3b.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, it is mentioned that a sum received on account of such transfer, which is chargeable under the head Capital gains, shall not be chargeable under the head Profits and gains of business or profession. In other words, the word receivable has been omitted from the 2025 Act.\n\nThis change does not seem to have any impact on the taxability of such a sum under the head of capital gains because the fundamental principle under Section 67(1) of the 2025 Act Corresponding to section 45 of the 1961 Act is that any profits or gains arising from the transfer of a capital asset effected in the tax year shall be chargeable to income-tax under the head Capital gains and shall be deemed to be the income of the previous year in which the transfer took place. Thus, the chargeable event for taxability is the transfer of the capital asset and not the receipt of the consideration for taxation of the capital gains, unless explicitly specified otherwise.\n\nFurther, Section 5 of the 2025 Act lays down the fundamental principles for determining the total income of a person, distinguishing between the receipt basis and the accrual basis of income recognition. This section sets the stage for how income, including capital gains, is brought to tax. For a resident, the total income of any previous year includes all income from whatever source derived, which:\n\nis received or is deemed to be received in India in such year; or\n\naccrues or arises or is deemed to accrue or arise to him in India during such year; or\n\naccrues or arises to him outside India during such year. This implies that a resident is taxable on their global income, either on a receipt basis or an accrual basis, whichever occurs earlier.\n\nIncome is considered received when the assessee gains control over the money. This refers to the first occasion when the amount comes into the hands of the assessee. It signifies actual physical or constructive receipt. Income accrues or arises when the assessee acquires a vested right to receive it, irrespective of whether the actual payment has been made or received. The right to receive the income crystallises, even if the actual realisation is deferred. The capital gains provision primarily operates on an\n\naccrual basis\n\n.\n\nThe taxability is linked to the event of transfer, which is when the right to receive the consideration for the asset typically arises, even if the full consideration is not actually received in that year.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 14A:\nSection 14A (ITA 1961) v. Section 80-IB (ITA 1961)\n\nSection - 92CA:\nSection 92CA (ITA 1961) v. Section 166/Section 532 (ITA 2025)\n\nReference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 92CA\n\n: Reference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 166\n\n: Reference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\nSection 532\n\n: Power to frame schemes\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 92CA(1) | Section 166(1) |\n| Section 92CA(1), 1st Proviso | Section 166(2) |\n| Section 92CA(1), 2nd Proviso | Section 166(3) |\n| Section 92CA(2) | Section 166(4) |\n| Section 92CA(2A) | Section 166(5)( a ) & long line |\n| Section 92CA(2B) | Section 166(5)( b ) & long line |\n| Section 92CA(2C) | — |\n| Section 92CA(3) | Section 166(6) |\n| Section 92CA(3A) | Section 166(7) |\n| Section 92CA(3A), Proviso | Section 166(8) |\n| Section 92CA(3AA) | Section 166(7) |\n| Section 92CA(3B) | Section 166(9) |\n| Section 92CA(3B), Proviso | Section 166(10) |\n| Section 92CA(4) | Section 166(11) |\n| Section 92CA(4A) | Section 166(12) |\n| Section 92CA(5) | Section 166(13)( a ) |\n| Section 92CA(6) | Section 166(13)( b ) |\n| Section 92CA(7) | Section 166(14) |\n| Section 92CA(8) | Section 532(1) |\n| Section 92CA(9) | Section 532(2) |\n| Section 92CA(10) | Section 532(4) |\n| Section 92CA(11) | Section 166(15) |\n| Section 92CA(11), Proviso | — |\n| Section 92CA(12) | Section 166(16) |\n| Section 92CA, Explanation | Section 166(17) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nChange in the time limit to pass the order by the Transfer Pricing Officer Section 166 of ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 92CA of ITA 1961)\n\n3.1-1.\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nSection 92CA of the ITA 1961 deals with the case where the assessee has entered into an international transaction or specified domestic transaction in any previous year, and the AO may refer the computation of the Arms Length Price (ALP) in relation to the said international transaction or specified domestic transaction under Section 92C to the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO).\n\nSub-section (3A) to section 92CA states that TPO is required to pass an order before sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation under section 153, or as the case may be, in Section 153B for making the order of assessment, reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, as the case may be, expires.\n\n3.1-1a.\n\nExclusion of the last day of assessment limitation\n\nThe interpretation of the phrase before sixty days prior to the date has been a consistent point of contention between taxpayers and the revenue, leading to significant litigation.\n\nThe Madras High Court, in the case of\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras)\n\n, which affirmed the Single Judges decision in\n\nJt. CIT vs. Pfizer Healthcare India (P.) Ltd. 2021 124 taxmann.com 536 (Madras)\n\n, held that the word date in Section 92CA(3A) refers to the last date for the AO to pass the assessment order under Section 153 (e.g., December 31st). The crucial words prior to indicate that, for the purpose of calculating the 60 days, this last date must be excluded. Therefore, if the assessment limitation expires on December 31st, the TPO order ought to have been passed on or before October 31st.\n\nThis interpretation was consistently followed across numerous ITAT benches, including in the following cases:\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nZydus Lifesciences Ltd. 2024 163 taxmann.com 349 (Ahmedabad Trib.)\n\nFrost & Sullivan (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 739 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nShell India Markets (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 366 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nSiemens Technology and Services (P.) Ltd. 2024 162 taxmann.com 357 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nHuhtamaki India Ltd. 2024 164 taxmann.com 194 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nAtos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nCAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\nPioneer India Electronics (P.) Ltd. 2025 178 taxmann.com 553 (Delhi Trib.)\n\nSigma Aldrich Chemicals (P.) Ltd. 2022 141 taxmann.com 431 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\n3.1-1b.\n\nMandatory nature of the time limit\n\nAnother critical aspect clarified by the judiciary is whether the time limit prescribed in Section 92CA(3A) is mandatory or merely directory. The revenue often argued that the use of the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A) implies a directory nature.\n\nHowever, the Madras High Court in\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras\n\n)\n\n, after a detailed analysis of the scheme of the Act and the consequences of non-compliance, unequivocally held that the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A)\n\nhas to be construed as shall and the time period fixed therein has to be scrupulously followed.\n\nThe court reasoned that the overall scheme of transfer pricing assessments, with various interlinked time limits (including extensions under the\n\nproviso\n\nto Section 92CA(3A) and Section 153), indicates a legislative intent to ensure timely completion. If the TPOs time limit were merely directory, it would undermine the entire assessment process. This principle has been reiterated in the case of\n\nAsst. CIT vs. CAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore - Trib.).\n\n3.1-1c.\n\nConsequence of time-barred TPO order\n\nThe judicial consensus is that a TPO order passed beyond the prescribed limitation period is\n\nillegal, null and void ab initio\n\nand\n\nnon est in the eyes of law\n\n. This has a cascading effect on subsequent assessment proceedings.\n\nAs established in\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai - Trib.) and Atos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai - Trib.),\n\nif the TPO order is time-barred, the assessee ceases to be an eligible assessee under Section 144C(15)(b)(i). Consequently, the extended time period for assessment under Section 153 (due to TPO reference) becomes inapplicable, and the draft assessment order and the final assessment order passed by the AO also become\n\nvoid ab initio\n\nand are liable to be quashed. This principle has been consistently applied across various ITAT decisions, such as\n\nIQVIA RDS (India) (P.) Ltd. 2025 181 taxmann.com 623 (Bangalore - Trib.) and World Sports (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 154 taxmann.com 181 (Mumbai - Trib.).\n\n3.1-2.\n\nAmendment by the Finance Act 2026\n\nThe Finance Act 2026 inserted the following sub-section (3AA) to section 92CA:\n\n(3AA). Notwithstanding anything contained in any judgment, order or decree of any court, for the purposes of making order under sub-section (3), the calculation of sixty days shall be made and shall always be deemed to have been made in the following manner, namely:\n\n(a) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (not being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 30th of January of that year;\n\n(b) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 31st of January of that year;\n\n(c) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of December of any year, the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 1st of November of that year.\n\nThe new sub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA clarifies the\n\n60-day limitation\n\nperiod for passing an order under Section 92CA(3). It provides deemed computation rules:\n\n(a)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (non-leap year), the order can be passed up to 30th January.\n\n(b)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (in a leap year), the order can be passed up to 31st January.\n\n(c)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 December, the order can be passed up to 1\n\nst\n\nNovember.\n\nThe most significant aspect of this amendment is its retrospective application with effect from 01-06-2007 and the explicit notwithstanding clause, which states:\n\nNotwithstanding any judgment, order, or decree of any court\n\n.\n\nThis phrase clearly indicates a legislative intent to override and nullify any judicial pronouncements that may have interpreted the sixty-day period differently or in a manner perceived to be contrary to the revenues interest.\n\nFor instance\n\n, in the table below, the limitation period after the retrospective amendment is explained:\n\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | Position before amendment | Position before amendment | Position after amendment | Position after amendment |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | 60 days expires on (as per court rulings) | Legal Status of TPO order | 60 days expires on (after amendment) | Legal Status of TPO order |\n| 2021-22 | 31-12-2023 * | 01-11-2023 | 31-10-2023 | Time-Barred | 01-11-2023 | Valid |\n| 2022-23 | 31-03-2025 # | 31-01-2025 | 30-01-2025 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2025 | Valid |\n| 2023-24 | 31-03-2026 # | 31-01-2026 | 30-01-2026 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2026 | Valid |\n| * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. |\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nImpact on the validity of past TPO orders\n\nSub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA, with its retrospective operation, validates all TPO orders that were held time-barred by courts due to a 1-day delay. The notwithstanding clause ensures that no court order invalidating such TPO orders can survive as binding precedent on this specific point. This means all assessments set aside solely because the TPO order was issued one day beyond the judicially interpreted deadline are revived. Further, for cases currently in litigation in which the validity of a TPO order is challenged on limitation grounds, the retrospective amendment fundamentally alters the legal landscape.\n\n3.1-3.\n\nPosition under the ITA 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act 2026)\n\nSub-section (7) of Section 166 (corresponding to Section 92CA(3) of the ITA 1961) provided a similar time limit of sixty days before the expiry of the limitation period for making the order of assessment, reassessment, recomputation or fresh assessment. The Finance Act 2026 makes an amendment to Section 166 by substituting sub-section (7), which reads as follows:\n\n(7) Where a reference was made under sub-section (1), an order under sub-section (6) may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which period of limitation referred to in section 286 or 296, for making the order of assessment or reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, expires and accordingly, where such period expires on\n\n(a) the 31st March of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st January of that year;\n\n(b) the 31st December of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st October of that year.\n\nThis amendment will take effect from 1st April 2026 and will, accordingly, apply in relation to the tax year 2026-2027 and subsequent years.\n\n3.1-3a.\n\nShift from day-based to month-based computation\n\nThe substituted provision takes a notable departure from the ITA 1961 framework by replacing the day-based computation of limitation periods with a month-based approach. Section 166(7) provides that:\n\n.order may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which the period of limitation .. expires\n\nUnder Section 92CA of the ITA 1961, limitation periods and procedural timelines were prescribed in terms of days, sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation expires. Section 166(7) of the ITA 2025 sets out the timelines, expressed in months. The computation is straightforward. The TPO merely needs to identify the month in which the limitation expires and ensure that the order is passed in the month immediately before that month. Further, the substituted provision provides that where the limitation period expires on 31\n\nst\n\nMarch or 31\n\nst\n\nDecember, as the case may be, the order under Section 166(6) should be passed on or before 31\n\nst\n\nJanuary or 31\n\nst\n\nOctober, respectively.\n\n3.1-3b.\n\nIllustration\n\nThe limitation period to pass order under section 166(7) under ITA 2025 is as follows:\n\n| Tax Year | Section 286 limitation period | Extension of limitation period as matter is referred to TPO | Limitation period under Section 286 (including extension) | Time limit to pass order under section 166(7) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| 2024-25 | 31-03-2027 | 12 months | 31-03-2028 | 31-01-2028 |\n| 2025-26 | 31-03-2028 | 12 months | 31-03-2029 | 31-01-2029 |\n\n3.2.\n\nPowers given to the central government to make faceless schemes for various provisions\n\n3.2-1.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder the 1961 Act,\n\nthe Central Government was empowered to notify schemes under various sections for various purposes\n\n.\n\nThe intent of such schemes was to\n\nimpart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface between the Income-tax authority, adjudication/appellant authorities (ITAT, DRP/DRC, Board of Advance Rulings) and the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation;\n\n(c)\n\nIntroducing disposal of appeals/disputes, imposition of penalty and a team-based functioning, with dynamic jurisdiction.\n\nThe relevant provisions under which the Central Government was empowered to notify such schemes and their respective purposes were as follows:\n\n| FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Section | Description | Notified Scheme | Purpose | Purpose | Purpose | Purpose |\n| Section | to Assessments | Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments |\n| Section 142B | Faceless scheme for exercise of powers under Section 142 | Faceless Inquiry and Valuation Scheme, 2022, which refers to Section 144B | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 143 and 151A | Assessment under Section 143(3), 144 and Section 147 | Manner is prescribed under Section 144B itself | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution |\n| Section 274 | Imposition of penalty | Faceless Penalty Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 279 | Faceless sanction for initiation of prosecution and compounding of offence | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Others | Others | Others | Others | Others | Others | Others |\n| Section 92CA | Determination of Arm’s Length Price by the Transfer Pricing Officer | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 130 | Faceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities | Faceless Jurisdiction of Income-tax Authorities Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 135A | Faceless collection of information | e-Verification Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 231 | Faceless collection and recovery of tax | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 293D | Faceless approval or registration by an Income-tax authority under any provision of the Income-tax Act | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n\nIt must be noted that there were 4 purposes for which faceless schemes were notified: 1) to eliminate interface, 2) to optimise utilisation of resources, 3) to enable team-based functioning, and 4) to enable dynamic jurisdiction. Almost all the above provisions talked about the 1\n\nst\n\n, 2\n\nnd\n\n, and 4\n\nth\n\npurposes. However, the 3\n\nrd\n\npurpose, i.e., team-based functioning, was prescribed only under specified provisions. Under those specified provisions (except for the exercise of powers under section 142 and for assessments), no scheme has been issued yet. The manner of faceless assessment was prescribed under Section 144B itself. Further, the faceless schemes issued for the exercise of powers under Section 142 also refer to Section 144B.\n\n3.2-2.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, a single Section 532 (Power to frame schemes) is introduced to give the power to the Central Government to make schemes.\n\nUnder Section 532, the Central Government is empowered to make schemes for any purpose under the 2025 Act\n\nto impart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation.\n\nIt must be noted that the purpose of team-based functioning and dynamic jurisdiction is absent from Section 532 of the 2025 Act. Further, it also provides that if a scheme was already notified under the 1961 Act, the Central Government may amend or modify such scheme.\n\nThe transition provision, i.e., Section 536 of the 2025 Act, provides that any scheme which has been notified under the provisions of the repealed Income-tax Act with a view to eliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person, the said scheme shall be deemed to have been made and shall continue in force accordingly:\n\n(a)\n\nunder the corresponding provisions of this Act; or\n\n(b)\n\nunder section 532, where there is no such corresponding provision.\n\nIt must be noted that, though Section 532 empowers the Central Government to make a scheme for any purpose under the 2025 Act, there are specific provisions as well wherein the power has been given to make a scheme. Such provisions are as follows:\n\nFaceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities Section 245 of the 2025 Act/Section 130 of the 1961 Act\n\nFaceless collection of information Section 260 of the 2025 Act/Section 135A of the 1961 Act\n\nUnder these two specific provisions, the scheme may be made for all four purposes as were there under the old Act. Thus, the purpose of the scheme may also involve team-based functioning with dynamic jurisdiction, alongside eliminating the interface between the income-tax authority and the assessee and optimum utilisation of resources.\n\nFurther, it is provided under the 2025 Act that the manner of faceless assessment as currently provided under the relevant provision of the Act itself will be provided under the rules.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 142B:\nSection 92CA (ITA 1961) v. Section 166/Section 532 (ITA 2025)\n\nReference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\n1.
