Transfer Pricing Officer | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 130 | Faceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities | Faceless Jurisdiction of Income-tax Authorities Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 135A | Faceless collection of information | e-Verification Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 231 | Faceless collection and recovery of tax | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 293D | Faceless approval or registration by an Income-tax authority under any provision of the Income-tax Act | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n\nIt must be noted that there were 4 purposes for which faceless schemes were notified: 1) to eliminate interface, 2) to optimise utilisation of resources, 3) to enable team-based functioning, and 4) to enable dynamic jurisdiction. Almost all the above provisions talked about the 1\n\nst\n\n, 2\n\nnd\n\n, and 4\n\nth\n\npurposes. However, the 3\n\nrd\n\npurpose, i.e., team-based functioning, was prescribed only under specified provisions. Under those specified provisions (except for the exercise of powers under section 142 and for assessments), no scheme has been issued yet. The manner of faceless assessment was prescribed under Section 144B itself. Further, the faceless schemes issued for the exercise of powers under Section 142 also refer to Section 144B.\n\n3.2-2.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, a single Section 532 (Power to frame schemes) is introduced to give the power to the Central Government to make schemes.\n\nUnder Section 532, the Central Government is empowered to make schemes for any purpose under the 2025 Act\n\nto impart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation.\n\nIt must be noted that the purpose of team-based functioning and dynamic jurisdiction is absent from Section 532 of the 2025 Act. Further, it also provides that if a scheme was already notified under the 1961 Act, the Central Government may amend or modify such scheme.\n\nThe transition provision, i.e., Section 536 of the 2025 Act, provides that any scheme which has been notified under the provisions of the repealed Income-tax Act with a view to eliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person, the said scheme shall be deemed to have been made and shall continue in force accordingly:\n\n(a)\n\nunder the corresponding provisions of this Act; or\n\n(b)\n\nunder section 532, where there is no such corresponding provision.\n\nIt must be noted that, though Section 532 empowers the Central Government to make a scheme for any purpose under the 2025 Act, there are specific provisions as well wherein the power has been given to make a scheme. Such provisions are as follows:\n\nFaceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities Section 245 of the 2025 Act/Section 130 of the 1961 Act\n\nFaceless collection of information Section 260 of the 2025 Act/Section 135A of the 1961 Act\n\nUnder these two specific provisions, the scheme may be made for all four purposes as were there under the old Act. Thus, the purpose of the scheme may also involve team-based functioning with dynamic jurisdiction, alongside eliminating the interface between the income-tax authority and the assessee and optimum utilisation of resources.\n\nFurther, it is provided under the 2025 Act that the manner of faceless assessment as currently provided under the relevant provision of the Act itself will be provided under the rules.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 253:\nSection 92CA (ITA 1961) v. Section 166/Section 532 (ITA 2025)\n\nReference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 92CA\n\n: Reference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 166\n\n: Reference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\nSection 532\n\n: Power to frame schemes\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 92CA(1) | Section 166(1) |\n| Section 92CA(1), 1st Proviso | Section 166(2) |\n| Section 92CA(1), 2nd Proviso | Section 166(3) |\n| Section 92CA(2) | Section 166(4) |\n| Section 92CA(2A) | Section 166(5)( a ) & long line |\n| Section 92CA(2B) | Section 166(5)( b ) & long line |\n| Section 92CA(2C) | — |\n| Section 92CA(3) | Section 166(6) |\n| Section 92CA(3A) | Section 166(7) |\n| Section 92CA(3A), Proviso | Section 166(8) |\n| Section 92CA(3AA) | Section 166(7) |\n| Section 92CA(3B) | Section 166(9) |\n| Section 92CA(3B), Proviso | Section 166(10) |\n| Section 92CA(4) | Section 166(11) |\n| Section 92CA(4A) | Section 166(12) |\n| Section 92CA(5) | Section 166(13)( a ) |\n| Section 92CA(6) | Section 166(13)( b ) |\n| Section 92CA(7) | Section 166(14) |\n| Section 92CA(8) | Section 532(1) |\n| Section 92CA(9) | Section 532(2) |\n| Section 92CA(10) | Section 532(4) |\n| Section 92CA(11) | Section 166(15) |\n| Section 92CA(11), Proviso | — |\n| Section 92CA(12) | Section 166(16) |\n| Section 92CA, Explanation | Section 166(17) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nChange in the time limit to pass the order by the Transfer Pricing Officer Section 166 of ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 92CA of ITA 1961)\n\n3.1-1.\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nSection 92CA of the ITA 1961 deals with the case where the assessee has entered into an international transaction or specified domestic transaction in any previous year, and the AO may refer the computation of the Arms Length Price (ALP) in relation to the said international transaction or specified domestic transaction under Section 92C to the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO).\n\nSub-section (3A) to section 92CA states that TPO is required to pass an order before sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation under section 153, or as the case may be, in Section 153B for making the order of assessment, reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, as the case may be, expires.\n\n3.1-1a.\n\nExclusion of the last day of assessment limitation\n\nThe interpretation of the phrase before sixty days prior to the date has been a consistent point of contention between taxpayers and the revenue, leading to significant litigation.\n\nThe Madras High Court, in the case of\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras)\n\n, which affirmed the Single Judges decision in\n\nJt. CIT vs. Pfizer Healthcare India (P.) Ltd. 2021 124 taxmann.com 536 (Madras)\n\n, held that the word date in Section 92CA(3A) refers to the last date for the AO to pass the assessment order under Section 153 (e.g., December 31st). The crucial words prior to indicate that, for the purpose of calculating the 60 days, this last date must be excluded. Therefore, if the assessment limitation expires on December 31st, the TPO order ought to have been passed on or before October 31st.\n\nThis interpretation was consistently followed across numerous ITAT benches, including in the following cases:\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nZydus Lifesciences Ltd. 2024 163 taxmann.com 349 (Ahmedabad Trib.)\n\nFrost & Sullivan (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 739 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nShell India Markets (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 366 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nSiemens Technology and Services (P.) Ltd. 2024 162 taxmann.com 357 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nHuhtamaki India Ltd. 2024 164 taxmann.com 194 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nAtos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nCAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\nPioneer India Electronics (P.) Ltd. 2025 178 taxmann.com 553 (Delhi Trib.)\n\nSigma Aldrich Chemicals (P.) Ltd. 2022 141 taxmann.com 431 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\n3.1-1b.\n\nMandatory nature of the time limit\n\nAnother critical aspect clarified by the judiciary is whether the time limit prescribed in Section 92CA(3A) is mandatory or merely directory. The revenue often argued that the use of the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A) implies a directory nature.\n\nHowever, the Madras High Court in\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras\n\n)\n\n, after a detailed analysis of the scheme of the Act and the consequences of non-compliance, unequivocally held that the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A)\n\nhas to be construed as shall and the time period fixed therein has to be scrupulously followed.\n\nThe court reasoned that the overall scheme of transfer pricing assessments, with various interlinked time limits (including extensions under the\n\nproviso\n\nto Section 92CA(3A) and Section 153), indicates a legislative intent to ensure timely completion. If the TPOs time limit were merely directory, it would undermine the entire assessment process. This principle has been reiterated in the case of\n\nAsst. CIT vs. CAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore - Trib.).\n\n3.1-1c.\n\nConsequence of time-barred TPO order\n\nThe judicial consensus is that a TPO order passed beyond the prescribed limitation period is\n\nillegal, null and void ab initio\n\nand\n\nnon est in the eyes of law\n\n. This has a cascading effect on subsequent assessment proceedings.\n\nAs established in\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai - Trib.) and Atos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai - Trib.),\n\nif the TPO order is time-barred, the assessee ceases to be an eligible assessee under Section 144C(15)(b)(i). Consequently, the extended time period for assessment under Section 153 (due to TPO reference) becomes inapplicable, and the draft assessment order and the final assessment order passed by the AO also become\n\nvoid ab initio\n\nand are liable to be quashed. This principle has been consistently applied across various ITAT decisions, such as\n\nIQVIA RDS (India) (P.) Ltd. 2025 181 taxmann.com 623 (Bangalore - Trib.) and World Sports (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 154 taxmann.com 181 (Mumbai - Trib.).\n\n3.1-2.\n\nAmendment by the Finance Act 2026\n\nThe Finance Act 2026 inserted the following sub-section (3AA) to section 92CA:\n\n(3AA). Notwithstanding anything contained in any judgment, order or decree of any court, for the purposes of making order under sub-section (3), the calculation of sixty days shall be made and shall always be deemed to have been made in the following manner, namely:\n\n(a) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (not being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 30th of January of that year;\n\n(b) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 31st of January of that year;\n\n(c) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of December of any year, the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 1st of November of that year.\n\nThe new sub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA clarifies the\n\n60-day limitation\n\nperiod for passing an order under Section 92CA(3). It provides deemed computation rules:\n\n(a)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (non-leap year), the order can be passed up to 30th January.\n\n(b)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (in a leap year), the order can be passed up to 31st January.\n\n(c)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 December, the order can be passed up to 1\n\nst\n\nNovember.\n\nThe most significant aspect of this amendment is its retrospective application with effect from 01-06-2007 and the explicit notwithstanding clause, which states:\n\nNotwithstanding any judgment, order, or decree of any court\n\n.\n\nThis phrase clearly indicates a legislative intent to override and nullify any judicial pronouncements that may have interpreted the sixty-day period differently or in a manner perceived to be contrary to the revenues interest.\n\nFor instance\n\n, in the table below, the limitation period after the retrospective amendment is explained:\n\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | Position before amendment | Position before amendment | Position after amendment | Position after amendment |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | 60 days expires on (as per court rulings) | Legal Status of TPO order | 60 days expires on (after amendment) | Legal Status of TPO order |\n| 2021-22 | 31-12-2023 * | 01-11-2023 | 31-10-2023 | Time-Barred | 01-11-2023 | Valid |\n| 2022-23 | 31-03-2025 # | 31-01-2025 | 30-01-2025 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2025 | Valid |\n| 2023-24 | 31-03-2026 # | 31-01-2026 | 30-01-2026 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2026 | Valid |\n| * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. |\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nImpact on the validity of past TPO orders\n\nSub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA, with its retrospective operation, validates all TPO orders that were held time-barred by courts due to a 1-day delay. The notwithstanding clause ensures that no court order invalidating such TPO orders can survive as binding precedent on this specific point. This means all assessments set aside solely because the TPO order was issued one day beyond the judicially interpreted deadline are revived. Further, for cases currently in litigation in which the validity of a TPO order is challenged on limitation grounds, the retrospective amendment fundamentally alters the legal landscape.\n\n3.1-3.\n\nPosition under the ITA 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act 2026)\n\nSub-section (7) of Section 166 (corresponding to Section 92CA(3) of the ITA 1961) provided a similar time limit of sixty days before the expiry of the limitation period for making the order of assessment, reassessment, recomputation or fresh assessment. The Finance Act 2026 makes an amendment to Section 166 by substituting sub-section (7), which reads as follows:\n\n(7) Where a reference was made under sub-section (1), an order under sub-section (6) may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which period of limitation referred to in section 286 or 296, for making the order of assessment or reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, expires and accordingly, where such period expires on\n\n(a) the 31st March of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st January of that year;\n\n(b) the 31st December of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st October of that year.\n\nThis amendment will take effect from 1st April 2026 and will, accordingly, apply in relation to the tax year 2026-2027 and subsequent years.\n\n3.1-3a.\n\nShift from day-based to month-based computation\n\nThe substituted provision takes a notable departure from the ITA 1961 framework by replacing the day-based computation of limitation periods with a month-based approach. Section 166(7) provides that:\n\n.order may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which the period of limitation .. expires\n\nUnder Section 92CA of the ITA 1961, limitation periods and procedural timelines were prescribed in terms of days, sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation expires. Section 166(7) of the ITA 2025 sets out the timelines, expressed in months. The computation is straightforward. The TPO merely needs to identify the month in which the limitation expires and ensure that the order is passed in the month immediately before that month. Further, the substituted provision provides that where the limitation period expires on 31\n\nst\n\nMarch or 31\n\nst\n\nDecember, as the case may be, the order under Section 166(6) should be passed on or before 31\n\nst\n\nJanuary or 31\n\nst\n\nOctober, respectively.\n\n3.1-3b.\n\nIllustration\n\nThe limitation period to pass order under section 166(7) under ITA 2025 is as follows:\n\n| Tax Year | Section 286 limitation period | Extension of limitation period as matter is referred to TPO | Limitation period under Section 286 (including extension) | Time limit to pass order under section 166(7) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| 2024-25 | 31-03-2027 | 12 months | 31-03-2028 | 31-01-2028 |\n| 2025-26 | 31-03-2028 | 12 months | 31-03-2029 | 31-01-2029 |\n\n3.2.\n\nPowers given to the central government to make faceless schemes for various provisions\n\n3.2-1.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder the 1961 Act,\n\nthe Central Government was empowered to notify schemes under various sections for various purposes\n\n.\n\nThe intent of such schemes was to\n\nimpart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface between the Income-tax authority, adjudication/appellant authorities (ITAT, DRP/DRC, Board of Advance Rulings) and the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation;\n\n(c)\n\nIntroducing disposal of appeals/disputes, imposition of penalty and a team-based functioning, with dynamic jurisdiction.\n\nThe relevant provisions under which the Central Government was empowered to notify such schemes and their respective purposes were as follows:\n\n| FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Section | Description | Notified Scheme | Purpose | Purpose | Purpose | Purpose |\n| Section | prescribed under Section 144B itself | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Faceless functioning of the DRP | - | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 246 | Disposal of appeal by JCIT(A) | e-Appeals Scheme, 2023 | Yes | - | - | - |\n| Section 250 | Disposal of appeal by CIT(A) | Faceless Appeal Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 253 and 255 | Disposal of appeal by ITAT | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 245D | Faceless functioning of the Interim Board | e-Settlement Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 245MA | Faceless functioning of Dispute Resolution Committee | e-Dispute Resolution Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 245R | Advance Rulings | - | - | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions |\n| Section 157A | Faceless rectification, amendments in order, issuance of demand notice and intimation of loss | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 264A | Faceless revision of orders under Section 263 and 264 | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 264B | Faceless effect to the order passed by CIT (A), ITAT, High Court, Supreme Court and revision orders | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution |\n| Section 274 | Imposition of penalty | Faceless Penalty Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 279 | Faceless sanction for initiation of prosecution and compounding of offence | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Others | Others | Others | Others | Others | Others | Others |\n| Section 92CA | Determination of Arm’s Length Price by the Transfer Pricing Officer | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 130 | Faceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities | Faceless Jurisdiction of Income-tax Authorities Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 135A | Faceless collection of information | e-Verification Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 231 | Faceless collection and recovery of tax | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 293D | Faceless approval or registration by an Income-tax authority under any provision of the Income-tax Act | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n\nIt must be noted that there were 4 purposes for which faceless schemes were notified: 1) to eliminate interface, 2) to optimise utilisation of resources, 3) to enable team-based functioning, and 4) to enable dynamic jurisdiction. Almost all the above provisions talked about the 1\n\nst\n\n, 2\n\nnd\n\n, and 4\n\nth\n\npurposes. However, the 3\n\nrd\n\npurpose, i.e., team-based functioning, was prescribed only under specified provisions. Under those specified provisions (except for the exercise of powers under section 142 and for assessments), no scheme has been issued yet. The manner of faceless assessment was prescribed under Section 144B itself. Further, the faceless schemes issued for the exercise of powers under Section 142 also refer to Section 144B.\n\n3.2-2.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, a single Section 532 (Power to frame schemes) is introduced to give the power to the Central Government to make schemes.\n\nUnder Section 532, the Central Government is empowered to make schemes for any purpose under the 2025 Act\n\nto impart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation.\n\nIt must be noted that the purpose of team-based functioning and dynamic jurisdiction is absent from Section 532 of the 2025 Act. Further, it also provides that if a scheme was already notified under the 1961 Act, the Central Government may amend or modify such scheme.\n\nThe transition provision, i.e., Section 536 of the 2025 Act, provides that any scheme which has been notified under the provisions of the repealed Income-tax Act with a view to eliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person, the said scheme shall be deemed to have been made and shall continue in force accordingly:\n\n(a)\n\nunder the corresponding provisions of this Act; or\n\n(b)\n\nunder section 532, where there is no such corresponding provision.\n\nIt must be noted that, though Section 532 empowers the Central Government to make a scheme for any purpose under the 2025 Act, there are specific provisions as well wherein the power has been given to make a scheme. Such provisions are as follows:\n\nFaceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities Section 245 of the 2025 Act/Section 130 of the 1961 Act\n\nFaceless collection of information Section 260 of the 2025 Act/Section 135A of the 1961 Act\n\nUnder these two specific provisions, the scheme may be made for all four purposes as were there under the old Act. Thus, the purpose of the scheme may also involve team-based functioning with dynamic jurisdiction, alongside eliminating the interface between the income-tax authority and the assessee and optimum utilisation of resources.\n\nFurther, it is provided under the 2025 Act that the manner of faceless assessment as currently provided under the relevant provision of the Act itself will be provided under the rules.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 255:\nSection 92CA (ITA 1961) v. Section 166/Section 532 (ITA 2025)\n\nReference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 92CA\n\n: Reference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 166\n\n: Reference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\nSection 532\n\n: Power to frame schemes\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 92CA(1) | Section 166(1) |\n| Section 92CA(1), 1st Proviso | Section 166(2) |\n| Section 92CA(1), 2nd Proviso | Section 166(3) |\n| Section 92CA(2) | Section 166(4) |\n| Section 92CA(2A) | Section 166(5)( a ) & long line |\n| Section 92CA(2B) | Section 166(5)( b ) & long line |\n| Section 92CA(2C) | — |\n| Section 92CA(3) | Section 166(6) |\n| Section 92CA(3A) | Section 166(7) |\n| Section 92CA(3A), Proviso | Section 166(8) |\n| Section 92CA(3AA) | Section 166(7) |\n| Section 92CA(3B) | Section 166(9) |\n| Section 92CA(3B), Proviso | Section 166(10) |\n| Section 92CA(4) | Section 166(11) |\n| Section 92CA(4A) | Section 166(12) |\n| Section 92CA(5) | Section 166(13)( a ) |\n| Section 92CA(6) | Section 166(13)( b ) |\n| Section 92CA(7) | Section 166(14) |\n| Section 92CA(8) | Section 532(1) |\n| Section 92CA(9) | Section 532(2) |\n| Section 92CA(10) | Section 532(4) |\n| Section 92CA(11) | Section 166(15) |\n| Section 92CA(11), Proviso | — |\n| Section 92CA(12) | Section 166(16) |\n| Section 92CA, Explanation | Section 166(17) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nChange in the time limit to pass the order by the Transfer Pricing Officer Section 166 of ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 92CA of ITA 1961)\n\n3.1-1.\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nSection 92CA of the ITA 1961 deals with the case where the assessee has entered into an international transaction or specified domestic transaction in any previous year, and the AO may refer the computation of the Arms Length Price (ALP) in relation to the said international transaction or specified domestic transaction under Section 92C to the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO).\n\nSub-section (3A) to section 92CA states that TPO is required to pass an order before sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation under section 153, or as the case may be, in Section 153B for making the order of assessment, reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, as the case may be, expires.\n\n3.1-1a.\n\nExclusion of the last day of assessment limitation\n\nThe interpretation of the phrase before sixty days prior to the date has been a consistent point of contention between taxpayers and the revenue, leading to significant litigation.\n\nThe Madras High Court, in the case of\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras)\n\n, which affirmed the Single Judges decision in\n\nJt. CIT vs. Pfizer Healthcare India (P.) Ltd. 2021 124 taxmann.com 536 (Madras)\n\n, held that the word date in Section 92CA(3A) refers to the last date for the AO to pass the assessment order under Section 153 (e.g., December 31st). The crucial words prior to indicate that, for the purpose of calculating the 60 days, this last date must be excluded. Therefore, if the assessment limitation expires on December 31st, the TPO order ought to have been passed on or before October 31st.\n\nThis interpretation was consistently followed across numerous ITAT benches, including in the following cases:\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nZydus Lifesciences Ltd. 2024 163 taxmann.com 349 (Ahmedabad Trib.)\n\nFrost & Sullivan (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 739 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nShell India Markets (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 366 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nSiemens Technology and Services (P.) Ltd. 2024 162 taxmann.com 357 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nHuhtamaki India Ltd. 2024 164 taxmann.com 194 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nAtos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nCAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\nPioneer India Electronics (P.) Ltd. 2025 178 taxmann.com 553 (Delhi Trib.)\n\nSigma Aldrich Chemicals (P.) Ltd. 2022 141 taxmann.com 431 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\n3.1-1b.\n\nMandatory nature of the time limit\n\nAnother critical aspect clarified by the judiciary is whether the time limit prescribed in Section 92CA(3A) is mandatory or merely directory. The revenue often argued that the use of the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A) implies a directory nature.\n\nHowever, the Madras High Court in\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras\n\n)\n\n, after a detailed analysis of the scheme of the Act and the consequences of non-compliance, unequivocally held that the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A)\n\nhas to be construed as shall and the time period fixed therein has to be scrupulously followed.\n\nThe court reasoned that the overall scheme of transfer pricing assessments, with various interlinked time limits (including extensions under the\n\nproviso\n\nto Section 92CA(3A) and Section 153), indicates a legislative intent to ensure timely completion. If the TPOs time limit were merely directory, it would undermine the entire assessment process. This principle has been reiterated in the case of\n\nAsst. CIT vs. CAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore - Trib.).\n\n3.1-1c.\n\nConsequence of time-barred TPO order\n\nThe judicial consensus is that a TPO order passed beyond the prescribed limitation period is\n\nillegal, null and void ab initio\n\nand\n\nnon est in the eyes of law\n\n. This has a cascading effect on subsequent assessment proceedings.\n\nAs established in\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai - Trib.) and Atos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai - Trib.),\n\nif the TPO order is time-barred, the assessee ceases to be an eligible assessee under Section 144C(15)(b)(i). Consequently, the extended time period for assessment under Section 153 (due to TPO reference) becomes inapplicable, and the draft assessment order and the final assessment order passed by the AO also become\n\nvoid ab initio\n\nand are liable to be quashed. This principle has been consistently applied across various ITAT decisions, such as\n\nIQVIA RDS (India) (P.) Ltd. 2025 181 taxmann.com 623 (Bangalore - Trib.) and World Sports (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 154 taxmann.com 181 (Mumbai - Trib.).\n\n3.1-2.\n\nAmendment by the Finance Act 2026\n\nThe Finance Act 2026 inserted the following sub-section (3AA) to section 92CA:\n\n(3AA). Notwithstanding anything contained in any judgment, order or decree of any court, for the purposes of making order under sub-section (3), the calculation of sixty days shall be made and shall always be deemed to have been made in the following manner, namely:\n\n(a) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (not being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 30th of January of that year;\n\n(b) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 31st of January of that year;\n\n(c) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of December of any year, the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 1st of November of that year.\n\nThe new sub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA clarifies the\n\n60-day limitation\n\nperiod for passing an order under Section 92CA(3). It provides deemed computation rules:\n\n(a)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (non-leap year), the order can be passed up to 30th January.\n\n(b)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (in a leap year), the order can be passed up to 31st January.\n\n(c)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 December, the order can be passed up to 1\n\nst\n\nNovember.\n\nThe most significant aspect of this amendment is its retrospective application with effect from 01-06-2007 and the explicit notwithstanding clause, which states:\n\nNotwithstanding any judgment, order, or decree of any court\n\n.\n\nThis phrase clearly indicates a legislative intent to override and nullify any judicial pronouncements that may have interpreted the sixty-day period differently or in a manner perceived to be contrary to the revenues interest.\n\nFor instance\n\n, in the table below, the limitation period after the retrospective amendment is explained:\n\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | Position before amendment | Position before amendment | Position after amendment | Position after amendment |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | 60 days expires on (as per court rulings) | Legal Status of TPO order | 60 days expires on (after amendment) | Legal Status of TPO order |\n| 2021-22 | 31-12-2023 * | 01-11-2023 | 31-10-2023 | Time-Barred | 01-11-2023 | Valid |\n| 2022-23 | 31-03-2025 # | 31-01-2025 | 30-01-2025 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2025 | Valid |\n| 2023-24 | 31-03-2026 # | 31-01-2026 | 30-01-2026 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2026 | Valid |\n| * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. |\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nImpact on the validity of past TPO orders\n\nSub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA, with its retrospective operation, validates all TPO orders that were held time-barred by courts due to a 1-day delay. The notwithstanding clause ensures that no court order invalidating such TPO orders can survive as binding precedent on this specific point. This means all assessments set aside solely because the TPO order was issued one day beyond the judicially interpreted deadline are revived. Further, for cases currently in litigation in which the validity of a TPO order is challenged on limitation grounds, the retrospective amendment fundamentally alters the legal landscape.\n\n3.1-3.\n\nPosition under the ITA 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act 2026)\n\nSub-section (7) of Section 166 (corresponding to Section 92CA(3) of the ITA 1961) provided a similar time limit of sixty days before the expiry of the limitation period for making the order of assessment, reassessment, recomputation or fresh assessment. The Finance Act 2026 makes an amendment to Section 166 by substituting sub-section (7), which reads as follows:\n\n(7) Where a reference was made under sub-section (1), an order under sub-section (6) may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which period of limitation referred to in section 286 or 296, for making the order of assessment or reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, expires and accordingly, where such period expires on\n\n(a) the 31st March of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st January of that year;\n\n(b) the 31st December of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st October of that year.\n\nThis amendment will take effect from 1st April 2026 and will, accordingly, apply in relation to the tax year 2026-2027 and subsequent years.\n\n3.1-3a.\n\nShift from day-based to month-based computation\n\nThe substituted provision takes a notable departure from the ITA 1961 framework by replacing the day-based computation of limitation periods with a month-based approach. Section 166(7) provides that:\n\n.order may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which the period of limitation .. expires\n\nUnder Section 92CA of the ITA 1961, limitation periods and procedural timelines were prescribed in terms of days, sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation expires. Section 166(7) of the ITA 2025 sets out the timelines, expressed in months. The computation is straightforward. The TPO merely needs to identify the month in which the limitation expires and ensure that the order is passed in the month immediately before that month. Further, the substituted provision provides that where the limitation period expires on 31\n\nst\n\nMarch or 31\n\nst\n\nDecember, as the case may be, the order under Section 166(6) should be passed on or before 31\n\nst\n\nJanuary or 31\n\nst\n\nOctober, respectively.\n\n3.1-3b.\n\nIllustration\n\nThe limitation period to pass order under section 166(7) under ITA 2025 is as follows:\n\n| Tax Year | Section 286 limitation period | Extension of limitation period as matter is referred to TPO | Limitation period under Section 286 (including extension) | Time limit to pass order under section 166(7) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| 2024-25 | 31-03-2027 | 12 months | 31-03-2028 | 31-01-2028 |\n| 2025-26 | 31-03-2028 | 12 months | 31-03-2029 | 31-01-2029 |\n\n3.2.\n\nPowers given to the central government to make faceless schemes for various provisions\n\n3.2-1.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder the 1961 Act,\n\nthe Central Government was empowered to notify schemes under various sections for various purposes\n\n.\n\nThe intent of such schemes was to\n\nimpart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface between the Income-tax authority, adjudication/appellant authorities (ITAT, DRP/DRC, Board of Advance Rulings) and the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation;\n\n(c)\n\nIntroducing disposal of appeals/disputes, imposition of penalty and a team-based functioning, with dynamic jurisdiction.\n\nThe relevant provisions under which the Central Government was empowered to notify such schemes and their respective purposes were as follows:\n\n| FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Section | Description | Notified Scheme | Purpose | Purpose | Purpose | Purpose |\n| Section | Description | Notified Scheme | Eliminate Interface | Optimum utilisation of resources | Team-based functioning | Dynamic Jurisdiction |\n| Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments | Relating to Assessments |\n| Section 142B | Faceless scheme for exercise of powers under Section 142 | Faceless Inquiry and Valuation Scheme, 2022, which refers to Section 144B | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 143 and 151A | Assessment under Section 143(3), 144 and Section 147 | Manner is prescribed under Section 144B itself | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals |\n| Section 144C | Faceless functioning of the DRP | - | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 246 | Disposal of appeal by JCIT(A) | e-Appeals Scheme, 2023 | Yes | - | - | - |\n| Section 250 | Disposal of appeal by CIT(A) | Faceless Appeal Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 253 and 255 | Disposal of appeal by ITAT | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 245D | Faceless functioning of the Interim Board | e-Settlement Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 245MA | Faceless functioning of Dispute Resolution Committee | e-Dispute Resolution Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 245R | Faceless functioning of Board for Advance Rulings | e-advance rulings Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 245W | Faceless appeal to High Court by AO against an Order or Ruling of Board for Advance Rulings | - | - | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions | Relating to Rectifications and Revisions |\n| Section 157A | Faceless rectification, amendments in order, issuance of demand notice and intimation of loss | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 264A | Faceless revision of orders under Section 263 and 264 | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 264B | Faceless effect to the order passed by CIT (A), ITAT, High Court, Supreme Court and revision orders | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution | Relating to Penalties and Prosecution |\n| Section 274 | Imposition of penalty | Faceless Penalty Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 279 | Faceless sanction for initiation of prosecution and compounding of offence | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Others | Others | Others | Others | Others | Others | Others |\n| Section 92CA | Determination of Arm’s Length Price by the Transfer Pricing Officer | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 130 | Faceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities | Faceless Jurisdiction of Income-tax Authorities Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 135A | Faceless collection of information | e-Verification Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 231 | Faceless collection and recovery of tax | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 293D | Faceless approval or registration by an Income-tax authority under any provision of the Income-tax Act | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n\nIt must be noted that there were 4 purposes for which faceless schemes were notified: 1) to eliminate interface, 2) to optimise utilisation of resources, 3) to enable team-based functioning, and 4) to enable dynamic jurisdiction. Almost all the above provisions talked about the 1\n\nst\n\n, 2\n\nnd\n\n, and 4\n\nth\n\npurposes. However, the 3\n\nrd\n\npurpose, i.e., team-based functioning, was prescribed only under specified provisions. Under those specified provisions (except for the exercise of powers under section 142 and for assessments), no scheme has been issued yet. The manner of faceless assessment was prescribed under Section 144B itself. Further, the faceless schemes issued for the exercise of powers under Section 142 also refer to Section 144B.\n\n3.2-2.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, a single Section 532 (Power to frame schemes) is introduced to give the power to the Central Government to make schemes.\n\nUnder Section 532, the Central Government is empowered to make schemes for any purpose under the 2025 Act\n\nto impart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation.\n\nIt must be noted that the purpose of team-based functioning and dynamic jurisdiction is absent from Section 532 of the 2025 Act. Further, it also provides that if a scheme was already notified under the 1961 Act, the Central Government may amend or modify such scheme.\n\nThe transition provision, i.e., Section 536 of the 2025 Act, provides that any scheme which has been notified under the provisions of the repealed Income-tax Act with a view to eliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person, the said scheme shall be deemed to have been made and shall continue in force accordingly:\n\n(a)\n\nunder the corresponding provisions of this Act; or\n\n(b)\n\nunder section 532, where there is no such corresponding provision.\n\nIt must be noted that, though Section 532 empowers the Central Government to make a scheme for any purpose under the 2025 Act, there are specific provisions as well wherein the power has been given to make a scheme. Such provisions are as follows:\n\nFaceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities Section 245 of the 2025 Act/Section 130 of the 1961 Act\n\nFaceless collection of information Section 260 of the 2025 Act/Section 135A of the 1961 Act\n\nUnder these two specific provisions, the scheme may be made for all four purposes as were there under the old Act. Thus, the purpose of the scheme may also involve team-based functioning with dynamic jurisdiction, alongside eliminating the interface between the income-tax authority and the assessee and optimum utilisation of resources.\n\nFurther, it is provided under the 2025 Act that the manner of faceless assessment as currently provided under the relevant provision of the Act itself will be provided under the rules.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 264A:\nSection 92CA (ITA 1961) v. Section 166/Section 532 (ITA 2025)\n\nReference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\n1. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nChange in the time limit to pass the order by the Transfer Pricing Officer Section 166 of ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 92CA of ITA 1961)\n\n3.1-1.\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nSection 92CA of the ITA 1961 deals with the case where the assessee has entered into an international transaction or specified domestic transaction in any previous year, and the AO may refer the computation of the Arms Length Price (ALP) in relation to the said international transaction or specified domestic transaction under Section 92C to the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO).\n\nSub-section (3A) to section 92CA states that TPO is required to pass an order before sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation under section 153, or as the case may be, in Section 153B for making the order of assessment, reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, as the case may be, expires.\n\n3.1-1a.\n\nExclusion of the last day of assessment limitation\n\nThe interpretation of the phrase before sixty days prior to the date has been a consistent point of contention between taxpayers and the revenue, leading to significant litigation.\n\nThe Madras High Court, in the case of\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras)\n\n, which affirmed the Single Judges decision in\n\nJt. CIT vs. Pfizer Healthcare India (P.) Ltd. 2021 124 taxmann.com 536 (Madras)\n\n, held that the word date in Section 92CA(3A) refers to the last date for the AO to pass the assessment order under Section 153 (e.g., December 31st). The crucial words prior to indicate that, for the purpose of calculating the 60 days, this last date must be excluded. Therefore, if the assessment limitation expires on December 31st, the TPO order ought to have been passed on or before October 31st.\n\nThis interpretation was consistently followed across numerous ITAT benches, including in the following cases:\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nZydus Lifesciences Ltd. 2024 163 taxmann.com 349 (Ahmedabad Trib.)\n\nFrost & Sullivan (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 739 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nShell India Markets (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 366 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nSiemens Technology and Services (P.) Ltd. 2024 162 taxmann.com 357 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nHuhtamaki India Ltd. 2024 164 taxmann.com 194 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nAtos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nCAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\nPioneer India Electronics (P.) Ltd. 2025 178 taxmann.com 553 (Delhi Trib.)\n\nSigma Aldrich Chemicals (P.) Ltd. 2022 141 taxmann.com 431 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\n3.1-1b.\n\nMandatory nature of the time limit\n\nAnother critical aspect clarified by the judiciary is whether the time limit prescribed in Section 92CA(3A) is mandatory or merely directory. The revenue often argued that the use of the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A) implies a directory nature.\n\nHowever, the Madras High Court in\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras\n\n)\n\n, after a detailed analysis of the scheme of the Act and the consequences of non-compliance, unequivocally held that the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A)\n\nhas to be construed as shall and the time period fixed therein has to be scrupulously followed.\n\nThe court reasoned that the overall scheme of transfer pricing assessments, with various interlinked time limits (including extensions under the\n\nproviso\n\nto Section 92CA(3A) and Section 153), indicates a legislative intent to ensure timely completion. If the TPOs time limit were merely directory, it would undermine the entire assessment process. This principle has been reiterated in the case of\n\nAsst. CIT vs. CAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore - Trib.).\n\n3.1-1c.\n\nConsequence of time-barred TPO order\n\nThe judicial consensus is that a TPO order passed beyond the prescribed limitation period is\n\nillegal, null and void ab initio\n\nand\n\nnon est in the eyes of law\n\n. This has a cascading effect on subsequent assessment proceedings.\n\nAs established in\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai - Trib.) and Atos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai - Trib.),\n\nif the TPO order is time-barred, the assessee ceases to be an eligible assessee under Section 144C(15)(b)(i). Consequently, the extended time period for assessment under Section 153 (due to TPO reference) becomes inapplicable, and the draft assessment order and the final assessment order passed by the AO also become\n\nvoid ab initio\n\nand are liable to be quashed. This principle has been consistently applied across various ITAT decisions, such as\n\nIQVIA RDS (India) (P.) Ltd. 2025 181 taxmann.com 623 (Bangalore - Trib.) and World Sports (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 154 taxmann.com 181 (Mumbai - Trib.).\n\n3.1-2.\n\nAmendment by the Finance Act 2026\n\nThe Finance Act 2026 inserted the following sub-section (3AA) to section 92CA:\n\n(3AA). Notwithstanding anything contained in any judgment, order or decree of any court, for the purposes of making order under sub-section (3), the calculation of sixty days shall be made and shall always be deemed to have been made in the following manner, namely:\n\n(a) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (not being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 30th of January of that year;\n\n(b) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 31st of January of that year;\n\n(c) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of December of any year, the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 1st of November of that year.\n\nThe new sub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA clarifies the\n\n60-day limitation\n\nperiod for passing an order under Section 92CA(3). It provides deemed computation rules:\n\n(a)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (non-leap year), the order can be passed up to 30th January.\n\n(b)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (in a leap year), the order can be passed up to 31st January.\n\n(c)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 December, the order can be passed up to 1\n\nst\n\nNovember.\n\nThe most significant aspect of this amendment is its retrospective application with effect from 01-06-2007 and the explicit notwithstanding clause, which states:\n\nNotwithstanding any judgment, order, or decree of any court\n\n.\n\nThis phrase clearly indicates a legislative intent to override and nullify any judicial pronouncements that may have interpreted the sixty-day period differently or in a manner perceived to be contrary to the revenues interest.\n\nFor instance\n\n, in the table below, the limitation period after the retrospective amendment is explained:\n\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | Position before amendment | Position before amendment | Position after amendment | Position after amendment |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | 60 days expires on (as per court rulings) | Legal Status of TPO order | 60 days expires on (after amendment) | Legal Status of TPO order |\n| 2021-22 | 31-12-2023 * | 01-11-2023 | 31-10-2023 | Time-Barred | 01-11-2023 | Valid |\n| 2022-23 | 31-03-2025 # | 31-01-2025 | 30-01-2025 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2025 | Valid |\n| 2023-24 | 31-03-2026 # | 31-01-2026 | 30-01-2026 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2026 | Valid |\n| * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. |\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nImpact on the validity of past TPO orders\n\nSub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA, with its retrospective operation, validates all TPO orders that were held time-barred by courts due to a 1-day delay. The notwithstanding clause ensures that no court order invalidating such TPO orders can survive as binding precedent on this specific point. This means all assessments set aside solely because the TPO order was issued one day beyond the judicially interpreted deadline are revived. Further, for cases currently in litigation in which the validity of a TPO order is challenged on limitation grounds, the retrospective amendment fundamentally alters the legal landscape.\n\n3.1-3.\n\nPosition under the ITA 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act 2026)\n\nSub-section (7) of Section 166 (corresponding to Section 92CA(3) of the ITA 1961) provided a similar time limit of sixty days before the expiry of the limitation period for making the order of assessment, reassessment, recomputation or fresh assessment. The Finance Act 2026 makes an amendment to Section 166 by substituting sub-section (7), which reads as follows:\n\n(7) Where a reference was made under sub-section (1), an order under sub-section (6) may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which period of limitation referred to in section 286 or 296, for making the order of assessment or reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, expires and accordingly, where such period expires on\n\n(a) the 31st March of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st January of that year;\n\n(b) the 31st December of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st October of that year.\n\nThis amendment will take effect from 1st April 2026 and will, accordingly, apply in relation to the tax year 2026-2027 and subsequent years.\n\n3.1-3a.\n\nShift from day-based to month-based computation\n\nThe substituted provision takes a notable departure from the ITA 1961 framework by replacing the day-based computation of limitation periods with a month-based approach. Section 166(7) provides that:\n\n.order may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which the period of limitation .. expires\n\nUnder Section 92CA of the ITA 1961, limitation periods and procedural timelines were prescribed in terms of days, sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation expires. Section 166(7) of the ITA 2025 sets out the timelines, expressed in months. The computation is straightforward. The TPO merely needs to identify the month in which the limitation expires and ensure that the order is passed in the month immediately before that month. Further, the substituted provision provides that where the limitation period expires on 31\n\nst\n\nMarch or 31\n\nst\n\nDecember, as the case may be, the order under Section 166(6) should be passed on or before 31\n\nst\n\nJanuary or 31\n\nst\n\nOctober, respectively.\n\n3.1-3b.\n\nIllustration\n\nThe limitation period to pass order under section 166(7) under ITA 2025 is as follows:\n\n| Tax Year | Section 286 limitation period | Extension of limitation period as matter is referred to TPO | Limitation period under Section 286 (including extension) | Time limit to pass order under section 166(7) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| 2024-25 | 31-03-2027 | 12 months | 31-03-2028 | 31-01-2028 |\n| 2025-26 | 31-03-2028 | 12 months | 31-03-2029 | 31-01-2029 |\n\n3.2.\n\nPowers given to the central government to make faceless schemes for various provisions\n\n3.2-1.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder the 1961 Act,\n\nthe Central Government was empowered to notify schemes under various sections for various purposes\n\n.\n\nThe intent of such schemes was to\n\nimpart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface between the Income-tax authority, adjudication/appellant authorities (ITAT, DRP/DRC, Board of Advance Rulings) and the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation;\n\n(c)\n\nIntroducing disposal of appeals/disputes, imposition of penalty and a team-based functioning, with dynamic jurisdiction.\n\nThe relevant provisions under which the Central Government was empowered to notify such schemes and their respective purposes were as follows:\n\n| FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Section | Description | Notified Scheme | Purpose | Purpose | Purpose | Purpose |\n| Section | prescribed under Section 144B itself | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Faceless Appeal Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 253 and 255 | Disposal of appeal by ITAT | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 245D | Faceless functioning of the Interim Board |
