to the welfare of workers employed in that business or, all businesses of that class;\n( 28 ) “securities transaction tax” shall have the meaning assigned to it under Chapter VII of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2004 (23 of 2004);\n( 29 ) “service”, for the purposes of section 26(2)( h ) 88 , means a service of any description which is made available to potential users and includes the provision of services in connection with business of any industrial or commercial nature such as—\n( a ) accounting;\n( b ) banking;\n( c ) communication;\n( d ) conveying of news or information;\n( e ) advertising;\n( f ) entertainment;\n( g ) amusement;\n( h ) education;\n( i ) financing;\n( j ) insurance;\n( k ) chit funds;\n( l ) real estate;\n( m ) construction;\n( n ) transport;\n( o ) storage;\n( p ) processing;\n( q ) supply of electrical or other energy; and\n( r ) boarding and lodging;\n( 30 ) “small enterprise” 89 shall be an enterprise classified as such under the notification in this behalf by the Central Government under the Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Development Act, 2006 (27 of 2006);\n90 ( 31 ) 91 “speculative transaction” 92 means a transaction in which a contract 92 for the purchase or sale of any commodity 92 , including stocks and shares, is periodically or ultimately 92 settled 92 otherwise than by the actual delivery 92 or transfer of the commodity or scrips, other than the following transactions:—\n( a ) a specified derivative 92 transaction as defined in clause ( 33 );\n( b ) a contract in respect of raw materials or merchandise entered into by a person in the course of his manufacturing or merchandising business to guard against loss through future price fluctuations in respect of his contracts for actual delivery of goods manufactured, or merchandise sold by him;\n( c ) a contract in respect of stocks and shares entered into by a dealer or investor therein to guard against loss in his holdings of stocks and shares through price fluctuations;\n( d ) a contract entered into by a member of a forward market or a stock exchange in the course of any transaction in the nature of jobbing or arbitrage, to guard against loss which may arise in the ordinary course of his 92 business as such member;\n92a ( 32 ) “Specified Banking or Online Mode” shall mean transaction by an account payee cheque or an account payee bank draft or use of electronic clearing system through a bank account 92 or through such other electronic mode, as may be prescribed 92a ;\n90 ( 33 ) “specified derivative transaction” means any transaction in respect of trading in derivatives referred to in section 2( ac ) 93 of the Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 (42 of 1956); or in respect of trading in commodity derivatives (other than agricultural commodity derivatives) which is chargeable to commodities transaction tax under Chapter VII of the Finance Act, 2013 (17 of 2013) or in respect of trading in agricultural commodity derivatives, if such transactions are—\n( a ) is carried out—\n( i ) through a stock broker or such other intermediary registered under section 12 of the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992 (15 of 1992) in accordance with the provisions of the Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956 (42 of 1956) or the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992 (15 of 1992) or the Depositories Act, 1996 (22 of 1996) and the rules, regulations or bye-laws made or directions issued under those Acts; or\n( ii ) by banks or mutual funds,\nelectronically on screen-based systems of a recognised stock exchange; and\n( b ) supported by a time stamped contract note issued by the intermediary to every client indicating in the contract note—\n( i ) the unique client identity number allotted under any law in force; and\n( ii ) the Permanent Account Number allotted under this Act;\n( 34 ) “State Government undertaking” includes—\n( a ) a corporation established by or under any State Act;\n( b ) a company in which more than 50% of the paid-up equity share capital is held by the State Government;\n( c ) a company in which more than 50% of the paid-up equity share capital is held by the entity referred to in clause ( a ) or ( b ) (whether singly or taken together);\n( d ) a company or corporation in which the State Government has the right to appoint the majority of the directors or to control the management or policy decisions, directly or indirectly, including by virtue of its shareholding or management rights or shareholders agreements or voting agreements or in any other manner;\n( e ) an authority, a board or an institution or a body established or constituted by or under any State Act, or owned or controlled by the State Government;\n( 35 ) “State Industrial Investment Corporation” means a Government company within the meaning of section 2( 45 ) 94 of the Companies Act, 2013 (18 of 2013), engaged in the business of providing long-term finance for industrial projects;\n( 36 ) “State Financial Corporation” means a Financial Corporation established under section 3 or 3A or an institution notified under section 46 of the State Financial Corporations Act, 1951 (63 of 1951) 94a ;\n( 37 ) “successor entity” means—\n( a ) the amalgamated Indian company, in the case of amalgamation;\n( b ) the resulting Indian company, in the case of demerger;\n( c ) a company, in case of a succession of a firm by a company as referred to in section 70(1)( zd ) 95 ;\n( d ) a limited liability partnership, in case of conversion of private company or unlisted public company to a limited liability partnership, as referred to in section 70(1)( ze ) 96 ;\n( 38 ) “taxable commodities transaction” shall have the meaning assigned to it under Chapter No. 7) 97 .\n\nSection - 40:\nSpecial provision for computation of cost of acquisition of certain assets. [ S. 43C of the 1961 Act ]\n40. (1) For the purposes of computation of income under the head “Profits and gains of business or profession”, cost of acquisition of an asset which becomes property of—\n( a ) an amalgamated company under a scheme of amalgamation; or\n( b ) an assessee, under a gift, or will, or an irrevocable trust, or on total or partial partition of a Hindu undivided family,\nwhen sold as stock-in-trade shall be the sum of—\n( i ) cost of acquisition of the said asset in the hands of the amalgamating company in case of clause ( a ), or the transferor or donor in case of clause ( b );\n( ii ) any cost of improvement made;\n( iii ) any expenditure incurred by the amalgamating company or transferor or donor, as the case may be, wholly and exclusively in connection with such transfer.\n(2) This section shall not apply to an asset referred to in section 67(6) 39 .\n\nSection - 36:\nExpenses or payments not deductible in certain circumstances. [ S. 40A of the 1961 Act ]\n6 36 7 . (1) The provisions of this section shall have effect irrespective of anything to the contrary contained in any other provision of this Act relating to computation of income under the head “Profits and gains of business or profession”.\n7a (2) If the assessee incurs any expenditure for which payment has been or is to be made to any “specified person” 8 , which in the opinion of the Assessing Officer is excessive or unreasonable having regard to the—\n( a ) fair market value of the goods, services or facilities; or\n( b ) legitimate needs of the business or profession of the assessee; or\n( c ) benefit derived by or accruing to the assessee therefrom,\nso much of the expenditure as considered excessive or unreasonable by him shall not be allowed as a deduction.\n7a (3) For the purposes of sub-section (2) and this sub-section,—\n( a ) “specified person” shall mean the following,—\n( i ) in relation to an assessee mentioned in column B of the Table below, the person referred to in column C thereof:—\nTABLE\nAssessee Sl. No. Specified person\nA B C\n1 . Individual. Any relative of the assessee.\n2. Company. Any director of the company or his relative.\n3. Firm. Partner of the firm or his relative.\n4. Association of persons. Member of the association or his relative.\n5. Hindu undivided family. Member of the family or his relative;\n( ii ) any person being an individual or company or firm or association of persons or Hindu undivided family having substantial interest in the business or profession of the assessee, or any director, partner, member thereof or any relatives of such individual, director, partner, member or any other company in which the first mentioned company has substantial interest;\n( iii ) a company, firm, association of persons, or Hindu undivided family whose director, partner or member has substantial interest in the business or profession of the assessee, or any director, partner or member thereof and their relatives, as the case may be;\n( iv ) any person carrying on a business or profession, where assessee, being—\n( A ) an individual or his relative; or\n( B ) a company, its directors or their relatives; or\n( C ) a firm, its partners or their relatives; or\n( D ) an association of persons, its members or their relatives; or\n( E ) a Hindu undivided family, its members or their relatives,\nhas substantial interest in the business or profession of such person;\n( b ) a person is deemed to have “substantial interest in the business or profession” if—\n( i ) in a case where the business or profession is carried on by a company, such person is, at any time during the tax year, the beneficial owner of shares (not being shares entitled to a fixed rate of dividend whether with or without a right to participate in profits) carrying not less than 20% of the voting power; and\n( ii ) in any other case, such person is, at any time during the tax year, beneficially entitled to not less than 20% of the profits of such business or profession.\n9 (4) 10 Where in respect of any expenditure 11 incurred by the assessee, any payment or aggregate of payments made in a day to a person exceeds ₹10000 and is not made through specified banking or online mode, then the expenditure by way of such payments shall not be allowed as a deduction.\n9 (5) Where any deduction was made in any preceding tax year for a liability incurred for any expenditure and payment in respect of such liability is made during a subsequent tax year and if such payment or aggregate of payments made in a day to a person exceeds ₹10000 and is not made through specified banking or online mode, such payment shall be deemed to be the income under the head “Profits and gains of business or profession” in such subsequent tax year.\n(6) For the purposes of sub-sections (4) and (5), the figures “₹10000” shall be read as “₹ 35000” in case the payment is made for plying, hiring or leasing of goods carriages.\n(7) The provisions of sub-sections (4) and (5) shall not be applicable in cases and circumstances, as may be prescribed 11a , having regard to the nature and extent of banking facilities available, considerations of business expediency and other relevant factors.\n(8) Nothing (with reference to mode of payment) contained in any other law in force or in any contract, shall apply in respect of any payment which has been made through specified banking or online mode, in compliance of sub-sections (4) to (7), and no plea shall be allowed to be raised, in any suit or other proceeding on the ground that the payment was not made or tendered in cash or in mode other than through specified banking or online mode.\n(9) No deduction or allowance shall be allowed in respect of marked to market loss or other expected loss, except as allowable under section 32(1) * ( h ) 12 .\n\nSection - 14:\nIncome not forming part of total income and expenditure in relation to such income. [ S. 14A of the 1961 Act ]\n29 14. (1) Irrespective of anything to the contrary contained in this Act, for the purposes of computing the total income under this Chapter, no deduction shall be allowed in respect of expenditure incurred 30 by the assessee in relation to 30 income which does not form part of the total income 30 .\n(2) Where the Assessing Officer, having regard to the accounts of the assessee, is not satisfied with—\n( a ) the correctness of the claim of expenditure incurred by the assessee; or\n( b ) the claim made by the assessee that no expenditure has been incurred,\nin relation to income which does not form part of the total income under this Act, he shall determine such amount of expenditure in accordance with any method, as may be prescribed 31 .\n(3) Irrespective of anything to the contrary contained in this Act, the provisions of this section shall apply in a case where any expenditure has been incurred during any tax year in relation to income which does not form part of the total income under this Act, but such income has not accrued or arisen or has not been received during that tax year.\n\nSection - 536:\nRepeal and savings. [ S. 297 of the 1961 Act ]\n536 . (1) The Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) is hereby repealed.\n(2) Irrespective of the repeal of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) (herein referred to as the repealed Income-tax Act), and subject to 64 [ sub-section (4) ] —\n( a ) nothing shall affect the previous operation of the repealed Income-tax Act and orders or anything duly done or suffered thereunder; or\n( b ) nothing shall affect any right, privilege, obligation or liability acquired, accrued or incurred under the repealed Income-tax Act or orders under such repealed Act;\n( c ) the provisions of the repealed Income-tax Act shall continue to apply to any proceeding pending on the date of commencement of this Act and to any proceedings initiated on or after the 1st April, 2026 (including notices, assessment, reassessment, recomputation, rectification, penalty, reference, revision and appeals) in respect of any tax year beginning before the 1st April, 2026 and such proceedings shall be carried out as per the procedure specified in the repealed Income-tax Act;\n( d ) any proceeding for the imposition of a penalty in respect of any tax year beginning before the 1st April, 2026, may be initiated and any such penalty may be imposed under the repealed Income-tax Act, as if this Act had not been enacted;\n( e ) any proceeding pending on the commencement of this Act before any income-tax authority or any other authority constituted under the repealed Income-tax Act, Appellate Tribunal, or any court, by way of application, appeal, reference or revision or by any other means, shall be continued and disposed of as if this Act had not been enacted;\n( f ) any election or declaration made, or option exercised, by an assessee under any provision of the repealed Income-tax Act and in force immediately before the commencement of this Act shall be deemed to have been an election or declaration made, or option exercised, under the corresponding provision of this Act;\n64a [ ( g ) where in respect of any proceeding relating to any tax year beginning before the 1st April, 2026,—\n( i ) a refund falls due on or after such date; or\n( ii ) default is made on or after such date, in the payment of any sum due under such proceeding 65 ,\nthe provisions of the repealed Income-tax Act, relating to the interest payable by the Central Government on refunds or the interest payable by the assessee for default, shall apply for the period on or after such date, subject to the effect that—\n( A ) the rate of interest on refund or on the default, as the case may be, as provided in the repealed Income-tax Act has been substituted with the rate as provided in the corresponding provisions of this Act; and\n( B ) such substitution shall apply from the date on which such rate has been modified under this Act; ]\n66 [ ( h ) where any sum has been allowed as a deduction or has not been included in the total income of any person, either on account of fulfilment of certain conditions or for any other reason, for any tax year beginning before the 1st April, 2026, and such sum was required to be included in the total income of any subsequent tax year including beginning on or after the 1st April, 2026 under the repealed Income-tax Act, if it had not been so repealed, on account of violation of such conditions or for any other reason, then such sum shall be––\n(i) deemed to be the income of such subsequent tax year; and\n( ii ) included in the total income of the said person under the same head of income as it would have been included under the repealed Income-tax Act; ]\n( i ) any sum payable under the repealed Income-tax Act may be recovered under this Act without prejudice to any action already taken for the recovery of such sum under repealed Income-tax Act;\n( j ) any agreement entered into, appointment made, approval given, recognition granted, circular, direction, instruction, notification, order or rule or any scheme framed therein issued under any provision of the repealed Income-tax Act shall, so far as it is not inconsistent with the corresponding provisions of this Act, be deemed to have been entered into, made, granted, given or issued under the corresponding provision of this Act and shall continue in force accordingly;\n( k ) where the period provided for any application, appeal, reference or revision under the repealed Income-tax Act had expired on or before the commencement of this Act, nothing in this Act shall be construed as enabling any such application, appeal, reference or revision to be made under this Act by reason only of the fact that a longer period therefor is prescribed or provision is made for extension of time in suitable cases by the appropriate authority;\n( l ) any amount of credit, in respect of tax paid, allowable to be carried forward in the case of an assessee, under the provisions of section 115JAA or 115JD of the repealed Income-tax Act for the tax year beginning before the 1st April, 2026, had the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) not been repealed,—\n67 [ ( i ) shall be deemed to be the amount eligible for credit under corresponding provisions or section 206(3) or (4) 68 of this Act, as the case may be in the case of said assessee; and\n( ii ) credit for the tax paid under the repealed Income-tax Act shall be allowed under this Act for the period for which it would have been allowed under the repealed Income-tax Act if the assessee otherwise continues to satisfy the conditions as specified in the corresponding provisions or section 206(3) or (4) 68 of this Act, as the case may be in such tax years; 141:\nDeduction in respect of profits and gains from certain industrial under-takings. 52 [ S. 80-IB of the 1961 Act ]\n53 141. 54 In respect of any tax year, where—\n( a ) the gross total income of an assessee, includes any profits and gains derived from 55 any business referred to in section 80-IB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961); and\n( b ) such assessee is eligible to claim a deduction from the profits and gains derived from such business for such tax year under the provisions of the said section, as if the said Act had not been repealed,\nthere shall be allowed, in computing the total income of the assessee, a deduction from the profits and gains derived from such business, subject to the conditions that—\n( i ) the amount of deduction is calculated as per the provisions of section 80-IB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961); and\n( ii) the deduction under this Act shall be allowed only for such tax years, as would have been allowed under section 80-IB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961), as if the said Act had not been repealed.\n\nSection - 532:\nPower to frame schemes. [ Ss. 92CA, 142B, 144C, 151A, 157A, 231, 245MA, 245R, 245W, 250, 253, 255, 264A, 264B, 274, 279 and 293D of the 1961 Act ]\n43 532 . (1) The Central Government may, by notification, make a scheme 43a for any of the purposes of this Act, so as to impart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by—\n( a ) eliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible 44 ;\n( b ) optimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation.\n(2) The Central Government may, for the purposes of giving effect to the scheme made under sub-section (1), by notification, direct that any of the provisions of this Act shall not apply or shall apply with such exceptions, modifications and adaptations as specified in the notification.\n(3) Where a scheme has been notified under the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) with a view to eliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person, the Central Government may, by notification, amend or modify the said scheme as per the provisions of sub-section (1), and the provisions of sub-section (2) shall apply accordingly.\n(4) Every notification issued under sub-sections (1), (2) and (3) shall, as soon as may be after the notification is issued, be laid before each House of Parliament.\n\nSection - 162:\nMeaning of associated enterprise. [ Ss. 92A, 92CE & 94B of the 1961 Act ]\n28 162. 29 (1) For the purposes of this Chapter, the expression “associated enterprise”, in relation to another enterprise, means an enterprise—\n( a ) which participates, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in the management or control or capital of the other enterprise in the following manner,—\n( i ) one or more persons who participate, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in management or control or capital of one enterprise, also participate, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in the management or control or capital of the other enterprise; or\n( ii ) one enterprise holds, at any time during the tax year, directly or indirectly, shares carrying not less than 26% of the voting power in the other enterprise; or\n( iii ) any person or enterprise holds, at any time during the tax year, directly or indirectly, shares carrying not less than 26% of the voting power in each of such enterprises; or\n( b ) which has advanced a loan to the other enterprise and such loan constitutes not less than 51% of the book value of the total assets of the other enterprise; or\n( c ) which guarantees not less than 10% of the total borrowings of the other enterprise; or\n( d ) whose more than half of the board of directors or members of the governing board, or one or more executive directors or executive members of the governing board, are appointed by the other enterprise; or\n( e ) whose more than half of the directors or members of the governing board, or one or more of the executive directors or members of the governing board, are appointed by the same person or persons, who has or have done so for the other enterprise; or\n( f ) in case of which, manufacturing or processing of goods or articles or business carried out by such enterprise is wholly dependent on the use of know-how, patents, copyrights, trademarks, licences, franchises or any other business or commercial rights of similar nature, or any data, documentation, drawing or specification relating to any patent, invention, model, design, secret formula or process, of which the other enterprise is the owner or in respect of which the other enterprise has exclusive rights; or\n( g ) in case of which, 90% or more of the raw materials and consumables required for the manufacture or processing of goods or articles carried out by such enterprise, are supplied by the other enterprise, or by persons specified by the other enterprise, and the prices and other conditions relating to the supply are influenced 30 by such other enterprise; or\n( h ) in case of which, the goods or articles manufactured or processed by such enterprise, are sold to the other enterprise or to persons specified by the other enterprise, and the prices and other conditions relating thereto are influenced 30 by such other enterprise; or\n( i ) which is controlled by an individual, and the other enterprise is also controlled by such individual or his relative or jointly by such individual and relative of such individual; or\n( j ) which is controlled by a Hindu undivided family, and the other enterprise is controlled by a member of such Hindu undivided family or by a relative of a member of such Hindu undivided family or jointly by such member and his relative; or\n( k ) which is a firm, association of persons or body of individuals, and the other enterprise holds not less than 10% interest in such firm, association of persons or body of individuals; or\n( l ) which has any relationship of mutual interest with the other enterprise, as may be prescribed.\n(2) In relation to a specified domestic transaction entered into by an assessee, associated enterprise shall also include—\n( a ) other units or undertakings or businesses of such assessee in respect of a transaction referred to in section 122 or 140(9) 31 ;\n( b ) any other person referred to in section 140(13) or 205(4) 32 in respect of a transaction referred to therein; and\n33 [ ( c ) other units, undertakings, enterprises or business of such assessee, or other person referred to in section 140(13) 34 in respect of transactions referred to in Chapter VIII, to which the provisions of section 140(9) or (13) 35 of this Act or section 80-IA(8) or (10) of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) are applicable. ]\n\nSection - 355:\n7. —Interpretation\nInterpretation. [ Ss. 2(15), 11, 12, 13, 115BBC, 115TD, 115TE and 115TF of the 1961 Act ]\n355. For the purposes of this Part,—\n( a ) “anonymous donation” means any voluntary contribution referred to in section 2( 49 )( c ) 84 , where a person receiving such contribution does not maintain a record of the identity indicating the name and address of the person making such contribution and such other particulars, as may be prescribed;\n( b ) “approval” means an approval under the second proviso to section 80G(5) of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) or section 354 ;\n( c ) “cancellation” includes withdrawal;\n( d ) “donation” means any voluntary contribution received by a registered non-profit organisation from any person;\n( e ) “commercial activity” means any activity in the nature of trade, commerce or business, or any activity of rendering any service in relation to any trade, commerce or business, for a cess or fee or any other consideration, irrespective of the nature of use or application, or retention, of the income from such activity;\n( f ) “registration” includes provisional registration, provisional approval or approval, as referred to in the second proviso to section 10( 23C ) or 12AB(1) of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) and under section 332 , but shall not include approval under the second proviso to section 80G(5) of the said Act or section 354 ;\n( g ) “registered non-profit organisation” means any person having a valid registration under any specified provision and such registration has not been cancelled;\n84a ( h ) “related person” means any of the following persons:—\n( i ) the author or the founder 85 of the registered non-profit organisation;\n( ii ) any person whose total contribution to such registered non-profit organisation, during the relevant tax year exceeds ₹ 100000, or, in aggregate up to the end of the relevant tax year exceeds ten lakh rupees, as the case may be;\n( iii ) where such author, founder or person is a Hindu undivided family, a member of the family;\n( iv ) any trustee or manager (by whatever name called) of the registered non-profit organisation;\n( v ) any relative of any persons referred to in sub-clause ( i ), ( iii ) or ( iv );\n( vi ) any concern in which any of the persons referred to in sub-clause ( i ), ( iii ), ( iv ) or ( v ) has a substantial interest;\n( i ) “relative”, in relation to an individual, means—\n( i ) spouse of the individual;\n( ii ) brother or sister of the individual;\n( iii ) brother or sister of the spouse of the individual;\n( iv ) any lineal ascendant (maternal or paternal) or descendant of the individual;\n( v ) any lineal ascendant (maternal or paternal) or descendant of the spouse of the individual;\n( vi ) spouse of a person referred to in sub-clause ( ii ), ( iii ), ( iv ) or ( v );\n( vii ) any lineal descendant of a brother or sister of either the individual or of the spouse of the individual;\n( j ) “residual income” means the total income without giving effect to the provisions of this Part, as reduced by regular income and specified income;\n( k ) “specified asset” means any asset which is established to have been directly acquired by the specified person—\n( i ) out of its income of the nature referred to in Schedule II (Table: Sl. No. 1) 86 ;\n( ii ) during the period beginning from the date of its creation or establishment and ending on the date from which the registration under specified provision became effective, if the specified person has not been allowed any benefit under this Part or under sections 11 and 12 or section 10( 23C )( iv ) or ( v ) or ( vi ) or ( via ) of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) during the said period, where provisions of the first proviso or the second proviso to section 12A(2) or the eighth proviso to section 10( 23C ) of the said Act, are not applicable;\n( iii ) during the period beginning from the date of its creation or establishment and ending on the date from which the registration under specified provision became effective due to the provisions of the first proviso or the second proviso to section 12A(2) or the eighth proviso to section 10( 23C ),where provisions of the first proviso or the second proviso to section 12A(2) or the eighth proviso to section 10( 23C ), of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961), are applicable; and\n( iv ) which has been transferred to any other specified person within twelve months from the end of the month in which the dissolution takes place in respect of a case specified in section 352(4) (Table: Sl. No. 9) 87 ;\n( l ) “specified person” means any person which is registered under any specified provision at any time since its incorporation or creation;\n( m ) “specified provision” means section 12A, 12AA or 12AB or section 10( 23C ) of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961) or section 332 ;\n( n ) “substantial interest”, in relation to a person in a concern, means—\n( i ) in a case where the concern is a company, if its shares (not being shares entitled to a fixed rate of dividend whether with or without a further right to participate in profits) carrying not less than 20% of the voting power are, at any time during the tax year, owned beneficially by such person or partly by such person and partly by one or more of the other related persons; or\n( ii ) in the case of any other concern, if such person is entitled, or such person and one or more of the other related persons are entitled in the aggregate at any time during the tax year, to not less than 20% of the profits of such concern;\n( o ) “value” means the value of any benefit or facility granted or provided free of cost or at concessional rate to any related person;\n( p ) “wholly for charitable or religious purposes” shall mean wholly for charitable purposes or wholly for religious purposes or wholly for charitable and religious purposes.\n\nSchedule XIV:\n39 SCHEDULE XIV 40\n[ See section 55 ]\n41 INSURANCE BUSINESS\nA.—Life insurance business\nProfits of life insurance business to be computed separately.\n1. If a person is engaged in life insurance business during the tax year, the profits and gains of such business shall be computed separately from profits and gains of any other business.\nComputation of profits of life insurance business.\n2. (1) The profits and gains * life insurance business shall be the annual average of the surplus after adjusting the surplus or deficit disclosed by the actuarial valuation made as per the Insurance Act, 1938 (4 of 1938) for the last inter-valuation period ending before the commencement of tax year, so as to exclude from it any surplus or deficit from any earlier inter-valuation period.\n(2) Any expenditure which is inadmissible under section 34 42 in computing the profits and gains of a business, shall be added to such profits and gains of life insurance business.\nAdjustment of tax paid by deduction at source.\n3. When an assessment of the life insurance business profits is made based on the annual average of a surplus disclosed by a valuation for an inter-valuation period exceeding twelve months, then, in computing the income-tax, payable for that year credit shall––\n( a ) not be given as per section 390 43 for the income-tax paid in the preceding tax year;\n( b ) be given for the annual average of the income-tax paid by deduction at source from interest on securities or otherwise during such period.\nB.—Other insurance business\nComputation of profits and gains of other insurance business.\n4. (1) The profits and gains of any insurance business other than life insurance shall be the profit before tax and appropriations as disclosed in the profit and loss account prepared as per the Insurance Act, 1938 (4 of 1938) or the rules made thereunder or the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority Act, 1999 (4 of 1999) or the regulations made subject to the following adjustments:––\n( a ) subject to the other provision of 44 [ this paragraph ] , any expenditure or allowance 45 including any amount debited to profit and loss account either by way of a provision for any tax, dividend, reserve, or any other provision as may be prescribed, which is inadmissible under sections 28 to 54 46 shall be added back;\n( b ) any gain or loss from realisation of investments shall be added or deducted, as the case may be, if not already credited or debited to the profit and loss account;\n( c ) any provision for diminution in investment value debited to the profit and loss account, shall be added back; and\n( d ) such amount carried over to a reserve for unexpired risks as may be prescribed shall be allowed as a deduction.\n(2) The amount payable under section 37 47 , which is added under sub-paragraph (1)( a ) shall be allowed as deduction in the tax year in which it is actually paid.\n48 [ (3) The amount not deductible under sub-clause ( i ) or ( ii ) of section 35 ( b ) 49 , which is added under sub-paragraph (1)( a ), shall be allowed subsequently as a deduction in a tax year in accordance with the provisions of the said sub-clause, as the case may be. ]\nC.—Other provisions\nProfits and gains of non-resident person.\n5. (1) The profits and gains of a person non-resident in India who is engaged in the insurance business through its branches in India may, in the absence of more reliable data, be deemed to be that proportion of his global income which corresponds to the proportion which his premium income derived from India bears to his total premium income.\n(2) In this paragraph, the global income in relation to life insurance business of a person not resident in India shall be computed as per this Act for computing the profits and gains of life Insurance business carried on in India.\nInterpretation.\n6. (1) For the purposes of this schedule,––\n( a ) “investments” include securities, stocks and shares;\n( b ) “life insurance business” 50 means life insurance business as defined in section 2( 11 ) of the Insurance Act, 1938 (4 of 1938).\n(2) References to the Insurance Act, 1938 (4 of 1938) in this Schedule regarding the Life Insurance Corporation of India shall be treated as references to that Act or section 43 51 of the Life Insurance Corporation Act, 1956 (31 of 1956).\n\nSection - 35:\nSection 40 (ITA 1961) v. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nSalary payment outside India or to a non-resident in India Section 40(a)(iii) of the 1961 Act/Section 35(c) of the 2025 Act\n\nSection 40 of the 1961 Act enumerates certain expenses which are disallowed, wholly or partly, while computing the taxable income of an assessee. Section 40(a)(iii) of the 1961 Act provides that if any payment was chargeable to tax under the head Salaries and such payment was payable either to any person outside India or to any non-resident in India, then such payment was not allowable as a deduction only when the following twin conditions were satisfied:\n\n| (a) | Tax has not been paid on such payment as per Chapter XVII-B; and |\n| --- | --- |\n\n| (b) | Tax has not been deducted on such payment as per Chapter XVII-B. |\n| --- | --- |\n\nHowever, under the 2025 Act, the above provision of the 1961 Act has been modified in line with the provisions of sections 40(a)(i), 40(a)(ia) and 40(a)(iib) of the 1961 Act which corresponds to sections 35(b)(i), 35(b)(ii) and 35(d) of the 2025 Act.\n\nIn effect, under the 1961 Act, deduction was not allowable under section 40(a)(iii) only if both the conditions are satisfied cumulatively, i.e., (i) no deduction of TDS; and (ii) no payment of TDS. But, under the 2025 Act, deduction is not allowable under section 35(c) if either\n\n| (i) | TDS is deductible but not deducted during the tax year; or |\n| --- | --- |\n\n| (ii) | TDS is deducted but has not been paid during the tax year. |\n| --- | --- |\n\nStill, there remains a difference in the provisions of sections 35(b)(i), 35(b)(ii) and 35(d) vis-à-vis section 35(c). In case of former sections, the deduction during the tax year is not allowable only if TDS is deducted but has not been paid up to the due date to file the return of income as specified in section 263(1) corresponds to Section 139(1) of the 1961 Act, however, in case of section 35(c), the deduction during the tax year is not allowable if TDS is deducted but has not been paid during that tax year. Thus, the extra timeline (i.e., payment of TDS up to the due date) has not been extended to the provisions of section 35(c), making this provision stricter and more restrictive than the former ones.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\n111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000009187 direct tax laws 0 111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000009188 international taxation 0 111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000017592 transfer pricing 0 111050000000020048 comparative 000012092025 111050000000020049 income tax act 1961 000120920251 0000 Section 40 (ITA 1961) v. Section 35/Section 66 (ITA 2025)\n\nSection - 66:\nSection 28 (ITA 1961) v. Section 26/Section 66 (ITA 2025)\n\nProfits and gains of business or profession\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 28\n\n: Profits and gains of business or profession\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 26\n\n: Income under head Profits and gains of business or profession\n\nSection 66\n\n: Interpretation\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 28 | Section 26(1) & (2) |\n| Section 28( i ) | Section 26(2)( a ) |\n| Section 28( ii )( a ) | Section 26(2)( b )( i )( A ) & long line |\n| Section 28( ii )( b ) | Section 26(2)( b )( i )( B ) & long line |\n| Section 28( ii )( c ) | Section 26(2)( b )( ii ) & long line |\n| Section 28( ii )( d ) | Section 26(2)( c ) |\n| Section 28( ii )( e ) | Section 26(2)( b )( iii ) & long line |\n| Section 28( iii ) | Section 26(2)( d ) |\n| Section 28( iiia ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iiib ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iiic ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iiid ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iiie ) | Section 26(2)( e ) |\n| Section 28( iv ) | Section 26(2)( f ) |\n| Section 28( v ) & its proviso | Section 26(2)( g ) |\n| Section 28( va ) | Section 26(2)( h ) |\n| Proviso to Section 28( va ) | Section 26(2)( h )( i )(A) & (B) |\n| Explanation (i) to Section 28( va ) | Section 66(1) |\n| Explanation (ii) to Section 28( va ) | Section 66(29) |\n| Section 28( vi ) | Section 26(2)( i ) |\n| Explanation to Section 28( vi ) | Section 66(8) |\n| Section 28( via ) | Section 26(2)( j ) |\n| Section 28( vii ) | Section 26(2)( k ) |\n| Explanation 2 to Section 28 | Section 26(3) |\n| Explanation 3 to Section 28 | Section 26(4) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nChanges in the charging provision for the profits and gains from business and profession\n\nThe following FAQs are issued by the Income-tax Department concerning Chapter IV-D containing sections 26 to 66 of the 2025 Act:\n\nQV.1 What are the major changes in the chapter Profits and gains of business and profession\n\nAns:\n\nMajor changes brought about in the chapter Profits and Gains of Business and Profession are as follows:\n\n(i)\n\nThe flow of the sections has been revised to improve coherence, create logical links, and ensure a smoother progression.\n\n(ii)\n\nSimilar sections, such as those on presumptive taxation for residents and non-residents, have been merged to simplify the content, make it more concise, and consolidate all related scenarios in one place.\n\n(iii)\n\nProvisions on similar topics, such as deductions on employee welfare, which were previously scattered across the chapter, have been grouped together for easier understanding and compliance.\n\n(iv)\n\nFormula-based explanations have been added to clarify complex concepts that were previously difficult to understand, such as the definition of Written-down Value.\n\n(v)\n\nProvisions involving multiple scenarios, like those for determining Actual Cost, which were previously presented as provisos or explanations, have been organized into a table for better readability and comprehension.\n\n(vi)\n\nAll provisos and explanations in the chapter have been converted into sub-sections to eliminate ambiguity and ensure clarity.\n\n(vii)\n\nRedundant and already sun-set provisions have been removed.\n\n(viii)\n\nProvisions specific to a single business segment which are used by small sub set of taxpayers have been moved to a new Schedule, while some procedural provisions have been transferred to rules to reduce complexity and focus on the substance of the section.\n\n(ix)\n\nAs a result, the total word count has been reduced by more than half, and the number of sections has decreased from 65 to 41.\n\nQV.2 How and why the order of sections been changed in the chapter on PGBP\n\nAns:\n\nAn effort has been made to group similar provisions together. For example, provisions related to general expenditure allowances (section 37) and deductions for actual payments (section 43B) have been placed along with the provisions of sections 40 and 40A. Similarly, provisions on scientific research expenditure, section 35AD (investment-linked deductions), skill development project expenditure, and agricultural extension project expenditure have been grouped together in the proposed sections 45, 46 and 47.\n\nQV.9 Which provisions have been shifted to Schedule and the reasons for the same\n\nAns:\n\nProvisions related to the Site Restoration Fund, as well as those for the Tea Development Account, Coffee Development Account, and Rubber Development Account, which apply only to specific trades and a small number of taxpayers, have been moved to the Schedules. The provisions related to the insurance business remain in the schedule as they were previously.\n\n3.1-1.\n\nAll export incentives are taxable Section 28(iiia) to (iiie) of the 1961 Act/Section 26(2)(e) of the 2025 Act\n\n3.1-1a.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nAs a general rule, all revenue receipts arising in the course of business shall be taxable under the head profits and gains from business or profession. Section 28 of the 1961 Act provides an inclusive list of all receipts or income that are chargeable to tax under this head, including the following.\n\n(a)\n\nProfits on the sale of licenses granted under the Import (Control) Order 1955\n\nSection 28(iiia)\n\n.\n\n(b)\n\nCash assistance received or receivable against exports under any Government of India scheme\n\nSection 28(iiib)\n\n.\n\n(c)\n\nAny customs or excise duty repaid or repayable as a drawback against exports\n\nSection 28(iiic)\n\n.\n\n(d)\n\nAny profit on the transfer of the Duty Entitlement Pass Book Scheme, being the Duty Remission Scheme under the export and import policy formulated and announced under section 5 of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 Section 28(iiid)\n\n.\n\n(e)\n\nany profit on the transfer of the Duty Free Replenishment Certificate, being the Duty Remission Scheme under the export and import policy formulated and announced under section 5 of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 Section 28(iiie)\n\n.\n\n3.1-1b.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nSection 26(2)(e) of the 2025 Act brings all kinds of import-export incentives within the ambit of the charging provision for taxability under the head of business income. The new provision refrained from specifying the name of the scheme under which the incentives or benefits are received for taxability. Instead, it provides that\n\nthe amount of any profit on sale of import licence, cash assistance against export, duty drawback or duty remission or any other export incentive, received or receivable\n\nshall be covered within the scope of business or profession income. Thus, all export benefit schemes, whether in the form of remission, refund, incentive, or profit on the sale of a license, shall be considered income and taxable as business income. Here is an illustrative list of Government Export Benefit Schemes:\n\n(a)\n\nDuty Remission and Refund Schemes\n\nRoDTEP (Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products)\n\nDuty Drawback Scheme\n\nRoSCT (Rebate of State and Central Taxes and Levies)\n\n(b)\n\nDuty-Free Import Schemes\n\nAdvance Authorization Scheme\n\nDuty-Free Import Authorization (DFIA)\n\n(c)\n\nCapital Goods Schemes\n\nExport Promotion Capital Goods (EPCG) Scheme\n\nPost Export EPCG\n\n(d)\n\nCredit and Financial Schemes\n\nNIRVIK Scheme\n\nECGC Export Credit Insurance\n\n(e)\n\nSpecial Economic Zone and Unit Benefits\n\nSpecial Economic Zones (SEZ) Benefits\n\nExport Oriented Units (EOU) Benefits\n\nDeemed Exports\n\n(f)\n\nSector-Specific Benefits\n\nTowns of Export Excellence (TEE)\n\nFocus Product and Market Schemes\n\n3.1-2.\n\nGross amount v. Profit arising from export incentives Sections 28(iiid) and 28(iiie) of the 1961 Act/Section 26(2)(e) of the 2025 Act\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder Sections 28(iiid) and 28(iiie) of the 1961 Act, only the profit portion on transfer of Duty Remission Scheme (such as the Duty Entitlement Pass Book Scheme and the Duty Free Replenishment Certificate) under the export and import policy formulated and announced under Section 5 of the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992 are taxable.\n\nThe Supreme Court, in the case of\n\nTopman Exports v. CIT 2012 18 taxmann.com 120 (SC)\n\n, provided a definitive interpretation of Section 28(iiid) concerning DEPB. In the instant case, the assessee, an exporter, sold DEPB and DFRC certificates. The core dispute revolved around whether the entire sale proceeds of these certificates or only the profit on their transfer (i.e., sale value minus face value) should be considered as business income under Section 28(iiid) for tax purposes. The Supreme Court meticulously analysed the DEPB scheme and the language of Section 28. T\n\nhe Supreme Court held that\n\nwhile the face value of the DEPB will fall under clause (iiib) of section 28, the difference between the sale value and the face value of the DEPB will fall under clause (iiid) of section 28\n\n. The Court explicitly stated that the High Court was incorrect in holding that the entire sale proceeds represented profit on transfer. This interpretation avoids double taxation, as the face value is taxed when it accrues (under Section 28(iiib)) and the profit on transfer is taxed when the transfer occurs (under Section 28(iiid)).\n\n3.1-2b.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, the words used are\n\nduty remission or any other export incentive\n\n. Even in the absence of an explicit mention that the profit arising on the transfer of DEPB/DFRC shall be taxable, it cannot be assumed that the whole amount of duty remission shall be taxed as profits and gains of business or profession, instead of taxing only the profit portion on the transfer of specified duty remission scheme(s).\n\nThe principle that one income cannot be taxed twice is a well-established tenet of Indian income tax law, primarily aimed at preventing the same income from being subjected to tax more than once in the hands of the same person under the same law. The judiciary has consistently upheld the general principle that income should not be taxed twice. This is not merely a matter of convenience but is rooted in the inherent fairness expected from fiscal statutes.\n\nIn the case of\n\nBarnes (Inspector of Taxes), Hutchinson 1939 7 ITR 124 (CA)\n\n, while interpreting the English Income-tax Act, 1918, the Court of Appeal recognised this fundamental principle. Clauson, LJ., stated that ...the Court recognises the principle that the Acts do not impose double taxation.... Brett, LJ., in Gilbertson v. Fergusson (as cited in Barnes (Inspector of Taxes), Hutchinson), articulated this strongly, observing that ...it would be so clearly unjust and obviously contrary to the meaning of the statute that the Government should have the tax payable twice over by the same person in respect of the same thing, that I should say it was a necessary implication that that could not be right. This highlights that even in the absence of specific statutory words, the principle against double taxation is a necessary implication of a just tax system. The principle is further emphasised in\n\nMutha Sarvarayudu v. CIT 1926 2 ITC 208 (Madras)\n\nand\n\nK.V.A.L.M. Ramanathan Chettiar, Commissioner of Income-tax 1973 88 ITR 169 (SC)\n\n.\n\nThus, where the gross amount of the DEPB/DFRC is already taxed on face value at the time of accrual, only the profit arising on its transfer should be taxed, which shall be the consideration received on transfer less the face value.\n\n3.1-3.\n\nSum received or receivable on non-compete business/professional agreement, whether chargeable under the head Profits and gains of business or profession or Capital gains Proviso (i) to Section 28(va) of the 1961 Act/Section 26(2)(h)(i)(A) of the 2025 Act\n\n3.1-3a.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder the 1961 Act, any sum, whether received or receivable in cash or in kind, on account of transfer of the right to manufacture, produce or process any article or thing or right to carry on any business or profession, which was chargeable under the head Capital gains, was not chargeable under the head Profits and gains of business or profession.\n\nThe 1961 Act generally taxes capital gains on an accrual basis, meaning the gain is taxable in the previous year in which the transfer of the capital asset takes place, as the right to receive consideration typically accrues at that point. This is reflected in Section 45(1) and the phrase consideration received or accruing in Section 48. However, the Act carves out significant exceptions where taxability is explicitly linked to the actual receipt of the sum or the occurrence of a specific event, rather than the date of transfer. For example, Section 45(5) for compulsory acquisition (taxable on receipt of compensation); Section 45(5A) for Joint Development Agreements (taxable on issuance of completion certificate); and Section 45(1B) for certain Unit Linked Insurance Policies (taxable on receipt of the amount). These exceptions are designed to align tax liability with the actual realisation by the taxpayer, providing relief or addressing practical challenges.\n\n3.1-3b.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, it is mentioned that a sum received on account of such transfer, which is chargeable under the head Capital gains, shall not be chargeable under the head Profits and gains of business or profession. In other words, the word receivable has been omitted from the 2025 Act.\n\nThis change does not seem to have any impact on the taxability of such a sum under the head of capital gains because the fundamental principle under Section 67(1) of the 2025 Act Corresponding to section 45 of the 1961 Act is that any profits or gains arising from the transfer of a capital asset effected in the tax year shall be chargeable to income-tax under the head Capital gains and shall be deemed to be the income of the previous year in which the transfer took place. Thus, the chargeable event for taxability is the transfer of the capital asset and not the receipt of the consideration for taxation of the capital gains, unless explicitly specified otherwise.\n\nFurther, Section 5 of the 2025 Act lays down the fundamental principles for determining the total income of a person, distinguishing between the receipt basis and the accrual basis of income recognition. This section sets the stage for how income, including capital gains, is brought to tax. For a resident, the total income of any previous year includes all income from whatever source derived, which:\n\nis received or is deemed to be received in India in such year; or\n\naccrues or arises or is deemed to accrue or arise to him in India during such year; or\n\naccrues or arises to him outside India during such year. This implies that a resident is taxable on their global income, either on a receipt basis or an accrual basis, whichever occurs earlier.\n\nIncome is considered received when the assessee gains control over the money. This refers to the first occasion when the amount comes into the hands of the assessee. It signifies actual physical or constructive receipt. Income accrues or arises when the assessee acquires a vested right to receive it, irrespective of whether the actual payment has been made or received. The right to receive the income crystallises, even if the actual realisation is deferred. The capital gains provision primarily operates on an\n\naccrual basis\n\n.\n\nThe taxability is linked to the event of transfer, which is when the right to receive the consideration for the asset typically arises, even if the full consideration is not actually received in that year.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 40(a)(ii):\nSection 35 (ITA 1961) v. Section 45/Section 66 (ITA 2025)\n\nExpenditure on scientific research\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 35\n\n: Expenditure on scientific research\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 45\n\n: Expenditure on scientific research\n\nSection 66\n\n: Interpretation\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| Provisions of 1961 Act | Corresponding provisions of 2025 Act |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 35(1) | Section 45(1)( a ) |\n| Section 35(1)( i ) & its Explanation | Section 45(1)( b ) & 45(11)( b ) |\n| Section 35(1)( ii ) | Section 45(3)( a )( i ) |\n| Section 35(1)( ii ), 1st Proviso | Section 45(4)( b ) |\n| Section 35(1)( ii ), 2nd Proviso | Section 45(3)( a )( i ) |\n| Section 35(1)( iia ) & its Proviso | Section 45(3)( b ) |\n| Section 35(1)( iii ) | Section 45(3)( a )( ii ) |\n| Section 35(1)( iii ), Proviso | Section 45(4)( b ) |\n| Explanation below Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nProcedural aspects relating to making an application by scientific research institutions shall be prescribed by rules\n\nCertain procedural aspects relating to making an application to the Central Government for grant of approval (or its continuance) under 1st proviso below section 35(1)(iv), or calling for such documents or information before granting approval under 2nd proviso below section 35(1)(iv), or preparing a statement, etc., under section 35(1A), or for satisfying about the feasibility of carrying out the scientific research under the 1st proviso to section 35(2AA), which were enumerated under the 1961 Act, shall now be prescribed under the rules made by the CBDT in view of section 45(4) of the 2025 Act.\n\nSimilar is the situation in case of section 35(2AB) procedures enumerated in the erstwhile sub-sections (3) and (4) under the 1961 Act. Those procedures shall not be prescribed under the rules made by the CBDT in view of sections 45(2)(i) and 45(2)(iv) of the 2025 Act.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\n111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000009187 direct tax laws 0 111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000009188 international taxation 0 111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000017592 transfer pricing 0 111050000000020048 comparative 000012092025 111050000000020049 income tax act 1961 000120920251 0000 Section 35 (ITA 1961) v. Section 45/Section 66 (ITA 2025)\n\nSection - 36:\nSection 2 (ITA 1961) v. Section 2 (ITA 2025)\n\nDefinitions\n\n1.
