109 ) |\n| 2( 47A ) | 2( 111 ) |\n| 2( 48 ) | 2( 112 ) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1\n\nMeaning of Assessment expanded\n\nSection 2(8) (ITA 1961) v. Section 2(13) (ITA 2025)\n\n3.1-1\n\nProvision of the ITA 1961\n\nSection 2(8) of the 1961 Act provides that assessment includes reassessment. This provision does not mention whether recomputation should be considered within the meaning of assessment. However, it is explicitly covered within Section 147 (reassessment process), Section 154 (Rectification of Mistake) and Section 155 (Amendment in Assessment Order).\n\nSection 154 allows an income-tax authority to amend any order passed by it to rectify any mistake apparent from the record. This is a simpler process for correcting obvious errors without resorting to a full assessment or reassessment. Section 155 provides for specific circumstances under which an assessment order can be amended or recomputed due to subsequent events or changes in facts or legal positions. This is distinct from a full reassessment under Section 147, as it addresses specific adjustments rather than a general escapement of income.\n\nThus, the 1961 Act uses the term assessment in various contexts, sometimes broadly to refer to the entire process of determining tax liability, and at other times more narrowly. This definition of assessment in Section 2(8) is inclusive, meaning it expands the scope of assessment to cover reassessment but does not explicitly mention recomputation. However, the courts have consistently interpreted the term assessment in a comprehensive manner that it encompasses all steps involved in determining the correct tax liability.\n\nThe Supreme Court, in the case of\n\nAuto & Metal Engineers, Union of India 1998 97 Taxman 363 (SC)\n\n, observed that the assessment proceeding is a broad term covering the entire journey from return filing to demand notice. The Karnataka High Court in the case of\n\nJoint CIT vs. Wipro Ltd. 2021 130 taxmann.com 84 (Karnataka)\n\n, extensively discussed the terms assessment, reassessment, and recomputation in the context of Section 153 (time limits). The Court noted that Section 153(1) uses assessment to mean the entire process, while Section 153(2) uses assessment, reassessment or recomputation, and Section 153(3) uses fresh assessment. The Court clarified that the word assessment would mean the whole process of determination of income, and the same should not be restricted to a mere passing of an assessment order.\n\nThis reinforces that the term assessment in its broader sense encompasses the entire process of determining the correct income and tax liability, which would also include recomputation. This is further supported by the legislative language in Section 153, where recomputation is often used alongside assessment and reassessment to define the scope of proceedings and time limits. Therefore, for most procedural and substantive purposes under the Act, recomputation can be considered an integral part of the assessment process, even if not explicitly listed in the inclusive definition of Section 2(8).\n\n3.1-2\n\nAnalysis of change in the ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nSection 2(13) of the 2025 Act\n\nprovides a statutory definition of the assessment that includes reassessment and recomputation. The definition in the new Act enhances\n\nclarity and removes ambiguity by explicitly covering the term recomputation within the broader ambit of the expression assessment.\n\n3.2\n\nAssistant Director shall also mean Deputy Director\n\nSection 2(9B) (ITA 1961) v. Section 2(15) (ITA 2025)\n\nUnder Section 2(9B) of the 1961 Act, Assistant Director did not mean a person appointed to be a Deputy Director of Income-tax. But the definition of the expression Assistant Commissioner also means a person appointed to be a Deputy Commissioner of Income-tax, both under Section 2(9A) of the 1961 Act as well as Section 2(14) of the 2025 Act. Therefore, to maintain consistency, the definition of the expression Assistant Director has been modified in Section 2(15) of the 2025 Act to also mean a person appointed to be a Deputy Director of Income-tax.\n\n3.3\n\nBooks of account to cover records maintained on cloud-based storage\n\nSection 2(12A) (ITA 1961) v. Section 2(19) (ITA 2025)\n\n3.3-1\n\nProvision of the ITA 1961\n\nThe 1961 Act provides a comprehensive but inclusive definition of books or books of account under Section 2(12A). It explicitly provides that these books, whether ledgers, day-books, cash books, account-books, or other books, can be kept in written, electronic or digital form. It also covers the print-outs of data stored in such electronic or digital form.\n\nWhen Section 2(12A) was first inserted\n\n1\n\n, it defined books or books of account to include ledgers, day-books, cash books, account-books, and other books, whether kept in the written form or as print-outs of data stored in a floppy, disc, tape or any other form of electro-magnetic data storage device. Recognising the rapid advancements in technology and the widespread adoption of digital record-keeping, the Finance Act 2022 introduced a pivotal amendment to Section 2(12A). The phrase in the written form or as print-outs of data stored in was substituted with in the written form or in electronic form or in digital form or as print-outs of data stored in such electronic form or in digital form or in. This amendment explicitly included electronic form and digital form as valid modes of maintaining books of account. This was a deliberate legislative action to update the definition and ensure it remains relevant in the digital age.\n\nThe existing definition, although updated to include electronic and digital forms, may encounter challenges with newer, more advanced forms of data storage or distributed ledger technologies. However, the phrase any other form of electro-magnetic data storage device is designed to be forward-looking and generally covers future technological advancements in data storage. Cloud servers, despite being remote and accessed over a network, function as sophisticated electro-magnetic data storage devices. The legislative intent behind such a broad phrasing is to future-proof the definition, ensuring it encompasses various evolving methods of electronic data storage without needing constant amendments for every new technology.\n\n3.3-2\n\nAnalysis of change in the ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nThe 2025 Act has explicitly provided that keeping of books or books of account on any cloud-based storage will also come within the ambit of the definition of books of account. With respect to this insertion, the Select Committee also noted that the provision has been suitably modernised to encompass contemporary methods of data storage while retaining the essence of the original section. Specifically, the inclusion of records maintained in any digital form, through cloud-based storage, and on various portable data storage devices represents a timely and essential update to the law. The Committee find the Ministrys rationale for this update to remove ambiguity surrounding modern technology to be sound.\n\n3.4\n\nDefinition of Charitable Purpose\n\nSection 2(15) (ITA 1961) v. Section 2(23)/Section 346/Section 355 (ITA 2025)\n\n3.4-1\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nThe definition of\n\ncharitable purpose\n\nprovided in Section 2(15) of the 1961 Act provides as follows:\n\nCharitable Purpose includes:\n\n(a)\n\nrelief of the poor;\n\n(b)\n\neducation;\n\n(c)\n\nyoga;\n\n(d)\n\nmedical relief;\n\n(e)\n\npreservation of the environment (including watersheds, forests and wildlife);\n\n(f)\n\npreservation of monuments or places or objects of artistic or historic interest;\n\n(g)\n\nthe advancement of any other object of general public utility.\n\nProvided\n\nthat the advancement of any other object of general public utility shall not be a charitable purpose, if it involves the carrying on of any activity in the nature of trade, commerce or business, or any activity of rendering any service in relation to any trade, commerce or business, for a cess or fee or any other consideration, irrespective of the nature of use or application, or retention, of the income from such activity, unless-\n\n(i) such activity is undertaken in the course of actual carrying out of such advancement of any other object of general public utility; and\n\n(ii) the aggregate receipts from such activity or activities during the previous year, do not exceed twenty per cent of the total receipts, of the trust or institution undertaking such activity or activities, of that previous year.\n\nThe proviso in Section 2(15)\n\nof the 1961 Act restricts\n\nthe NPOs\n\nengaged in the\n\nadvancement of any other object of general public utility (GPU)\n\nfrom engaging in\n\ntrade, commerce, or business activities\n\n. It provided that the advancement of any other object of a general public utility shall not be a charitable purpose if it involves the carrying any activity in the nature of trade, commerce or business or any activity of rendering any service in relation to any trade, commerce or business, for a cess or fee or any other consideration, irrespective of the nature of use or application, or retention, of the income from such activity. It may be noted that if the receipts from such activity do not exceed 20% of the total receipts in that year, then the institution would not lose its charitable status even if its main object is advancement of any other object of general public utility if such activity is undertaken in the course of actual carrying of such advancement of any other object of general public utility.\n\n3.4-2\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 Act vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nThe\n\nnew definition under Section 2(23)\n\nomits this restrictive proviso\n\n. Instead, Section 346 of the 2025 Act incorporates a\n\nsimilar restriction on commercial activities\n\nby\n\nregistered NPOs engaged in GPU\n\n. The revised definition clarifies the\n\nconcept of a charitable purpose\n\nwithout imposing any limitations. The\n\nrestrictions on commercial activities remain unchanged but\n\nare addressed\n\nunder Section 346\n\n, effectively distinguishing\n\nthe definition of a charitable purpose\n\nfrom eligibility for tax exemption. NPOs engaged in GPU activities must now comply with\n\nSection 346\n\nto adhere to the restrictions on commercial operations while maintaining their\n\ncharitable status\n\n.\n\nThe other notable change in the provision is changing the reference from trust to non-profit organisation for the voluntary contributions (including anonymous donations) received by such trusts. The 2025 Act introduces a comprehensive new taxation framework for non-profit organisations (NPOs), replacing the existing provisions applicable to trusts. The new regime adopts the term Non-Profit Organisation (NPO) instead of trust, which was previously used\n\n.\n\nClick here for more details on the changes in the provisions for the taxability of charitable trusts or registered NPOs\n\n3.5\n\nA co-operative society registered under the Multi-State Co-operative Societies Act, 2002, is included within the definition of a co-operative society Section 2(32) of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 2(19) of the ITA 1961)\n\n3.5-1\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nSection 2(19) of the ITA 1961 defines a co-operative society to mean a co-operative society registered under the Co-operative Societies Act, 1912, or under any other law for the time being in force in any State for the registration of co-operative societies.\n\n3.5-2\n\nPosition under the ITA 2025\n\nSection 2(32) of the ITA, 2025 expands the definition and covers more co-operative societies. A co-operative society registered under the following laws will be covered under the definition of co-operative society:\n\n(a)\n\nCo-operative Societies Act, 1912 (same as in ITA 1961)\n\n(b)\n\nMulti-State Co-operative Societies Act, 2002 (included by the Finance Act 2026)\n\n(c)\n\nAny other law for the time being in force in any State for the registration of co-operative societies (same as in ITA 1961)\n\n(d)\n\nAny law for the time being in force in a Union Territory governing the registration of co-operative societies (included in the ITA 2025)\n\nFAQs issued by the Income Tax Department on Budget 2026-27 clarified that any multi-state co-operative society registered under the Multi-unit Co-operative Societies Act, 1942, or the Multi-State Co-operative Societies Act, 1984, is deemed to be registered under the relevant provisions of the Multi-State Co-operative Societies Act, 2002.\n\nThese amendments will take effect from 01-04-2026.\n\n3.6\n\nDefinition of Income to include all import and export incentives\n\nSection 2(24) (ITA 1961) v. Section 2(49) (ITA 2025)\n\n3.6-1\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nThe concept of income is fundamental to the taxability under the Income-tax Act, as it is the basis upon which tax is levied. Section 2(24) of the 1961 Act provides an inclusive definition of income, meaning it lists various specific receipts and benefits that are considered income, but it does not restrict the scope only to these items. Some of the receipts which are explicitly included within the meaning of income include the following:\n\n(a)\n\nProfits on the sale of licenses granted under the Import (Control) Order 1955\n\nas referred to in Section 28(iiia)\n\n.\n\n(b)\n\nCash assistance received or receivable against exports under any Government of India scheme\n\nas referred to in Section 28(iiib)\n\n.\n\n(c)\n\nAny customs or excise duty repaid or repayable as a drawback against exports\n\nas referred to in Section 28(iiic)\n\n.\n\nThe other similar receipts arising under the duty remission schemes, as referred to in Section 28(iiid) and Section 28(iiie), are not explicitly covered within the meaning of income under Section 2(24). However, the courts have held that such receipts will be taxable as they are revenue receipts and covered within the meaning of income.\n\nThe Supreme Court, in the case of\n\nCIT v. G.R. Karthikeyan 1993 201 ITR 866 (SC)\n\n, provided a foundational understanding of the term income under the Act. The Court observed that ...the expression income as defined in the statute is inclusive and not exclusive. The Court emphasised that ...anything which can properly be described as income, is taxable under the Act unless expressly exempted.\n\nThis broad interpretation means that even if a specific receipt is not explicitly listed in the sub-clauses of Section 2(24), it can still be considered income if it partakes of the nature of income. The idea behind providing an inclusive definition in section 2(24) is not to limit its meaning but to widen its net. This Court has repeatedly said that the word income is of the widest amplitude, and that it must be given its natural and grammatical meaning..\n\nThe ITAT Mumbai Special Bench, in\n\nITO v. Topman Exports 2010 124 ITD 1 (Mumbai) (SB)\n\n, while addressing the non-inclusion of Section 28(iiid) and (iiie) in Section 2(24), reiterated this principle. It held that ...sub-section (24) of section 2 defines income in an inclusive manner and not exhaustively. This definition includes various items as mentioned in clauses (i) to (xiv)\n\n2\n\n. If an item of receipt is/or contains the element of income in common parlance, that would still be included within income under this provision, notwithstanding the fact that there is no specific inclusion of such item of income in the definition. The word income has to be understood in the generic sense.. The Tribunal concluded that ...since profit on transfer of the DEPB as well as the DEPB entitlement itself is in the nature of income, the same is required to be included within the general meaning of income, even if it is not specifically enshrined in section 2 (24)..\n\nAlso see the Supreme Court judgment in the case of\n\nCIT vs. Topman Exports 2012 342 ITR 49 (SC)\n\n, wherein the court clarified the distinction between the face value of DEPB and the profit on its transfer for taxability under Section 28.\n\n3.6-2\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnlike, giving a reference to each clause of Section 28 clauses (iiia), (iiib) and (iiic) of Section 28 in Section 2(24), its corresponding clause (i)(B) of Section 2(49) of the 2025 Act provides that any sum chargeable to income-tax under Section 26(2)(e) shall be covered within the scope of the income.\n\nSection 26(2)(e) of the 2025 Act brings all kinds of import-export incentives within the ambit of the charging provision for taxability under the head of business income. The new provision refrained from specifying the name of the scheme under which the incentives or benefits are received for taxability. Instead, it provides that\n\nthe amount of any profit on sale of import licence, cash assistance against export, duty drawback or duty remission or any other export incentive, received or receivable\n\nshall be covered within the scope of business or profession income. Thus, all export benefit schemes, whether in the form of remission, refund, incentive, or profit on the sale of a license, shall be considered income and taxable as business income. Here is an illustrative list of\n\nGovernment Export Benefit Schemes:\n\n(a)\n\nDuty Remission and Refund Schemes\n\nRoDTEP (Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products)\n\nDuty Drawback Scheme\n\nRoSCT (Rebate of State and Central Taxes and Levies)\n\n(b)\n\nDuty-Free Import Schemes\n\nAdvance Authorization Scheme\n\nDuty-Free Import Authorization (DFIA)\n\n(c)\n\nCapital Goods Schemes\n\nExport Promotion Capital Goods (EPCG) Scheme\n\nPost Export EPCG\n\n(d)\n\nCredit and Financial Schemes\n\nNIRVIK Scheme\n\nECGC Export Credit Insurance\n\n(e)\n\nSpecial Economic Zone and Unit Benefits\n\nSpecial Economic Zones (SEZ) Benefits\n\nExport Oriented Units (EOU) Benefits\n\nDeemed Exports\n\n(f)\n\nSector-Specific Benefits\n\nTowns of Export Excellence (TEE)\n\nFocus Product and Market Schemes\n\n3.7\n\nRelative of an individual to cover both Maternal and Paternal Lineal Ascendants\n\nSection 2(41) (ITA 1961) v. Section 2(94) (ITA 2025)\n\n3.7-1\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nRelative, as defined in Section 2(41) of the 1961 Act in relation to an individual, means the husband, wife, brother or sister or any lineal ascendant or descendant of that individual.\n\nThe definition of the word relative for the purpose of\n\nsection 56(2)(x)\n\nof the 1961 Act is as follows:\n\n(a)\n\nspouse of the individual\n\n(b)\n\nbrother or sister of the individual;\n\n(c)\n\nbrother or sister of the spouse of the individual;\n\n(d)\n\nbrother or sister of either of the parents of the individual;\n\n(e)\n\nany lineal ascendant or descendant of the individual;\n\n(f)\n\nany lineal ascendant or descendant of the spouse of the individual;\n\n(g)\n\nspouse of the person referred to in items (b) to (f).\n\nBoth these provisions are silent on whether the lineal ascendant and descendant of that individual should be from the paternal side, the maternal side, or both.\n\nSection 2(41) of the 1961 Act defines the meaning of the term relative, which applies to those provisions that do not provide another definition of this term.\n\nVarious\n\njudicial precedents support the interpretation that Section 2(41) serves as the default definition of relative when a specific section does not provide its own.\n\nThis is clearly demonstrated in the context of Section 40A(2)(b), where courts\n\n3\n\nhave consistently held that the narrower definition of Section 2(41) applies. Conversely, for provisions like Section 56(2)(x) concerning gifts, the courts have affirmed that the specific and often broader definition provided within that provision takes precedence, and the general definition of Section 2(41) is not applicable. Some examples of the provisions where the general definition of relative is considered are Section 2(24)(iv), Section 40A and Section 92A of the 1961 Act. There are provisions which define the meaning of relative which may be broader or narrower than the one defined in Section 2(41), i.e., Section 56, Section 80E, Section 13, etc.\n\nThe Rajasthan High Court in\n\nthe case of\n\nCIT\n\nv. Dhannalal Devilal 1956 29 ITR 165 (Rajasthan)\n\nheld that w\n\nhen the law speaks of lineal descendant, the intention is that a person must be descended in a right line without any deviation from father to son, grandson, great\n\n-\n\ngrandson and so on.\n\nSimilarly, the descent is lineal if it goes from mother to daughter, and grand-daughter, and great\n\n-\n\ngrand-daughter, because here also it is in a right line without any deviation.\n\nIt concluded that\n\na son will be a lineal descendant of the mother as well as of his grandmother\n\n,\n\nirrespective of whether the mother or the grandmother can form a line of succession under the Hindu law.\n\nThis interpretation clearly establishes that the term lineal descendant (and by extension, lineal ascendant) is not confined to the paternal line but includes the maternal line as well, as long as the descent or ascent is in a direct line. The courts have consistently held that the natural meaning of lineal descendant should be applied, without importing specific notions of Hindu law regarding succession that might restrict it to the male line.\n\nThis means that for the purposes of Section 2(41) and Section 56(2)(x), lineal ascendant or descendant would encompass individuals in a direct line, whether through the paternal or maternal side. For example, a mother, grandmother, or great-grandmother would be a lineal ascendant, and a daughter, granddaughter, or great-granddaughter would be a lineal descendant.\n\n3.7-2\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nSection 2(94) of the 2025 Act clarifies that relative, in relation to an individual, means the husband, wife, brother, sister or any lineal ascendant (maternal as well as paternal) or descendant of that individual. This explicitly clarifies that a lineal ascendant of an individual may be maternal or paternal. A similar amendment is made to Section 92(5) of the 2025 Act corresponding to Section 56(2)(x) of the 1961 Act to provide that any lineal ascendant of an individual and his spouse shall mean maternal as well as paternal lineal ascendant. However, the law is silent on which side the lineal descendant of an individual should be considered as a relative.\n\nKey judicial pronouncements, such as\n\nCIT\n\nv. Dhannalal Devilal\n\n(supra.)\n\nand\n\nByomkesh Chakravarty v. C\n\nIT\n\n1960 39 ITR 303 (Patna)\n\n, have firmly established that lineal descent is about a straight, unbroken line, irrespective of gender or traditional Hindu law notions of succession. Furthermore, an adopted child is also considered a lineal descendant, as affirmed in\n\nITAT\n\nv. Estate of Late Muli Lakshminarayana 1979 1 Taxman 133 (Andhra Pradesh)\n\n, due to their complete legal integration into the adoptive family. Thus, the term lineal descendant comprehensively encompasses individuals in both the paternal and maternal lines. This means that direct descendants, whether through the fathers side or the mothers side (e.g., children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren), are considered lineal descendants for the purpose of defining relative.\n\nAn illustrative list of relatives covered under Section 2(94) of the 2025 Act corresponding to Section 2(41) of the 1961 Act and for Section 91 of the 2025 Act corresponding to Section 56 of the 1961 Act is given in the table below.\n\n| Relatives | Whether relative for Section 2(94) of the 2025 Act | Whether relative for Section 91 of the 2025 Act |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Husband/Wife | Yes | Yes |\n| Son/Daughter (Including Step child and Adopted child) | Yes | Yes |\n| Son-in-Law/Daughter-in-Law | - | Yes |\n| Father/Mother or Step-father/mother | Yes | Yes |\n| Mother-In-Law or Father-In-Law | - | Yes |\n| Brother/Sister or Half-brother/Sister (and their wife/husband) | Yes | Yes |\n| Brother-in-Law (and his wife) or Sister-in-law (and her husband) | - | Yes |\n| Grandfather or Grandmother | Yes | Yes |\n| Spouse’s Grandfather or Spouse’s Grandmother | - | Yes |\n| Grandson or Granddaughter | Yes | Yes |\n| Great Grandson or Great Granddaughter | Yes | Yes |\n| Spouse of Grand-Child or Great-Grand-Child | - | Yes |\n| Great Grandfather or Great Grandmother | Yes | Yes |\n| Spouse’s Great Grandfather or Spouse’s Great Grandmother | - | Yes |\n| Father’s Brother (and his wife) or Father’s Sister (and her husband) | - | Yes |\n| Mother’s Brother (and his wife) or Sister (and her husband) | - | Yes |\n| Step-brother/sister | - | - |\n| Nephew/Niece | - | - |\n| Cousins | - | - |\n\n3.8\n\nBuyback of shares will be taxable as capital gains Section 69 of ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 46A of ITA 1961)\n\nThe Finance Act, 2026 has made several amendments to the provisions of the ITA 2025 regarding the taxation of buy-back of shares. Under the amended provisions, the sum paid by a domestic company to the shareholders under a buyback shall be treated as capital gains in the hands of the shareholders.\n\n3.8-1\n\nIntroduction\n\nCorporate action to buyback shares can serve multiple strategic purposes. Primarily, its used to increase earnings per share by reducing the number of outstanding shares, often leading to a higher stock price. Additionally, buybacks are a method to return excess cash to shareholders, an alternative to dividend payouts. Companies might also repurchase shares if they believe their stock is undervalued, signalling confidence in the future. Moreover, buybacks can deter hostile takeovers by increasing the companys ownership percentage.\n\nSection 68 of the Companies Act, 2013 gives the company the power to purchase its own securities. It defines a buyback as the purchase of its own shares or other specified securities (i.e., Employee Stock Options or Sweat Equity) by the Company itself.\n\n3.8-2\n\nHistory of tax on buyback of shares as per the ITA 1961\n\nBefore the assessment year 2000-01, the Income Tax Act did not include a special provision for taxing share buybacks. There were two views on the buyback of shares: either it could attract dividend distribution tax, or shareholders would be liable to pay tax on any gains.\n\nTo set the controversy at rest, the Finance Act, 1999 inserted Section 46A with effect from assessment year 2000-01, which stated that shareholders would be charged to tax on the capital gains arising from the buyback of shares. A corresponding amendment was also made to Section 2(22) by inserting clause (iv), which provides that a payment for the purchase of shares does not constitute a dividend. Therefore, the consideration received by a shareholder on the buyback of shares by the company in accordance with Section 77A of the Companies Act, 1956\n\n4\n\n(corresponding to Section 68 of the Companies Act, 2013) was not treated as a dividend. The same was taxed under Section 46A of the Act as capital gains.\n\nSubsequently, the unlisted companies, as part of the tax avoidance scheme, were found resorting to buyback of shares instead of payment of dividends in order to avoid payment of tax by way of DDT, particularly where the capital gains arising to the shareholders were either not chargeable to tax or were taxable at a lower rate under respective Double Taxation Avoidance Agreements. In\n\nA Mauritius, In re 2012 20 taxmann.com 52\n\n, the AAR held that only a genuine buyback is excluded from the definition of dividend under Section 2(22)(iv) and exempt from DDT under Section 115-O. A colourable buyback will attract DDT. In that case, the buyback was held to be a colourable device for tax avoidance, as dividends were distributed in the guise of a buyback to avoid DDT.\n\nTaking a cue from the above decision of the AAR, the Finance Act 2013 inserted a new Chapter XII-DA, Special Provisions relating to tax on distributed income of domestic company for buyback of shares, consisting of Sections 115QA to 115QC, with effect from 1-6-2013. The Finance (No. 2) Act 2019 extended the levy of tax on the buy-back of shares listed on a recognised stock exchange on or after 5 July 2019 by amending Section 115QA.\n\nIn November 2022, the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) released a consultation paper on a review of the SEBI (Buyback of Securities) Regulations, 2018, proposing various changes to existing buyback norms. One of the proposed changes is to shift the tax burden of buybacks from the company to the shareholders. This proposal was based on the fact that promoters tendered more shares than their pre-buyback holdings, resulting in the company paying taxes not only on shares tendered by the promoters but also on the additional shares tendered by them. These taxes were paid from the companys reserves on behalf of the existing shareholders and promoters at the expense of continuing shareholders. Therefore, it was suggested that the tax burden on buybacks should be shifted to shareholders, as it is with dividend distributions.\n\nIt is, therefore, with effect from 01-10-2024, the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2024 introduced a clause (f) in Section 2(22) to provide that the sum paid by a domestic company to the shareholders under a buyback shall be treated as a dividend in the hands of the shareholders. Since the buyback involves a transfer of shares and the consideration paid by the company has already been taxed as a dividend, for the purpose of computing capital gains under Section 46A, the full value of consideration is deemed to be nil. This results in a capital loss equal to the cost of acquisition of the shares.\n\nThe chronology of the taxation of the buyback of shares is as follows:\n\n(a)\n\nUntil 31\n\nst\n\nMarch 1999:\n\nNo provision to tax the buyback of shares\n\n(b)\n\nFrom 1\n\nst\n\nApril 1999 to 31\n\nst\n\nMay 2013:\n\nThe buyback of shares by a company was taxed as capital gains under Section 46A.\n\n(c)\n\nFrom 1\n\nst\n\nJune 2013 to 4\n\nth\n\nJuly 2019:\n\nThe buyback of shares by a listed domestic company was taxed as capital gains under Section 46A, and the buyback of shares by an unlisted domestic company was subject to levy under Section 115QA.\n\n(d)\n\nFrom 5\n\nth\n\nJuly 2019 to 30\n\nth\n\nSeptember 2024:\n\nThe buyback of shares by a listed and unlisted domestic company was subject to levy under Section 115QA.\n\n(e)\n\nFrom 1\n\nst\n\nOctober 2024 to 31\n\nst\n\nMarch 2026:\n\nThe buyback of shares by a listed and unlisted domestic company was treated as a deemed dividend under Section 2(22)(f).\n\n(f)\n\nFrom 1\n\nst\n\nApril 2026:\n\nThe buyback of shares by a company is taxed as capital gains under Section 69 of ITA 2025 corresponding to Section 46A of ITA 1961.\n\n3.8-3\n\nAmendment by the Finance Act 2026 in the ITA 2025\n\nThe Finance Act, 2026, substituted sub-sections (2) and (3) of Section 69 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 46A of the ITA 1961) to bring back the taxation of buybacks of securities under the head of capital gains. The substituted provision reads as follows:\n\nCapital gains on purchase by company of its own shares or other specified securities.\n\n69. (1) If a shareholder or a holder of other specified securities receives any consideration from any company for the purchase of its own shares or other specified securities held by such shareholder or holder of other specified securities, then, subject to the provisions of section 72, the difference between the cost of acquisition and the value of consideration so received shall be deemed to be the Capital gains arising to such shareholder or the holder of other specified securities, as the case may be, in the year in which the company purchases the shares or other specified securities.\n\n(2) In respect of capital gains referred to in sub-section (1), where a company purchases its own shares or other specified securities in accordance with the provisions of section 68 of the Companies Act, 2013 and the\n\nshareholder or holder of other specified securities is a promoter, the\n\naggregate income-tax payable on such capital gains shall be\n\n(a)\n\nthe income-tax payable on such capital gains in accordance with the provisions of this Act; and\n\n(b)\n\nan additional income tax in respect of capital gains specified in column B of the Table below, computed at the rate specified in column C or column D of the said Table.\n\n| Sl. No. | Income | Rate, where the promoter is a domestic company | Rate, where the promoter is other than a domestic company |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| A | B | C | D |\n| 1. | Short-term capital gains referred to in section 196 arising from the transfer of such securities. | 2% | 10% |\n| 2. | Long-term capital gains referred to in section 197 or section 198 arising from the transfer of such securities. | 9.5% | 17.5% |\n\n(3) For the purposes of this section,\n\n(a)\n\nin the case of a company whose shares are listed on a recognised stock exchange in India, promoter shall have the same meaning as assigned to it in regulation 2(k) of the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Buy-Back of Securities) Regulations, 2018 made under the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992;\n\n(b)\n\nin any other case, promoter means,\n\n(i)\n\na promoter as defined in section 2(69) of the Companies Act, 2013; or\n\n(ii)\n\na person who holds, directly or indirectly, more than 10% of the shareholding in the company;\n\n(c)\n\nspecified securities shall have the same meaning as assigned to it in Explanation 1 to section 68 of the Companies Act, 2013.\n\nThe italicsed text has been substituted by the Finance Act, 2026 with effect from 01-04-2026.\n\nThe ingredients of the new provisions are as follows:\n\n(a)\n\nThe buy-back of specified securities will be taxable as capital gains\n\nsee Para 3.8-4\n\n(b)\n\nAdditional tax will be payable by the promoters who tender shares in the buyback\n\nsee Para 3.8-5\n\n3.8-4\n\nTaxable as capital gains\n\nSection 69 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 46A of the ITA 1961) is a charging provision that provides the taxable event and the mechanism for computing the capital gains due to consideration received by a shareholder from any company (domestic or foreign company) for the buyback of shares and specified securities (employees stock options). The calculation of capital gains under this provision shall be done as per Section 72 (corresponding to Section 48 of the ITA 1961), which provides as follows:\n\n| Particulars | Amount |\n| --- | --- |\n| Full value of consideration Less: (a) Cost of acquisition (as per FIFO method) (b) Cost of improvement (c) Expenditure in connection with transfer (d) Capital gains taxable under Section 67(10) [5] , which is attributable to the capital asset remaining with the firm, AOP or BOI after reconstitution (e) Exemption under Section 86 [6] | *** (***) (***) (***) (***) (***) |\n| Capital gains/Capital loss | *** |\n\nFor the taxability under this provision, the trigger points will be:\n\n(a)\n\nThere is a buyback\n\nsee Para 3.8-4a\n\n.\n\n(b)\n\nSuch a buyback is of shares or specified securities\n\nsee Para 3.8-4b\n\n.\n\n(c)\n\nSuch a buyback is done by a company\n\nsee Para 3.8-4c\n\n.\n\n(d)\n\nConsideration is received by a shareholder or holder of securities\n\nsee Para 3.8-4d\n\n.\n\n3.8-4a\n\nMeaning of the buyback\n\nThe provision does not specify any condition under which the company should buy back shares. Thus, a buyback by a domestic company under Sections 68, 230 to 232, or 242 of the Companies Act, 2013, or by a foreign company, will be taxable under this provision.\n\nHowever, any reduction of capital by the company under Section 66(1)(b) of the Companies Act, 2013, could not be equated to share buyback\n\n7\n\nand thus will not be taxable under this provision. Therefore, any distribution to its shareholders by a company on reduction of its capital to the extent to which the company possesses accumulated profits is deemed to be a dividend under Section 2(40)(d) corresponding to Section 2(22)(d) of the ITA 1961.\n\n3.8-4b\n\nBuyback of shares or securities\n\nTo trigger liability under this provision, the buyback by the company should be of its own shares or specified securities.\n\nAs per Section 2(84) of the Companies Act, 2013, share means a share in the share capital of a company and includes stock. Thus, the shares can be listed or unlisted, equity or preference shares, and their buyback will be taxable under this provision. Even redemption of preference shares will be taxable under this provision\n\n8\n\n.\n\nThe specified securities shall mean as defined in\n\nExplanation I\n\nto Section 68 of the Companies Act, 2013, which includes employees stock option or other securities as may be notified by the Central Government from time to time.\n\n3.8-4c\n\nApplicable to a company\n\nUnder the provisions, the taxability arises from payments made by a Company, as defined in Section 2(28) of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 2(17) of the ITA 1961). Based on the definition of the company under Section 2(28), it could be said that the capital gains will arise on the buyback of shares by the following entities:\n\n(a)\n\nA company (private or public) incorporated and registered in India;\n\n(b)\n\nA company incorporated and registered outside India (foreign company);\n\n(c)\n\nA corporation established by or under a Central, State or Provincial Act;\n\n(d)\n\nAny institution, association or body declared by CBDT to be a company under Section 2(17);\n\n(e)\n\nAny other entity declared by the Board to be a company, as assessed as a company under the Act or the Indian Income-tax Act. 1922.\n\n3.8-4d\n\nTaxability at the time of the payment of consideration\n\nThere are three stages in the buyback of shares:\n\n(a)\n\nPre-buyback activities\n\n(b)\n\nBuyback activities\n\n(c)\n\nPost buyback activities\n\nThe pre-buyback activities include passing the resolution, announcing the offer, filing the letter of offer and solvency, dispatching the letter of offer, etc. In buyback activities, the company verifies and accepts the offer within 15 days of the offers closure date and pays consideration to the shareholders. The post-buyback activities include extinguishing the shares bought back, filing Form SH-11 with the ROC, and maintaining a register of buybacks in Form SH-10.\n\nNo taxable event shall arise during the pre-buyback activities. It should happen when the company finishes the buyback activities, which include verification, acceptance of the shareholders offer to sell shares for buyback, and payment to the shareholders.\n\nSo, if buyback activities are completed before 01-04-2026, Section 2(22)(f) of the ITA 1961 should be attracted. In the case of a buyback by a foreign company, the liability to pay tax will arise under Section 46A. Where all buyback activities are completed on or before 31-03-2026, but payment is made to the shareholder on or after 01-04-2026, the capital gains should arise and be taxable under Section 69 of the ITA 2025. In this situation, the deemed dividend under Section 2(22)(f) will not arise because the taxable event under this provision is the\n\npayment\n\nby a company on purchase of its own shares from a shareholder. In the context of dividends, they should be considered paid when the company discharges its liability and makes the amount unconditionally available to the member entitled thereto\n\n9\n\n.\n\nThe dictionary meaning of payment is the performance of an obligation for the delivery of money. In legal contemplation, payment is the discharge of an obligation by the delivery of money or its equivalent\n\nState of Haryana v. Maruti Udyog Ltd., (2000) 7 SCC 348\n\n.\n\nBlacks Law Dictionary (Seventh Ed.) defines payment as the performance of an\n\nobligation by the delivery of money. Performance may occur through the delivery and acceptance of goods other than money, but payment occurs only if money or other valuable consideration is given and accepted in partial or full discharge of an obligation. According to P. Ramanatha Aiyers The Law Lexicon (Reprint 2002 - General Editor: Honble Justice Y.V. Chandrachud, p.1426), payment, in legal contemplation, is the discharge of an obligation by the delivery of money or its equivalent, and is generally made with the assent of both parties to the contract. Thus, in order to constitute payment, there should be an obligation to make the payment, and that obligation should then be discharged by payment of money or other valuable things. Further, the payment so made should be accepted by the payee in satisfaction of the obligation\n\nSantosh Kumar Kejriwal v. ACIT, (2004) 89 ITD 172 (Kol).\n\nWhere a negotiable instrument (bill, cheque or promissory note) is given for payment, the payment is, at the first instance, conditional. Only when the cheque or bill is honoured does the creditor benefit from it and consider it paid. If they are dishonoured, then the original loan is revived\n\nGorilal Baldeodas v. Ramjeelal Bhuralal, AIR 1961 MP 346\n\n. Handing over the cheque constitutes payment unless it is dishonoured. The payment takes effect from the delivery of the cheque but is defeated by non-payment at maturity\n\nCIT v. Ogale Glass Works Ltd. 1954 25 ITR 529 (SC)\n\n. However, payments by post-dated bearer cheques are not considered to be made on the date the cheque was issued or given. The payment of the money mentioned therein would be considered made on the date on which the cheque matured and was encashed\n\nM.A. Nek Mohd. & Sons v. CIT, (1982) 10 Taxman 298 (All)\n\n.\n\nThe buyback should be considered on the date the company discharges its obligation. Where the buyback activities are completed on or before 31-03-2026, but the payment is made on or after 01-04-2026, the capital gains should arise under Section 69.\n\n3.8-5\n\nTax on the capital gains\n\nFor computing capital gains and the tax thereon, capital assets are categorised as short-term or long-term. This distinction is crucial because short-term capital gains are taxed at a higher rate than long-term capital gains. The distinction between a long-term and a short-term capital asset is based on the period the owner holds it before transfer. Usually, the period of holding of a capital asset is reckoned from the date of its purchase. However, in certain special cases, the holding period is determined by the specific provisions.\n\n3.8-5a\n\nPeriod of holding\n\nThe listed shares or securities will be considered long-term if held for more than 12 months, and the unlisted shares or securities will be considered long-term if held for more than 24 months.\n\n3.8-5b\n\nTax rates on non-promoters\n\nIf the shareholder or security holder tendering shares or securities in the buy-back scheme is not a promoter, the tax rates on the long-term or short-term capital gains will be as follows:\n\n| Product | Tax on short-term capital gain | Tax on short-term capital gain | Tax on long-term capital gain | Tax on long-term capital gain | Tax on long-term capital gain |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Product | In case of FPIs or Specified fund | Others | In case of resident | In case of non-resident | In case of FPIs or Specified fund |\n| Listed equity shares (STT Paid) | 20% | 20% | 12.5% on capital gain exceeding Rs. 1,25,000 | 12.5% on capital gain exceeding Rs. 1,25,000 | 12.5% on capital gain exceeding Rs. 1,25,000 |\n| Listed equity shares (STT not paid) | 30% | Normal tax rate | 12.5% without indexation | 12.5% without indexation | 12.5% without indexation and forex fluctuation |\n| Unlisted equity shares | 30% | Normal tax rate | 12.5% without indexation | 12.5% without indexation and forex fluctuation | 12.5% without indexation and forex fluctuation |\n| Listed Preference shares | 30% | Normal tax rate | 12.5% without indexation | 12.5% without indexation | 12.5% without indexation and forex fluctuation |\n| Unlisted Preference shares | 30% | Normal tax rate | 12.5% without indexation | 12.5% without indexation and forex fluctuation | 12.5% without indexation and forex fluctuation |\n\n3.8-5c\n\nTax rates on promoters\n\nWhen promoters tender their shares in the buyback scheme, they will be liable to pay the following additional tax:\n\n| Capital gains arising from securities | Rate, where the promoter is a domestic company | Rate, where the promoter is other than a domestic company |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Short-term capital gains referred to in Section 196 [corresponding to Section 111A of ITA 1961] | 2% | 10% |\n| Long-term capital gains referred to in Section 197 [corresponding to Section 112 of ITA 1961] or Section 198 [corresponding to Section 112A of ITA 1961] arising from the transfer of such securities. | 9.5% | 17.5% |\n\nThe final tax liability of promoters on the buyback of shares or securities will be as follows:\n\n| Product | Tax on short-term capital gain | Tax on short-term capital gain | Tax on long-term capital gain | Tax on long-term capital gain |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Product | In case of domestic companies | Others | In case of domestic companies | Others |\n| Listed equity shares (STT Paid) | 22% | 30% | 22% [Note 2] | 30% [Note 2] |\n| Listed equity shares (STT not paid) | Normal tax rate  [Note 1] | Normal tax rate  [Note 1] | 22% | 30% |\n| Unlisted equity shares | Normal tax rate  [Note 1] | Normal tax rate  [Note 1] | 22% | 30% |\n| Listed Preference shares | Normal tax rate  [Note 1] | Normal tax rate  [Note 1] | 22% | 30% |\n| Unlisted Preference shares | Normal tax rate  [Note 1] | Normal tax rate  [Note 1] | 22% | 30% |\n| Note 1: The short-term capital gains from unlisted shares or securities will continue to be taxable at the applicable tax rate, and no additional tax will be charged on them . Note 2 : The long -term capital gains from listed shares (STT Paid) will be taxable when it exceeds Rs. 1,25,000 . | Note 1: The short-term capital gains from unlisted shares or securities will continue to be taxable at the applicable tax rate, and no additional tax will be charged on them . Note 2 : The long -term capital gains from listed shares (STT Paid) will be taxable when it exceeds Rs. 1,25,000 . | Note 1: The short-term capital gains from unlisted shares or securities will continue to be taxable at the applicable tax rate, and no additional tax will be charged on them . Note 2 : The long -term capital gains from listed shares (STT Paid) will be taxable when it exceeds Rs. 1,25,000 . | Note 1: The short-term capital gains from unlisted shares or securities will continue to be taxable at the applicable tax rate, and no additional tax will be charged on them . Note 2 : The long -term capital gains from listed shares (STT Paid) will be taxable when it exceeds Rs. 1,25,000 . | Note 1: The short-term capital gains from unlisted shares or securities will continue to be taxable at the applicable tax rate, and no additional tax will be charged on them . Note 2 : The long -term capital gains from listed shares (STT Paid) will be taxable when it exceeds Rs. 1,25,000 . |\n\n3.8-5d\n\nWho are the promoters as per ITA 2025\n\nSection 69(3) of the ITA 2025 defines the term promoter for the purpose of the tax payable on the buyback. This provision defines the promoter of a listed and unlisted company separately.\n\nI.\n\nPromoters of a listed company\n\nClause (a) of Section 69(3) provides that a promoter of a company whose shares are listed on a recognised stock exchange in India shall mean a promoter as defined in Regulation 2(k) of the SEBI (Buy-Back of Securities) Regulations, 2018. This regulation takes the meaning of this term from Regulation 2(1)(s) of the SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011, which in turn refers to Regulation 2(1)(oo) and Regulation 2(1)(pp) of SEBI (Issue of Capital and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2018\n\nThe combined reading of these regulations provides that a promoter includes a member of the promoter group.\n\nMeaning of promoter\n\nPromoter shall include a person:\n\n(a)\n\nWho has been named as such in a draft offer document or offer document or is identified by the issuer in the annual return referred to in section 92 of the Companies Act, 2013; or\n\n(b)\n\nWho has control over the affairs of the issuer, directly or indirectly, whether as a shareholder, director or otherwise; or\n\n(c)\n\nIn accordance with whose advice, directions or instructions the board of directors of the issuer is accustomed to act. However, a person acting solely in a professional capacity will not be considered a promoter under this provision.\n\nRegulation 2(1)(e) of the SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 provides that the control includes the right to appoint majority of the directors or to control the management or policy decisions exercisable by a person or persons acting individually or in concert, directly or indirectly, including by virtue of their shareholding or management rights or shareholders agreements or voting agreements or in any other manner.\n\nMeaning of promoter group\n\nPromoter Group includes the following:\n\n(a)\n\nThe promoter;\n\n(b)\n\nAn immediate relative of the promoter (i.e. any spouse of that person, or any parent, brother, sister or child of the person or of the spouse);\n\n(c)\n\nIn case the promoter is a body corporate:\n\nA subsidiary or holding company of such body corporate;\n\nAny body corporate in which the promoter holds 20% or more of the equity share capital;\n\nAny body corporate that holds 20% or more of the promoters equity share capital.\n\n(d)\n\nIn case the promoter is an individual:\n\nAny body corporate in which 20% or more of the equity share capital is held by the promoter or an immediate relative of the promoter, or a firm or HUF in which the promoter or any one or more of their relative is a member;\n\nAny body corporate in which a body corporate as provided above holds 20% or more of the equity share capital; and\n\nAny HUF or firm in which the aggregate share of the promoter and their relatives is equal to or more than 20% of the total capital;\n\n(e)\n\nAll persons whose shareholding is aggregated under the heading shareholding of the promoter group\n\n10\n\n.\n\nWho will not be a promoter\n\nPromoter shall not include the following person merely by virtue of the fact that 20% or more of the equity share capital of the issuer is held by them, unless such person satisfies other requirements prescribed under these regulations;\n\n(a)\n\nFinancial Institution\n\n(b)\n\nScheduled Commercial Bank\n\n(c)\n\nForeign Portfolio Investor (other than Individuals)\n\n(d)\n\nCorporate bodies and family offices\n\n(e)\n\nMutual Fund\n\n(f)\n\nVenture Capital Fund\n\n(g)\n\nAlternative Investment Fund\n\n(h)\n\nForeign Venture Capital Investor\n\n(i)\n\nInsurance Company registered with the IRDAI\n\n(j)\n\nAny other category as specified by the Board from time to time\n\nHowever, the above persons shall be treated as a promoter group for the subsidiaries or companies promoted by them or for the mutual fund sponsored by them.\n\nII.\n\nOther Promoters\n\nClause (b) of Section 69(3) provides that a promoter, in any other case, shall mean the following:\n\n(a)\n\nA promoter as defined in Section 2(69) of the Companies Act, 2013; or\n\n(b)\n\nA person who holds, directly or indirectly, more than 10% of the shareholding in the company.\n\nThus, in an unlisted and a foreign company, the promoter shall include a person:\n\n(a)\n\nWho has been named as such in a prospectus or is identified by the company in the annual return referred to in Section 92 of the Companies Act, 2013; or\n\n(b)\n\nWho has control over the affairs of the company, directly or indirectly, whether as a shareholder, director or otherwise; or\n\n(c)\n\nIn accordance with whose advice, directions or instructions the Board of Directors of the company is accustomed to act. However, a person acting solely in a professional capacity will not be considered a promoter under this provision.\n\n(d)\n\nWho holds, directly or indirectly, more than 10% of the shareholding in the company.\n\nSection 2(27) of the Companies Act, 2013 provides that the term control shall include the right to appoint majority of the directors or to control the management or policy decisions exercisable by a person or persons acting individually or in concert, directly or indirectly, including by virtue of their shareholding or management rights or shareholders agreements or voting agreements or in any other manner.\n\n3.8-5e\n\nHow to compute the 10% shareholding in the company\n\nClause (b) of Section 69(3) provides that a promoter, in case of an unlisted or foreign company, shall include a person who holds, directly or indirectly, more than 10% of the shareholding in the company. Should it be computed after aggregating the holdings of the spouse and holdings through other entities\n\nThe phrase directly or indirectly in the provision requires looking beyond mere legal or registered ownership to encompass the true beneficial ownership and control. This means that if a spouse holds shares, but the economic benefits, risks, and control ultimately rest with the individual, then the individual is considered the beneficial owner, and the spouses holding should be aggregated. However, if the spouse is the registered owner of the shares and at the same time the beneficial owner of the same, this holding should not be aggregated with the individuals holding to compute the 10% threshold.\n\nThe following are the relevant principles laid down by the court, which one can consider while determining if the shareholding of the relative should be aggregated:\n\n(a)\n\nThe term indirectly requires looking beyond the immediate transaction to the ultimate source and benefit\n\n11\n\n. If a spouses holding can be traced back to an indirect transfer or benefit from the individual, it would fall within the ambit of indirectly held.\n\n(b)\n\nOnce it was established that the assessee was the real owner of the shares, and they had been purchased benami in the names of his wife and two sons, the ownership was presumed to remain with the assessee\n\n12\n\n. This decision is crucial as it allows tax authorities to look beyond the registered owner to identify the true beneficial owner, especially in family arrangements.\n\n(c)\n\nThe courts have emphasised strict construction when dealing with deeming fictions. The Tribunal held that for invoking Section 2(22)(e) of ITA 1961, it is the ownership of the shareholder alone in the company to which the loan/advance has been made, and not his or her relative or family members, which is the determinative factor for substantial interest\n\n13\n\n.\n\n(d)\n\nThe substance over form principle, reinforced by the General Anti-Avoidance Rules (GAAR), empowers tax authorities to scrutinise transactions and holdings to ascertain their true economic reality. If a spouses holding lacks commercial substance and is primarily intended to circumvent a shareholding limit, authorities can aggregate it with the individuals holding.\n\n3.8-5f\n\nWhen should the promoters status be computed\n\nThe provision is silent on when the shareholders status as a promoter should be calculated. Thus, it may be assumed that it should be computed on the date of the transfer of the shares.\n\n3.8-5g\n\nTaxability as per the DTAA\n\nWhere the shareholder is a non-resident or a foreign company, the final tax liability shall be determined in accordance with the double tax avoidance agreements (DTAA) that India has signed with various foreign countries. These DTAAs allocate the taxing right to the resident and source countries. The Articles of the DTAA decide which of the two Contracting States shall levy tax on incomes specified under the Articles of the DTAA.\n\nShare buybacks raise a characterisation issue under the DTAAs. The central issue is whether the gain or income arising in the hands of the shareholder should be treated as a dividend, a capital gain, or other income. This characterisation is critical because it determines which article of the DTAA applies and, consequently, which country retains the right to tax such income.\n\nThe OECD Model Convention defines dividends under Article 10(3) to mean income from shares, jouissance shares or jouissance rights, mining shares, founders shares or other rights that are not debt-claims but participate in profits, as well as income from other corporate rights which is subjected to the same taxation treatment as income from shares by the laws of the state of which the company making the distribution is a resident. This definition is broad and explicitly connects the characterisation of dividend income to the domestic law of the source state. Paragraph 28 of the OECD Commentary on Article 10 addresses situations involving redemption, liquidation, and disguised distributions of profits, and notes that where the domestic law of the source state treats the amounts paid to a shareholder on buyback as a distribution of profits, such amounts fall within the definition of dividends under Article 10.\n\nPara 31 of the OCED Model Commentary on Article 13 provides that if shares are alienated by a shareholder in connection with the liquidation of the issuing company or the redemption of shares or reduction of paid-up capital of that company, the difference between the proceeds obtained by the shareholder and the par value of the shares may be treated in the State of which the company is a resident as a distribution of accumulated profits and not as a capital gain. The Article does not prevent the State of residence of the company from taxing such distributions at the rates provided for in Article 10.\n\nWhere the domestic law of the source country does not treat the buyback proceeds as dividend income but instead characterises the transaction as a sale or transfer of a capital asset, Article 13 of the treaty on Capital Gains would apply.\n\nIn simple terms,\n\nthe treatment of share buybacks under a DTAA is not governed by a single universal rule but depends critically on the characterisation of the proceeds under the domestic law of the source state, the specific treaty language, and the model convention on which the bilateral treaty is based. Where domestic law treats buyback proceeds as a deemed dividend or distribution of profits, Article 10 applies, and the source state retai\n\nSection - 14:\nSection 13 (ITA 1961) v. Section 351/Section 337/Schedule XVI/Section 355/Section 353/Section 342 (ITA 2025)\n\nProvisions for exemption to charitable trust not to apply in certain cases\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 13: Section 11 not to apply in certain cases\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 351: Specified violation\n\nSection 337: Specified income\n\nSchedule XVI: Permitted modes of investment or deposits Forms or modes of investment or deposits by a registered non-profit organisation\n\nSection 355: Interpretation\n\nSection 353: Other violations\n\nSection 342: Accumulated income\n\n2.
