Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nA new comprehensive and self-contained regime is introduced for the taxation of non-profit organisations\n\nThe ITA 2025 represents a substantial legislative overhaul of the taxation regime applicable to registered non-profit organisations (NPOs). It replaces and restructures the provisions governing charitable and religious trusts under the ITA 1961 into a comprehensive and self-contained legislative code.\n\nA defining hallmark of the new framework in the ITA 2025 is the consolidation of provisions. Provisions which, under the ITA 1961, were dispersed across multiple Chapters, Sections, provisos, and Explanations have now been brought into a single legislative block. Redundancies have been eliminated, terminologies have been standardised, and definitions previously scattered across various provisions have been harmonised within a unified framework.\n\nThe legislative structure in the ITA 2025 for the taxation of registered NPOs is contained in Part B of Chapter XVII (Special Provisions relating to certain persons), which is divided into seven sub-parts, covering:\n\nRegistration\n\nIncome\n\nCommercial Activities\n\nCompliance Requirements\n\nViolations\n\nApproval for Donations\n\nInterpretation\n\n3.1-1.\n\nScattered provisions under ITA 1961\n\nUnder ITA 1961, provisions applicable to NPOs were spread across multiple Chapters, namely:\n\nChapter I: Section 2(15) (Definition of Charitable Purpose)\n\nChapter III: Sections 10(23C), 11, 12, 12A, 12AA, 12AB, 12AC, and 13\n\nChapter VIA: Section 80G\n\nChapter XII: Sections 115BBC and 115BBI\n\nChapter XII-EB: Sections 115TD, 115TE, and 115TF\n\nThis scattered arrangement, augmented by numerous cross-references, developed over decades due to piecemeal amendments. The outcome is a fragmented, proviso-heavy framework that is often difficult for taxpayers and practitioners to navigate. For example, Section 11 alone includes 13 Explanations and 16 Provisos, which complicate interpretation and compliance.\n\n3.1-2.\n\nUnification under ITA 2025\n\nThe ITA 2025 introduces uniform terminology, replacing varied expressions such as trust, institution, university, educational institution, and hospital with the single defined term registered non-profit organisation. Under the ITA 2025, an NPO is an entity that:\n\nMeets prescribed eligibility criteria,\n\nSecures registration, and\n\nQualifies for the regimes tax concessions.\n\nThe ITA 2025 offers several advantages. It adopts a single term, registered non-profit organisation, replacing multiple terms. It eliminates the dual regime of approval under Section 10(23C) and registration under Section 12AB, introducing a single registration framework. All overlapping or obsolete provisions have been deleted. Additionally, tables have been introduced to provide clarity on processes like registration applications, the year of taxability for specified income, and the computation of tax on accreted income.\n\nThe special provisions for NPOs in ITA 2025 are contained in Sections 332 to 355, grouped as follows:\n\nRegistration: Sections 332 to 333\n\nIncome: Sections 334 to 343\n\nCommercial Activities: Sections 344 to 346\n\nCompliance Requirements: Sections 347 to 350\n\nViolations and Penalties: Sections 351 to 353\n\nApproval for Donations: Section 354\n\nMerger: Section 354A\n\nInterpretation: Section 355\n\nClick here for more details on the changes in the provisions for the taxability of charitable trusts or registered NPOs\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 536:\nSection 297 (ITA 1961) v. Section 536 (ITA 2025)\n\nRepeals and savings\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 297\n\n: Repeals and savings\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 536\n\n: Repeal and savings\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 297(1) | Section 536(1) |\n| Section 297(2) | Section 536(2) |\n| Section 297(2)( a ) | Section 536(2)( c ) |\n| Section 297(2)( b ) | — |\n| Section 297(2)( c ) | Section 536(2)( e ) |\n| Section 297(2)( d )( i ) | Section 536(2)( c ) |\n| Section 297(2)( d )( ii ) | — |\n| Section 297(2)( e ) | Section 536(2)( c ) |\n| Section 297(2)( f ) | Section 536(2)( d ) |\n| Section 297(2)( g ) | — |\n| Section 297(2)( h ) | Section 536(2)( f ) |\n| Section 297(2)( i ) | Section 536(2)( g ) |\n| Section 297(2)( j ) | Section 536(2)( i ) |\n| Section 297(2)( k ) | Section 536(2)( j ) |\n| Section 297(2)( l ) & its Proviso | — |\n| Section 297(2)( m ) | Section 536(2)( k ) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nRepeal and Savings provision repeals the ITA 1961 and preserves the rights, liabilities, and proceedings already existing Section 536 of the ITA 2025\n\nSection 536 of the ITA 2025 repeals the ITA 1961 while maintaining rights, liabilities, and proceedings that already exist under it. Section 536 contains four sub-sections, which provide as follows:\n\n(a)\n\nSub-section (1) repeals the ITA 1961,\n\n(b)\n\nSub-section (2) ensures the continued application of the repealed ITA 1961 in specific situations.\n\n(c)\n\nSub-section (3) provides that where any reference is made in ITA 2025 to any tax year 2025-26 or earlier, it shall be construed as a reference to the corresponding previous year under the repealed Income-tax Act.\n\n(d)\n\nSub-section (4) applies the provisions of Section 6 of the General Clauses Act 1897 with regard to the effect of repeal.\n\nThe Finance Act 2026 makes a curative amendment in the opening portion of sub-section (2) of Section 536 to provide the correct reference to sub-section (4) instead of sub-section (3).\n\nThe paragraph below provides an overview of the 22 clauses contained in Section 536(2), out of which two clauses (h) and (l) have been amended by the Finance Act 2026:\n\n3.1-1.\n\nPreservation of previous operations\n\nSection 536(2)(a) states that\n\nnothing shall affect the previous operation of the repealed Income-tax Act and orders or anything duly done or suffered thereunder\n\n.\n\nThis clause effectively maintains the validity of all actions performed under the ITA 1961 prior to its repeal. Any assessments, filings, or orders issued under the ITA 1961 remain valid as if the new Act had never been enacted.\n\nPractically, taxpayers and authorities can rely on the validity of past assessments, notices, and judgments; nothing in the new law unwinds or invalidates those prior operations. This protection of the previous operation of the repealed Act ensures that repeal does not create legal uncertainty or retroactively disturb settled matters.\n\n3.1-2.\n\nProtection of vested rights and liabilities\n\nClause (2)(b) similarly states that\n\nnothing shall affect any right, privilege, obligation or liability acquired, accrued or incurred under the repealed Income-tax Act or orders under such repealed Act.\n\nThis clause protects rights already established, and liabilities taxpayers have incurred regarding revenue. For instance, if a taxpayer has a legally enforceable claim or obligation under the ITA 1961, that claim remains valid even after the Act is repealed. Additionally, any accrued taxes, carried-forward credits, or benefits, such as tax holidays or deductions, claimed under the ITA 1961 remain enforceable.\n\n3.1-3.\n\nContinuation of proceedings for pre-2026 tax years\n\nClause (2)(\n\nc\n\n) provides a broad non obstante saving for all proceedings related to tax years beginning before 1 April 2026. It states that the provisions of the repealed Income-tax Act\n\nshall continue to apply to any proceeding pending on the date of commencement of this Act and to any proceedings initiated on or after the 1st April, 2026 (including notices, assessment, reassessment, recomputation, rectification, penalty, reference, revision and appeals) in respect of any tax year beginning before the 1st April, 2026, and such proceedings shall be carried out as per the procedure specified in the repealed Income-tax Act.\n\nIn simple words, any dispute or case relating to a tax year beginning before 01-04-2026, whether already in process or newly started after the ITA 2025 comes into force, shall follow the norms of the ITA 1961. This ensures there is no procedural gap when transitioning to the new Act. For taxpayers and the department, this means that for pending assessments, appeals, rectifications, and related matters, the old thresholds, forms, and timetables will apply. This clause embodies the principle that repeal does not extinguish pending rights of action.\n\n3.1-4.\n\nContinuation of penalty proceedings\n\nClause (2)(\n\nd\n\n) reinforces continuity specifically for penalties. It states that any penalty proceeding in respect of any tax year beginning before the 1st April, 2026 may be initiated and imposed under the repealed Act as if the ITA 2025 had not been enacted.\n\nThis means penalties for earlier years (for example, under sections like 271B, 271C, etc., of the ITA 1961) can still be levied using the provisions of the old law, even if the penalty notice is issued after repeal. The effect is twofold: taxpayers cannot avoid penalties due for pre-2026 tax years simply because the law has changed, and the authorities retain full power to enforce penalties under the familiar ITA 1961 framework.\n\n3.1-5.\n\nContinuation of pending appeals\n\nClause (2)(e) extends the saving to any proceeding pending on the commencement of this Act before any income-tax authority, Appellate Tribunal, or court (by application, appeal, reference, revision or other means). In effect, it states that all existing disputes before any forum under the ITA 1961 remain active and must be resolved as if the new Act had not been enacted.\n\n3.1-6.\n\nContinuation of elections, declarations, and options\n\nUnder clause (2)(\n\nf\n\n), any election, declaration, or option exercised by a taxpayer under the ITA 1961 that was in force immediately before the ITA 2025s commencement is deemed to have been made under the corresponding provision of the ITA 2025.\n\nUnder the ITA 1961, many provisions allow a taxpayer to choose whether to participate in certain treatments, such as opting out of the new tax regime under section 115BAC or declaring a specific accounting method. This clause ensures that if an assessee has already made such an election under the ITA 1961, that choice remains valid without interruption. It prevents taxpayers from having to re-file elections or lose benefits simply because the Acts section number has changed.\n\n3.1-7.\n\nInterest on refunds and payment defaults\n\nClause (2)(g), as amended by the Finance Act 2026, governs the computation of interest for the period commencing on or after 01-04-2026 in cases involving refunds or tax payments relating to any tax year beginning before 01-04-2026, where such payment/refund arises on or after 01-04-2026.\n\nIn such cases, provisions of the ITA 1961 governing interest on refunds or defaults will continue to apply for the period after 01-04-2026. However, the rate of interest will not remain the same as under the repealed ITA 1961. The applicable rate shall be the one prescribed under the corresponding provisions of the ITA 2025. In other words, the ITA 1961 mechanism for computing interest on refunds is retained, but the rate specified in ITA 2025 will apply. Further, such substitution of the interest rate will take effect on the date the new rate is modified under the ITA 2025.\n\nThus, for pending proceedings from earlier tax years, interest for the period from 01-04-2026 to the date of actual payment will be calculated in accordance with the provisions of ITA 1961, with the rates provided under ITA 2025.\n\n3.1-8.\n\nComputation of income upon breach of certain conditions or for any other reason\n\nClause (2)(h), as amended by the Finance Act 2026, deals with the situation where any sum has been allowed as a deduction (or not included in the total income of any person) due to fulfilment of certain conditions (or for any other reason) for the tax year 2025-26 or earlier and subsequently these conditions were violated. Consequently, due to non-fulfilment of prescribed conditions, such a sum was required to be included in the total income of any subsequent tax year under the repealed ITA 1961, if it had not been repealed. The sum that the ITA 1961 would have included in income must be included in the subsequent tax year under the same head, just as the ITA 1961 would have done under the new Act.\n\nFor example,\n\nunder the ITA 1961, the Section 54F exemption was available to the assessee. As per one of the conditions, the assessee cannot sell the newly acquired house property within three years of its acquisition. If, after the repeal of the ITA 1961, the asset is sold and the three-year period has not yet elapsed, the exemption will be revoked, and the income will be taxed under Section 86 of ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 54F of ITA 1961).\n\n3.1-9.\n\nRecovery of outstanding liabilities\n\nClause (2)(\n\ni\n\n) addresses unpaid amounts. It states that any sum payable under the repealed Act\n\nmay be recovered under this Act without prejudice to any action already taken for recovery of such sum under the repealed Income Tax Act.\n\nThis clause is procedural and allows the tax department to use the machinery of the ITA 2025 (e.g., collection processes, set-off procedures) to collect any tax due under the ITA 1961, without invalidating any recovery steps already initiated. It ensures enforcement continuity. If the department has already started a recovery action under the ITA 1961 (e.g., attachment of bank accounts), it can continue or complete that action; at the same time, it can also invoke the recovery provisions of the ITA 2025 if necessary (for example, adjustments against refunds, etc.).\n\n3.1-10.\n\nContinuity of DTAAs, notifications, circulars, approvals, etc.\n\nClause (2)(j) preserves all official acts and structures established under the old law. It states that any agreement, appointment, approval, recognition, circular, direction, instruction, notification, order, rule or any scheme issued under the ITA 1961, as long as it does not conflict with the new Act, will be considered issued under the corresponding provision of the new Act and will remain in effect.\n\n3.1-11.\n\nLimitation periods for appeals and applications\n\nClause (2)(k) safeguards the doctrine of finality in procedural timelines. It states that if the period for filing any application, appeal, reference, or revision under the ITA 1961 had already expired before the start of the ITA 2025, the ITA 2025 cannot be used to revive that right simply because it provides a longer period or an extension.\n\nIn other words, if a taxpayer missed the deadline under the old law before repeal, the new law does not reopen that window simply because the ITA 2025 provides more time. This aligns with the established rule that limitation periods are procedural, not substantive. A repealing law (or its savings provisions) does not usually reset expired limitation clocks.\n\n3.1-12.\n\nCarry forward of tax credits\n\nClause (2)(l), as amended by the Finance Act 2026, states that any tax credit, such as the MAT credit under Section 115JAA or the AMT credit under 115JD, that could be carried forward under the ITA 1961 for tax years 2025-26 or earlier, is considered eligible under the relevant provision of ITA 1961 or corresponding Section 206(3) or (4), as applicable, of the ITA 2025. Furthermore, such credit shall be granted under the ITA 2025 for the same duration as under the ITA 1961, provided the assessee fulfils the conditions of the ITA 2025.\n\n3.1-13.\n\nCarry forward of specified losses\n\nClause (2)(\n\nm\n\n) covers the carry-forward of losses under certain heads or sources. It states that any loss (or loss allowance) in the specified categories that was brought forward from tax years before 1 April 2026 (assuming the ITA 1961 had not been repealed) shall be set off and carried forward against income computed under the ITA 2025,\n\nin the manner provided in the respective section of the repealed Income-tax Act\n\n. The specified categories include losses specified in sections 71B, 72, 73, 73A, and 74A.\n\n3.1-14.\n\nCarry forward of capital losses\n\nClause (2)(\n\nn\n\n) specifically deals with capital losses brought forward from the tax year beginning before 01-04-2026. It states that any loss under the head Capital gains, whether long-term or short-term, determined under section 74 of the repealed ITA 1961 for a tax year commencing before the 1st April, 2026, shall continue to be carried forward and set off against income under the head Capital gains computed under the ITA 2025.\n\nCarry forward and set-off of such loss shall be allowed in the same manner as provided in the ITA 1961, but only up to eight assessment years immediately following the assessment year in which the loss was first determined under the repealed ITA 1961.\n\n3.1-15.\n\nAmalgamation loss\n\nClause (2)(o) addresses cases of corporate mergers or amalgamations covered by section 72A of the ITA 1961. It states that if, before repeal, an amalgamated (or successor) company had made any set-off of loss or depreciation in its hands (pursuant to section 72A), then if, after repeal, any of the old section 72A conditions are breached, the amount of set-off is deemed to be the income of the successor company under the ITA 2025 for the year of breach. In effect, this carries forward the regime of section 72A into the 2025 law.\n\n3.1-16.\n\nCooperative bank losses\n\nClause (2)(p) is similar to clause (2)(o) but pertains to cooperative banks under section 72AB. It states that any set-off of accumulated loss or unabsorbed depreciation allowed to a successor co-operative bank under section 72AB is considered the income of the successor bank under the ITA 2025 for the year in which any of the section 72AB conditions are not met.\n\n3.1-17.\n\nTaxation of previously exempt transfers\n\nClause (2)(q) governs certain transfers of capital assets that were exempt from capital gains under Section 47 of the ITA 1961 but become taxable upon breach of conditions (e.g., under Section 47A). It states that any profits or gains from such transfers (which were not taxed under the old Act by virtue of section 47(iv), (v), (xiii), (xiiib), or (xiv)) are considered income under Capital gains for the relevant tax year.\n\n| (a) In which the transfer took place if the conditions in section 47A(1)( i ) or ( ii ) of the ITA 1961 are satisfied, or |\n| --- |\n| (b) In which the conditions in section 47(xiii), (xiiib), or (xiv) of the ITA 1961 are not met. |\n\n3.1-18.\n\nDepreciation allowance\n\nClause (2)(r) provides a transition for depreciation and investment allowances carried forward. It states that if an allowance under sections 32(2) or 35(4) of the ITA 1961 would have been carried forward to the tax year starting 1 April 2026, then that carry-forward is added to the capital allowances under the corresponding provisions of the ITA 2025 for the tax year 2026-27 and is treated as part of that allowance. If there is no such allowance for that tax year, it is considered an allowance for the tax year 2026-27.\n\nIn effect, any unused investment allowance or initial allowance remaining as of FY 2025-26 is absorbed into the first tax year of the ITA 2025. Taxpayers who purchased eligible assets and still have unexpired allowances will retain those allowances.\n\n3.1-19.\n\nContinuation of certain deductions\n\nClause (2)(s) covers specific deductions under Sections 35ABA, 35ABB, 35D, 35DD, 35DDA, 35E, and the first proviso to section 36(1)(ix) of the ITA 1961, which relate to deferral or amortisation of business expenditure.\n\nThe clause states that, upon meeting the conditions, these deductions shall continue to be allowed under the ITA 2025 for tax years starting on or after 1 April 2026 (as if the ITA 1961 had not been repealed), and that such deduction is added to the deferred revenue expenditure allowance in the relevant provisions of the ITA 2025.\n\n3.1-20.\n\nAdjustment to bad debts provisioning\n\nClause (2)(t) addresses transitional provisions for bad and doubtful debts. It states that the credit balance in the provision for bad and doubtful debts under section 36(1)(viia) of the ITA 1961, as of the last day of the financial year 2025-26, will be added to the provision for bad debts account under the ITA 2025 for the tax year 2026-27 and will be considered part of that amount.\n\nFor example, if, on March 31, 2026, a bank or financial institution had set aside an amount as a provision for bad debts, that opening balance is carried forward into the ITA 2025. It is treated as if the ITA 2025s provision account already includes that amount. If the ITA 2025s provisions did not otherwise have an existing balance for that year, the old balance becomes the opening balance.\n\n3.1-21.\n\nContinuation of faceless schemes\n\nClause (2)(u) addresses tax administration schemes notified to eliminate interface with taxpayers. It states that any such scheme notified under the provisions of the repealed Act remains in force under the ITA 2025.\n\nFurthermore, if there is a corresponding provision in the ITA 2025, the scheme is deemed to be made under it. If no exact match exists, it is considered made under Section 532 (the residual savings clause for other cases).\n\n3.1-22.\n\nContinuation of search and requisition proceedings\n\nClause (2)(v) protects searches and requisitions made under the ITA 1961. It states that if a search under section 132 or a requisition under section 132A was started before the start date of the ITA 2025, the rules of the repealed Act still apply to any proceedings related to that search or requisition.\n\nIn other words, if the revenue had already initiated a search, survey, or investigation under the ITA 1961, then the rules, powers, and procedures of the ITA 1961 govern the resulting proceedings.\n\n3.1-23.\n\nInterpretation of tax year references\n\nSub-section (3) of Section 536 clarifies the tax year. It states that any reference in the ITA 2025 to a tax year starting on 1 April 2025 or earlier should be read as referring to the corresponding previous year under the repealed ITA 1961.\n\n3.1-24.\n\nApplication of the General Clauses Act, 1897\n\nSub-section (4) of section 536 confirms that, without prejudice to the provisions of sub-section (2), the effect of repeal is governed by Section 6 of the General Clauses Act, 1897. Section 6 of this Act is a longstanding saving provision that generally preserves rights, liabilities, and legal proceedings arising under a repealed enactment, unless a contrary intention is explicitly stated.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 141:\nSection 80-IB (ITA 1961) v. Analysis of changes\n\nThere are no substantial changes in the provisions of the ITA 2025 compared to the ITA 1961.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\n111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000009187 direct tax laws 0 111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000009188 international taxation 0 111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000017592 transfer pricing 0 111050000000020048 comparative 000012092025 111050000000020049 income tax act 1961 000120920251 0000 Section 80-IB (ITA 1961) v. Section 141 (ITA 2025)\n\nSection - 532:\nSection 92CA (ITA 1961) v. Section 166/Section 532 (ITA 2025)\n\nReference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 92CA\n\n: Reference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 166\n\n: Reference to Transfer Pricing Officer\n\nSection 532\n\n: Power to frame schemes\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 92CA(1) | Section 166(1) |\n| Section 92CA(1), 1st Proviso | Section 166(2) |\n| Section 92CA(1), 2nd Proviso | Section 166(3) |\n| Section 92CA(2) | Section 166(4) |\n| Section 92CA(2A) | Section 166(5)( a ) & long line |\n| Section 92CA(2B) | Section 166(5)( b ) & long line |\n| Section 92CA(2C) | — |\n| Section 92CA(3) | Section 166(6) |\n| Section 92CA(3A) | Section 166(7) |\n| Section 92CA(3A), Proviso | Section 166(8) |\n| Section 92CA(3AA) | Section 166(7) |\n| Section 92CA(3B) | Section 166(9) |\n| Section 92CA(3B), Proviso | Section 166(10) |\n| Section 92CA(4) | Section 166(11) |\n| Section 92CA(4A) | Section 166(12) |\n| Section 92CA(5) | Section 166(13)( a ) |\n| Section 92CA(6) | Section 166(13)( b ) |\n| Section 92CA(7) | Section 166(14) |\n| Section 92CA(8) | Section 532(1) |\n| Section 92CA(9) | Section 532(2) |\n| Section 92CA(10) | Section 532(4) |\n| Section 92CA(11) | Section 166(15) |\n| Section 92CA(11), Proviso | — |\n| Section 92CA(12) | Section 166(16) |\n| Section 92CA, Explanation | Section 166(17) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nChange in the time limit to pass the order by the Transfer Pricing Officer Section 166 of ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 92CA of ITA 1961)\n\n3.1-1.\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nSection 92CA of the ITA 1961 deals with the case where the assessee has entered into an international transaction or specified domestic transaction in any previous year, and the AO may refer the computation of the Arms Length Price (ALP) in relation to the said international transaction or specified domestic transaction under Section 92C to the Transfer Pricing Officer (TPO).\n\nSub-section (3A) to section 92CA states that TPO is required to pass an order before sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation under section 153, or as the case may be, in Section 153B for making the order of assessment, reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, as the case may be, expires.\n\n3.1-1a.\n\nExclusion of the last day of assessment limitation\n\nThe interpretation of the phrase before sixty days prior to the date has been a consistent point of contention between taxpayers and the revenue, leading to significant litigation.\n\nThe Madras High Court, in the case of\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras)\n\n, which affirmed the Single Judges decision in\n\nJt. CIT vs. Pfizer Healthcare India (P.) Ltd. 2021 124 taxmann.com 536 (Madras)\n\n, held that the word date in Section 92CA(3A) refers to the last date for the AO to pass the assessment order under Section 153 (e.g., December 31st). The crucial words prior to indicate that, for the purpose of calculating the 60 days, this last date must be excluded. Therefore, if the assessment limitation expires on December 31st, the TPO order ought to have been passed on or before October 31st.\n\nThis interpretation was consistently followed across numerous ITAT benches, including in the following cases:\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nZydus Lifesciences Ltd. 2024 163 taxmann.com 349 (Ahmedabad Trib.)\n\nFrost & Sullivan (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 739 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nShell India Markets (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 366 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nSiemens Technology and Services (P.) Ltd. 2024 162 taxmann.com 357 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nHuhtamaki India Ltd. 2024 164 taxmann.com 194 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nAtos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai Trib.)\n\nCAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\nPioneer India Electronics (P.) Ltd. 2025 178 taxmann.com 553 (Delhi Trib.)\n\nSigma Aldrich Chemicals (P.) Ltd. 2022 141 taxmann.com 431 (Bangalore Trib.)\n\n3.1-1b.\n\nMandatory nature of the time limit\n\nAnother critical aspect clarified by the judiciary is whether the time limit prescribed in Section 92CA(3A) is mandatory or merely directory. The revenue often argued that the use of the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A) implies a directory nature.\n\nHowever, the Madras High Court in\n\nDy. CIT vs. Saint Gobain India (P.) Ltd. 2022 137 taxmann.com 215 (Madras\n\n)\n\n, after a detailed analysis of the scheme of the Act and the consequences of non-compliance, unequivocally held that the word\n\nmay\n\nin Section 92CA(3A)\n\nhas to be construed as shall and the time period fixed therein has to be scrupulously followed.\n\nThe court reasoned that the overall scheme of transfer pricing assessments, with various interlinked time limits (including extensions under the\n\nproviso\n\nto Section 92CA(3A) and Section 153), indicates a legislative intent to ensure timely completion. If the TPOs time limit were merely directory, it would undermine the entire assessment process. This principle has been reiterated in the case of\n\nAsst. CIT vs. CAE India (P.) Ltd. 2023 153 taxmann.com 691 (Bangalore - Trib.).\n\n3.1-1c.\n\nConsequence of time-barred TPO order\n\nThe judicial consensus is that a TPO order passed beyond the prescribed limitation period is\n\nillegal, null and void ab initio\n\nand\n\nnon est in the eyes of law\n\n. This has a cascading effect on subsequent assessment proceedings.\n\nAs established in\n\nTeleperformance Global Services (P.) Ltd. 2023 155 taxmann.com 658 (Mumbai - Trib.) and Atos India (P.) Ltd. 2023 152 taxmann.com 217 (Mumbai - Trib.),\n\nif the TPO order is time-barred, the assessee ceases to be an eligible assessee under Section 144C(15)(b)(i). Consequently, the extended time period for assessment under Section 153 (due to TPO reference) becomes inapplicable, and the draft assessment order and the final assessment order passed by the AO also become\n\nvoid ab initio\n\nand are liable to be quashed. This principle has been consistently applied across various ITAT decisions, such as\n\nIQVIA RDS (India) (P.) Ltd. 2025 181 taxmann.com 623 (Bangalore - Trib.) and World Sports (India) (P.) Ltd. 2023 154 taxmann.com 181 (Mumbai - Trib.).\n\n3.1-2.\n\nAmendment by the Finance Act 2026\n\nThe Finance Act 2026 inserted the following sub-section (3AA) to section 92CA:\n\n(3AA). Notwithstanding anything contained in any judgment, order or decree of any court, for the purposes of making order under sub-section (3), the calculation of sixty days shall be made and shall always be deemed to have been made in the following manner, namely:\n\n(a) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (not being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 30th of January of that year;\n\n(b) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of March of any year (being a leap year), the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 31st of January of that year;\n\n(c) where the period of limitation expires on 31st of December of any year, the order under sub-section (3) may be made up to the 1st of November of that year.\n\nThe new sub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA clarifies the\n\n60-day limitation\n\nperiod for passing an order under Section 92CA(3). It provides deemed computation rules:\n\n(a)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (non-leap year), the order can be passed up to 30th January.\n\n(b)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 March (in a leap year), the order can be passed up to 31st January.\n\n(c)\n\nIf the limitation ends on 31 December, the order can be passed up to 1\n\nst\n\nNovember.\n\nThe most significant aspect of this amendment is its retrospective application with effect from 01-06-2007 and the explicit notwithstanding clause, which states:\n\nNotwithstanding any judgment, order, or decree of any court\n\n.\n\nThis phrase clearly indicates a legislative intent to override and nullify any judicial pronouncements that may have interpreted the sixty-day period differently or in a manner perceived to be contrary to the revenues interest.\n\nFor instance\n\n, in the table below, the limitation period after the retrospective amendment is explained:\n\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | Position before amendment | Position before amendment | Position after amendment | Position after amendment |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Assessment Years | Section 153 limitation period | TPO order date | 60 days expires on (as per court rulings) | Legal Status of TPO order | 60 days expires on (after amendment) | Legal Status of TPO order |\n| 2021-22 | 31-12-2023 * | 01-11-2023 | 31-10-2023 | Time-Barred | 01-11-2023 | Valid |\n| 2022-23 | 31-03-2025 # | 31-01-2025 | 30-01-2025 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2025 | Valid |\n| 2023-24 | 31-03-2026 # | 31-01-2026 | 30-01-2026 | Time-Barred | 31-01-2026 | Valid |\n| * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. | * For Assessment Year 2021–22, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 9 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. # For Assessment Years 2022–23 and 2023–24, the time limit under Section 153 for passing the assessment order is within 12 months from the end of the assessment year in which the income was first assessable. Since the matter has been referred to the TPO, the limitation period shall be further extended by 12 months. |\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nImpact on the validity of past TPO orders\n\nSub-section (3AA) of Section 92CA, with its retrospective operation, validates all TPO orders that were held time-barred by courts due to a 1-day delay. The notwithstanding clause ensures that no court order invalidating such TPO orders can survive as binding precedent on this specific point. This means all assessments set aside solely because the TPO order was issued one day beyond the judicially interpreted deadline are revived. Further, for cases currently in litigation in which the validity of a TPO order is challenged on limitation grounds, the retrospective amendment fundamentally alters the legal landscape.\n\n3.1-3.\n\nPosition under the ITA 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act 2026)\n\nSub-section (7) of Section 166 (corresponding to Section 92CA(3) of the ITA 1961) provided a similar time limit of sixty days before the expiry of the limitation period for making the order of assessment, reassessment, recomputation or fresh assessment. The Finance Act 2026 makes an amendment to Section 166 by substituting sub-section (7), which reads as follows:\n\n(7) Where a reference was made under sub-section (1), an order under sub-section (6) may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which period of limitation referred to in section 286 or 296, for making the order of assessment or reassessment or recomputation or fresh assessment, expires and accordingly, where such period expires on\n\n(a) the 31st March of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st January of that year;\n\n(b) the 31st December of any year, the order under sub-section (6) shall be made on or before the 31st October of that year.\n\nThis amendment will take effect from 1st April 2026 and will, accordingly, apply in relation to the tax year 2026-2027 and subsequent years.\n\n3.1-3a.\n\nShift from day-based to month-based computation\n\nThe substituted provision takes a notable departure from the ITA 1961 framework by replacing the day-based computation of limitation periods with a month-based approach. Section 166(7) provides that:\n\n.order may be made at any time before one month prior to the month in which the period of limitation .. expires\n\nUnder Section 92CA of the ITA 1961, limitation periods and procedural timelines were prescribed in terms of days, sixty days prior to the date on which the period of limitation expires. Section 166(7) of the ITA 2025 sets out the timelines, expressed in months. The computation is straightforward. The TPO merely needs to identify the month in which the limitation expires and ensure that the order is passed in the month immediately before that month. Further, the substituted provision provides that where the limitation period expires on 31\n\nst\n\nMarch or 31\n\nst\n\nDecember, as the case may be, the order under Section 166(6) should be passed on or before 31\n\nst\n\nJanuary or 31\n\nst\n\nOctober, respectively.\n\n3.1-3b.\n\nIllustration\n\nThe limitation period to pass order under section 166(7) under ITA 2025 is as follows:\n\n| Tax Year | Section 286 limitation period | Extension of limitation period as matter is referred to TPO | Limitation period under Section 286 (including extension) | Time limit to pass order under section 166(7) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| 2024-25 | 31-03-2027 | 12 months | 31-03-2028 | 31-01-2028 |\n| 2025-26 | 31-03-2028 | 12 months | 31-03-2029 | 31-01-2029 |\n\n3.2.\n\nPowers given to the central government to make faceless schemes for various provisions\n\n3.2-1.\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nUnder the 1961 Act,\n\nthe Central Government was empowered to notify schemes under various sections for various purposes\n\n.\n\nThe intent of such schemes was to\n\nimpart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface between the Income-tax authority, adjudication/appellant authorities (ITAT, DRP/DRC, Board of Advance Rulings) and the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation;\n\n(c)\n\nIntroducing disposal of appeals/disputes, imposition of penalty and a team-based functioning, with dynamic jurisdiction.\n\nThe relevant provisions under which the Central Government was empowered to notify such schemes and their respective purposes were as follows:\n\n| FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND THEIR PURPOSE | FACELESS PROCEEDINGS AND prescribed under Section 144B itself | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Relating to Dispute Resolution and Appeals | Faceless Appeal Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 253 and 255 | Disposal of appeal by ITAT | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 245D | Faceless functioning of the Interim Board | e-Settlement Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 245MA | Faceless functioning of Dispute Resolution Committee | e-Dispute Resolution Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | - | Yes |\n| Section 245R | rectification, amendments in order, issuance of demand notice and intimation of loss | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 264A | Faceless revision of orders under Section 263 and 264 | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 264B | Faceless effect to the order passed by CIT (A), ITAT, High Court, Supreme Court and revision orders | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Relating to Penalties and of prosecution and compounding of offence | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Others | Others | Others | Others | Others | Others | Others |\n| Section 92CA | Determination of Arm’s Length Price by the Transfer Pricing Officer | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 130 | Faceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities | Faceless Jurisdiction of Income-tax Authorities Scheme, 2022 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 135A | Faceless collection of information | e-Verification Scheme, 2021 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 231 | Faceless collection and recovery of tax | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Section 293D | Faceless approval or registration by an Income-tax authority under any provision of the Income-tax Act | - | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n\nIt must be noted that there were 4 purposes for which faceless schemes were notified: 1) to eliminate interface, 2) to optimise utilisation of resources, 3) to enable team-based functioning, and 4) to enable dynamic jurisdiction. Almost all the above provisions talked about the 1\n\nst\n\n, 2\n\nnd\n\n, and 4\n\nth\n\npurposes. However, the 3\n\nrd\n\npurpose, i.e., team-based functioning, was prescribed only under specified provisions. Under those specified provisions (except for the exercise of powers under section 142 and for assessments), no scheme has been issued yet. The manner of faceless assessment was prescribed under Section 144B itself. Further, the faceless schemes issued for the exercise of powers under Section 142 also refer to Section 144B.\n\n3.2-2.\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nUnder the 2025 Act, a single Section 532 (Power to frame schemes) is introduced to give the power to the Central Government to make schemes.\n\nUnder Section 532, the Central Government is empowered to make schemes for any purpose under the 2025 Act\n\nto impart greater efficiency, transparency and accountability by:\n\n(a)\n\nEliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person to the extent technologically feasible;\n\n(b)\n\nOptimising utilisation of the resources through economies of scale and functional specialisation.\n\nIt must be noted that the purpose of team-based functioning and dynamic jurisdiction is absent from Section 532 of the 2025 Act. Further, it also provides that if a scheme was already notified under the 1961 Act, the Central Government may amend or modify such scheme.\n\nThe transition provision, i.e., Section 536 of the 2025 Act, provides that any scheme which has been notified under the provisions of the repealed Income-tax Act with a view to eliminating the interface with the assessee or any other person, the said scheme shall be deemed to have been made and shall continue in force accordingly:\n\n(a)\n\nunder the corresponding provisions of this Act; or\n\n(b)\n\nunder section 532, where there is no such corresponding provision.\n\nIt must be noted that, though Section 532 empowers the Central Government to make a scheme for any purpose under the 2025 Act, there are specific provisions as well wherein the power has been given to make a scheme. Such provisions are as follows:\n\nFaceless jurisdiction of income-tax authorities Section 245 of the 2025 Act/Section 130 of the 1961 Act\n\nFaceless collection of information Section 260 of the 2025 Act/Section 135A of the 1961 Act\n\nUnder these two specific provisions, the scheme may be made for all four purposes as were there under the old Act. Thus, the purpose of the scheme may also involve team-based functioning with dynamic jurisdiction, alongside eliminating the interface between the income-tax authority and the assessee and optimum utilisation of resources.\n\nFurther, it is provided under the 2025 Act that the manner of faceless assessment as currently provided under the relevant provision of the Act itself will be provided under the rules.\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 162:\nSection 92A (ITA 1961) v. Section 162 (ITA 2025)\n\nMeaning of associated enterprise\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 92A\n\n: Meaning of associated enterprise\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 162\n\n: Meaning of associated enterprise\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 92A(1) | Section 162(1) |\n| Section 92A(1)( a ) | Section 162(1)( a ) |\n| Section 92A(1)( b ) | Section 162(1)( a )( i ) |\n| Section 92A(2) | — |\n| Section 92A(2)( a ) | Section 162(1)( a )( ii ) |\n| Section 92A(2)( b ) | Section 162(1)( a )( iii ) |\n| Section 92A(2)( c ) to ( m ) | Section 162(1)( b ) to ( l ) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1.\n\nRemoving ambiguity from the definition of Associated Enterprise under Section 162 of the ITA 2025 (Corresponding to Section 92A of the ITA 1961)\n\n3.1-1.\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nFor the purpose of the Transfer Pricing Chapter, the definition of Associated Enterprise (AE) is given under section 92A. It provides as follows:\n\n92A. (1) For the purposes of this section and sections 92, 92B, 92C, 92D, 92E and 92F, associated enterprise, in relation to another enterprise, means an enterprise-\n\n(a) which participates, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in the management or control or capital of the other enterprise; or\n\n(b) in respect of which one or more persons who participate, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in its management or control or capital, are the same persons who participate, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in the management or control or capital of the other enterprise.\n\n(2) For the purposes of sub-section (1), two enterprises shall be deemed to be associated enterprises if, at any time during the previous year,\n\n(a) one enterprise holds, directly or indirectly, shares carrying not less than twenty-six per cent of the voting power in the other enterprise; or\n\n(b) any person or enterprise holds, directly or indirectly, shares carrying not less than twenty-six per cent of the voting power in each of such enterprises; or\n\n(c) a loan advanced by one enterprise to the other enterprise constitutes not less than fifty-one per cent of the book value of the total assets of the other enterprise; or\n\n(d) one enterprise guarantees .\n\nSection 92A defined the term associated enterprise in two layers. Sub-section (1) gave a broad principle based on participation in management, control, or capital, while sub-section (2) contained 13 deeming rules to treat an enterprise as AE, such as shareholding, loan dependency, and supply dependency.\n\nSince sub-section (1) covers participation in management, control or capital and sub-section (2) separately lists the circumstances, which leads to confusion whether both must be satisfied together. Taxpayers sometimes argued that unless there was actual control under sub-section (1), merely satisfying the conditions of sub-section (2) would not make them AEs.\n\nSeveral rulings, including those of the Supreme Court, had to interpret this interplay, creating uncertainty for taxpayers.\n\nThe Ahmedabad Tribunal in\n\nAsst. CIT v. Veer Gems 2017 77 taxmann.com 127 (Ahmedabad - Trib.)\n\ndealt with this controversy. It held that sub-section (1) laid down the broad principle, while sub-section (2) provided concrete illustrations. At the same time, it also held that sub-section (2) governed the scope of sub-section (1) by defining what constituted participation in management, capital, or control. In effect, if a situation was not covered under section 92A(2), even clear participation under sub-section (1) would not, by itself, establish an AE relationship. Thus, the Tribunal concluded that both sub-sections must be read cumulatively.\n\nThis interpretation was subsequently endorsed by the Gujarat High Court in\n\nCIT v. Veer Gems 2017 83 taxmann.com 271 (Guj.)\n\nand later upheld by the Supreme Court in\n\n2018 95 taxmann.com 16 (SC)\n\n. The Court, while examining the Memorandum to the Finance Bill, 2002, noted that the amendment to section 92A(2) clarified legislative intent: mere participation in management, control, or capital was insufficient unless supported by the specific criteria of sub-section (2). Reading the two sub-sections disjunctively would render one of them meaningless, contrary to settled principles of statutory interpretation. The Karnataka High Court in\n\nPage Industries Ltd. v. PCIT 2021 124 taxmann.com 605 (Kar.)\n\nreiterated this position.\n\nHowever, the Mumbai Tribunal in\n\nKaybee (P.) Ltd. v. ITO 2015 57 taxmann.com 449 (Mum.)\n\nhad taken a different view. It held that section 92A(2) was a deeming provision that expanded, rather than restricted, the general scope of sub-section (1). Accordingly, satisfaction of section 92A(1) alone could be sufficient to establish an AE relationship, and the absence of a clause under sub-section (2) would not necessarily prevent AE status. Under this approach, sub-sections (1) and (2) could operate independently.\n\n3.1-2.\n\nAnalysis of change in the ITA 1961 vis-à-vis the ITA 2025\n\nSection 162 of the ITA 2025, while incorporating the definition of Associated Enterprise, addressed this ambiguity. Section 162 combines the two-tier structure of Section 92CA of the ITA 1961 into a single integrated provision. It no longer distinguishes between a broad definition and a separate deeming fiction.\n\nFurther, the revised language in the ITA 2025 clarifies that the conditions specified under clause (a) or clause (b) of sub-section (2), or clause (b) of sub-section (1) of old section are to be read in relation to an entity participating, whether directly, indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in the management, control, or capital of another enterprise.\n\nThe revised definition is as follows:\n\n162. (1) For the purposes of this Chapter, the expression associated enterprise, in relation to another enterprise, means an enterprise\n\n(a) which participates, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in the management or control or capital of the other enterprise in the following manner,\n\n(i) one or more persons who participate, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in management or control or capital of one enterprise, also participate, directly or indirectly, or through one or more intermediaries, in the management or control or capital of the other enterprise; or\n\n(ii) one enterprise holds, at any time during the tax year, directly or indirectly, shares carrying not less than 26% of the voting power in the other enterprise; or\n\n(iii) any person or enterprise holds, at any time during the tax year, directly or indirectly, shares carrying not less than 26% of the voting power in each of such enterprises; or\n\n(b) which has advanced a loan to the other enterprise and such loan constitutes not less than 51% of the book value of the total assets of the other enterprise; or..\n\nThe rest of the conditions specified in section 92A of the ITA 1961 remain intact in section 162 of the ITA 2025\n\n3.1-2a.\n\nAssociated Enterprise for specified domestic transaction (SDT)\n\nThe ITA 2025 also introduced the definition of associated enterprise in relation to a specified domestic transaction (SDT) undertaken by an assessee. In the ITA 1961, there was no specific provision defining the meaning of AE for the purpose of SDT.\n\nThis missing reference had no significant impact because the SDT is levied based on certain specified transactions, i.e., any transfer of goods or services referred to in section 80-IA(8), any business transacted between the persons referred to in section 115BAB(6), etc.\n\nAs transfer pricing provisions apply to Associated Enterprises, and to prevent potential litigation arising from the absence of a definition of Associated Enterprise in the context of SDT, the ITA 2025 has introduced such a definition. It provides that, for the purposes of SDT, the term Associated Enterprise shall also include:\n\n(a)\n\nany other units, undertakings, or businesses of the assessee in respect of transactions referred to in section 122 or section 140(9);\n\n(b)\n\nany other person specified under section 140(13) or section 205(4) in relation to the transactions referred to therein; and\n\n(c)\n\nother units, undertakings, enterprises, or businesses of the assessee, or any person referred to in section 140(13), in respect of transactions covered under section 144 or transactions under Chapter VIII to which section 140(9), section 140(13), or section 80-IA(8) or (10) of the Income-tax Act, 1961 apply.\n\n3.2.\n\nCurative amendments have been made to various provisions to omit the reference to Section 144 to remove redundancy\n\n3.2-1.\n\nPosition under the ITA 1961\n\nUnder the ITA 1961, exemption in respect of units located in Special Economic Zones (SEZs) is provided under section 10AA, while other profit-linked deductions are governed by the provisions of Chapter VI-A, Part C.\n\n3.2-2.\n\nPosition under the ITA 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act 2026)\n\nUnder the ITA 2025, a common framework for deductions has been introduced in Chapter VIII (corresponding to Chapter VIA of the ITA 1961), which also includes the deduction in respect of SEZ units under section 144 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to section 10AA of the ITA 1961). Since Section 144 already forms part of Chapter VIII, a separate reference to Section 144 in other provisions is redundant. To remove this redundancy, the Finance Act, 2026, omits the reference to section 144 from the following sections:\n\n(a)\n\nSpecial provision for computing profits and gains of business or profession on a presumptive basis for certain residents under Section 58 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 44AD of the ITA 1961).\n\n(b)\n\nMeaning of associated enterprise under Section 162 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 92A of the ITA 1961).\n\n(c)\n\nMeaning of specified domestic transaction under Section 164 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 92BA of the ITA 1961).\n\n(d)\n\nDetermination of arms length price under Section 165 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 92C of the ITA 1961).\n\n(e)\n\nNew tax regime for individuals, Hindu undivided family, and others under Section 202 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 115BAC of the ITA 1961).\n\n(f)\n\nAssessment under Section 270 of the ITA 2025 (corresponding to Section 143 of the ITA 1961).\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\nSection - 355:\nSection 2(15) (ITA 1961) v. Section 2(23)/Section 346/Section 355 (ITA 2025)\n\nCharitable purpose\n\n1. List of corresponding sections\n\nIncome Tax Act 1961\n\nSection 2(15)\n\n: Charitable purpose\n\nIncome Tax Act 2025\n\nSection 2(23)\n\n: Charitable purpose\n\nSection 346\n\n: Restriction on commercial activities by registered non-profit organisation, carrying out advancement of any other object of general public utility\n\nSection 355\n\n: Interpretation\n\n2. Table of Corresponding Sections\n\n| ITA 1961 | ITA 2025 |\n| --- | --- |\n| Section 2( 15 ) | Section 2( 23 ) |\n| Proviso to section 2(15) | Section 346 & Section 355( e ) |\n\n3. Analysis of changes\n\n3.1\n\nDefinition of Charitable Purpose\n\n3.1-1\n\nProvision of ITA 1961\n\nThe definition of\n\ncharitable purpose\n\nprovided in Section 2(15) of the 1961 Act provides as follows:\n\nCharitable Purpose includes:\n\n(a)\n\nrelief of the poor;\n\n(b)\n\neducation;\n\n(c)\n\nyoga;\n\n(d)\n\nmedical relief;\n\n(e)\n\npreservation of the environment (including watersheds, forests and wildlife);\n\n(f)\n\npreservation of monuments or places or objects of artistic or historic interest;\n\n(g)\n\nthe advancement of any other object of general public utility.\n\nProvided\n\nthat the advancement of any other object of general public utility shall not be a charitable purpose, if it involves the carrying on of any activity in the nature of trade, commerce or business, or any activity of rendering any service in relation to any trade, commerce or business, for a cess or fee or any other consideration, irrespective of the nature of use or application, or retention, of the income from such activity, unless-\n\n(i) such activity is undertaken in the course of actual carrying out of such advancement of any other object of general public utility; and\n\n(ii) the aggregate receipts from such activity or activities during the previous year, do not exceed twenty per cent of the total receipts, of the trust or institution undertaking such activity or activities, of that previous year.\n\nThe proviso in Section 2(15)\n\nof the 1961 Act restricts\n\nthe NPOs\n\nengaged in the\n\nadvancement of any other object of general public utility (GPU)\n\nfrom engaging in\n\ntrade, commerce, or business activities\n\n. It provided that the advancement of any other object of a general public utility shall not be a charitable purpose if it involves the carrying any activity in the nature of trade, commerce or business or any activity of rendering any service in relation to any trade, commerce or business, for a cess or fee or any other consideration, irrespective of the nature of use or application, or retention, of the income from such activity. It may be noted that if the receipts from such activity do not exceed 20% of the total receipts in that year, then the institution would not lose its charitable status even if its main object is advancement of any other object of general public utility if such activity is undertaken in the course of actual carrying of such advancement of any other object of general public utility.\n\n3.1-2\n\nAnalysis of change in ITA 1961 Act vis-à-vis ITA 2025\n\nThe\n\nnew definition under Section 2(23)\n\nomits this restrictive proviso\n\n. Instead, Section 346 of the 2025 Act incorporates a\n\nsimilar restriction on commercial activities\n\nby\n\nregistered NPOs engaged in GPU\n\n. The revised definition clarifies the\n\nconcept of a charitable purpose\n\nwithout imposing any limitations. The\n\nrestrictions on commercial activities remain unchanged but\n\nare addressed\n\nunder Section 346\n\n, effectively distinguishing\n\nthe definition of a charitable purpose\n\nfrom eligibility for tax exemption. NPOs engaged in GPU activities must now comply with\n\nSection 346\n\nto adhere to the restrictions on commercial operations while maintaining their\n\ncharitable status\n\n.\n\nThe other notable change in the provision is changing the reference from trust to non-profit organisation for the voluntary contributions (including anonymous donations) received by such trusts. The 2025 Act introduces a comprehensive new taxation framework for non-profit organisations (NPOs), replacing the existing provisions applicable to trusts. The new regime adopts the term Non-Profit Organisation (NPO) instead of trust, which was previously used\n\n.\n\n3.2\n\nA new comprehensive and self-contained regime is introduced for the taxation of non-profit organisations\n\nThe ITA 2025 represents a substantial legislative overhaul of the taxation regime applicable to registered non-profit organisations (NPOs). It replaces and restructures the provisions governing charitable and religious trusts under the ITA 1961 into a comprehensive and self-contained legislative code.\n\nA defining hallmark of the new framework in the ITA 2025 is the consolidation of provisions. Provisions which, under the ITA 1961, were dispersed across multiple Chapters, Sections, provisos, and Explanations have now been brought into a single legislative block. Redundancies have been eliminated, terminologies have been standardised, and definitions previously scattered across various provisions have been harmonised within a unified framework.\n\nThe legislative structure in the ITA 2025 for the taxation of registered NPOs is contained in Part B of Chapter XVII (Special Provisions relating to certain persons), which is divided into seven sub-parts, covering:\n\nRegistration\n\nIncome\n\nCommercial Activities\n\nCompliance Requirements\n\nViolations\n\nApproval for Donations\n\nInterpretation\n\n3.2-1\n\nScattered provisions under ITA 1961\n\nUnder ITA 1961, provisions applicable to NPOs were spread across multiple Chapters, namely:\n\nChapter I: Section 2(15) (Definition of Charitable Purpose)\n\nChapter III: Sections 10(23C), 11, 12, 12A, 12AA, 12AB, 12AC, and 13\n\nChapter VIA: Section 80G\n\nChapter XII: Sections 115BBC and 115BBI\n\nChapter XII-EB: Sections 115TD, 115TE, and 115TF\n\nThis scattered arrangement, augmented by numerous cross-references, developed over decades due to piecemeal amendments. The outcome is a fragmented, proviso-heavy framework that is often difficult for taxpayers and practitioners to navigate. For example, Section 11 alone includes 13 Explanations and 16 Provisos, which complicate interpretation and compliance.\n\n3.2-2\n\nUnification under ITA 2025\n\nThe ITA 2025 introduces uniform terminology, replacing varied expressions such as trust, institution, university, educational institution, and hospital with the single defined term registered non-profit organisation. Under the ITA 2025, an NPO is an entity that:\n\nMeets prescribed eligibility criteria,\n\nSecures registration, and\n\nQualifies for the regimes tax concessions.\n\nThe ITA 2025 offers several advantages. It adopts a single term, registered non-profit organisation, replacing multiple terms. It eliminates the dual regime of approval under Section 10(23C) and registration under Section 12AB, introducing a single registration framework. All overlapping or obsolete provisions have been deleted. Additionally, tables have been introduced to provide clarity on processes like registration applications, the year of taxability for specified income, and the computation of tax on accreted income.\n\nThe special provisions for NPOs in ITA 2025 are contained in Sections 332 to 355, grouped as follows:\n\nRegistration: Sections 332 to 333\n\nIncome: Sections 334 to 343\n\nCommercial Activities: Sections 344 to 346\n\nCompliance Requirements: Sections 347 to 350\n\nViolations and Penalties: Sections 351 to 353\n\nApproval for Donations: Section 354\n\nMerger: Section 354A\n\nInterpretation: Section 355\n\nClick here for more details on the changes in the provisions for the taxability of charitable trusts or registered NPOs\n\nNote: This comparative study examines the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 1961, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026, with the provisions of the Income-tax Act, 2025, as amended by the Finance Act, 2026.\n\n=== INCOME TAX RULES (1962 & 2026) ===\n\nRules from Income-tax Rules, 1962:\nRule - 1:\nINCOME-TAX RULES, 1962 *\n[SO 969, DATED 26-3-1962]\nIn exercise of the powers conferred by section 295 of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961), and rule 15 of Part A, rule 11 of Part B and rule 9 of Part C of the Fourth Schedule to that Act, the Central Board of Revenue hereby makes the following rules, namely :—\nPART I\nPRELIMINARY\nShort title and commencement.\n1. (1) These rules may be called the Income-tax Rules, 1962.\n(2) They shall come into force on the 1st day of April, 1962.\n\nRule - 2:\nDefinitions.\n2. (1) In these rules, unless the context otherwise requires,—\n( a ) \"Act\" means the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961);\n1 [( aa ) \"authorised bank\" means any bank as may be appointed by the Reserve Bank of India as its agent under the provisions of sub-section (1) of section 45 of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934 (2 of 1934);]\n( b ) \"Chapter\", \"section\" and \"Schedule\" means respectively Chapter and section of, and Schedule to, the Act.\n(2) All references to \"Forms\" in these rules shall be construed as references to the forms set out in Appendix II hereto.\n\nForm 56:\n111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000009187 direct tax laws 1 111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000009188 international taxation 0 111050000000000002 direct tax laws 111050000000017592 transfer pricing 0 111050000000011658 account audit 111050000000011659 account and audit 0 111050000000000026 rule 111050000000010121 income tax rules 1962 0000 form income tax rules 1962 Form 56 Omitted ```markdown # (1) These rules may be called the Income-tax Rules, 2026.\n(2) They shall come into force on the 1st April, 2026.\n\nRule - 2:\nDefinitions.\n[ R. 2 of the 1962 Rules ]\n2. (1) In these rules, unless the context otherwise requires,—\n( a ) \"Act\" means the Income-tax Act, 2025 (30 of 2025);\n( b ) \"authorised bank\" means any bank as may be appointed by the Reserve Bank of India as its agent under the provisions of sub-section (1) of section 45 of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934 2 (2 of 1934);\n( c ) \"Form\" means a Form in Appendix III appended to these rules;\n( d ) \"section\" means a section of the Act.\n(2) Words and expressions used herein and not defined but defined in the Act shall have the meanings respectively assigned to them in the Act.",
        "user_query": "section 40(a)(ii) disallowance under ITA 1961 vs relevant section of ITA 2025",
        "context": null,
        "context_marker": null,
        "response": "The disallowance of any rate or tax levied on business profits, previously governed by Section 40(a)(ii) of the Income-tax Act, 1961, is now regulated under Section 35 of the Income-tax Act, 2025. While the core principle of disallowing income-tax payments remains consistent, the new Act streamlines the provision by integrating former Explanations directly into the statutory clauses.\n\n## **1. Corresponding Provisions**\n\n- **Income-tax Act, 1961**\n  - Section - 40(a)(ii): Disallowance of any rate or tax levied on profits or gains.\n- **Income-tax Act, 2025**\n  - Section - 35(a)(i): Disallowance of tax paid on income.\n  - Section - 35(a)(iii): Disallowance of tax paid in another country eligible for relief.\n  - Section - 66: Interpretation of terms related to business profits.\n\n## **2. Comparative Analysis**\n\nThe transition from the 1961 Act to the 2025 Act involves a structural reorganisation of the disallowance rules for tax payments, moving away from a proviso-heavy structure to a more direct clause-based format.\n\n### **A. Scope of Disallowance and Inclusion of Surcharge/Cess**\n\n**1961 Act:** Section 40(a)(ii) disallows any sum paid on account of any rate or tax levied on the profits or gains of any business or profession. This includes sums assessed at a proportion of, or otherwise on the basis of, such profits. Explanation 3 to Section 40(a)(ii) clarifies that the term \"tax\" includes, and shall be deemed to have always included, any surcharge or cess, by whatever name called, on such tax.\n\n**2025 Act:** Section 35(a)(i) provides that any amount on account of tax paid on income shall not be allowed as a deduction. The long line following Section 35(a)(iii) explicitly states that such amounts \"shall include any surcharge or cess on such tax, by whatever name called.\"\n\nThe 2025 Act maintains the broad disallowance of income tax and its associated levies. The clarification regarding surcharge and cess, which was an Explanation in the 1961 Act, is now part of the main body of Section 35(a) for Tax Year 2026-27 onwards.\n\n### **B. Foreign Tax Relief**\n\n**1961 Act:** Explanations 1 and 2 to Section 40(a)(ii) clarify that the disallowance includes any sum eligible for relief of tax under Section 90 or Section 90A, or deduction from Indian income-tax under Section 91. This ensures that taxes paid abroad, for which the assessee claims relief in India, are not treated as deductible business expenses.\n\n**2025 Act:** Section 35(a)(iii) specifically disallows any amount on account of tax paid in any other country for which relief is eligible under Section 159 or Section 160 (the corresponding provisions for DTAA and unilateral relief in the 2025 Act).\n\nThe 2025 Act replaces the \"Explanation\" mechanism with a specific sub-clause, Section 35(a)(iii), to address foreign tax payments. This does not change the tax incidence but improves the readability of the statute.\n\n## **3. Summary Table of Key Differences**\n\n| Aspect | Income-tax Act, 1961 | Income-tax Act, 2025 | Impact |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| **Primary Provision** | Section 40(a)(ii) | Section 35(a)(i) | Drafting alignment with no substantive change in principle. |\n| **Surcharge and Cess** | Included via Explanation 3 | Included in the long line of Section 35(a) | Statutory clarity is enhanced by moving the rule into the main section. |\n| **Foreign Tax Relief** | Included via Explanations 1 and 2 | Included via Section 35(a)(iii) | Procedural continuity with updated cross-references to Sections 159/160. |\n\n## **3. Conclusion**\n\n- The disallowance of income tax, surcharge, and education cess remains absolute under both Acts, ensuring that these payments are not treated as deductible business expenditures.\n- The Income-tax Act, 2025, eliminates the use of Explanations for this provision, instead using distinct sub-clauses (i) and (iii) under Section 35(a) to define the scope of non-deductible taxes.\n- For Tax Year 2026-27 and onwards, taxpayers must refer to Section 35(a) for these disallowances, while any pending proceedings for Assessment Year 2025-26 or earlier will continue to be governed by Section 40(a)(ii) of the 1961 Act.\n\n### References\n\n- Section - 40 (ITA 1961)\n- Section - 35 (ITA 2025)\n- Section - 66 (ITA 2025)\n\n###"
      }
    }
  ]
}
